It has been determined that certiorari is not an action at law, and hence the statutes relating to costs in such actions can have no application. The proceedings have been classed as special proceedings, and hence such costs as by statute were allowed in such proceedings were to be allowed on certiorari. Such were early decisions in New York based upon a statute of that state. (Sec. 318, old code.) The court of appeals,

Hurlburt v. Wilcox, 19 Wis. 419; Justice Hornblower in Aller v. Nichol v. Patterson, 4 Ohio, 200; Robinson v. Hedges, 3 N. J. L. 262. Stetson v. County Commissioners, Richards v. Walton, 12 Johns. 434.

Sheldon v. Quinline, 5 Hill, 441; 72 Me. 17.

See Haviland v. White, 7 How. 17; Hendrix v. Kellogg, 32 Ga. 485; People v. Van Alstyne, 3 Keyes (N. Y.), 35.

State v. Washoe County, 12 Nev. Pr. 154; People v. Flake, 14 How. 527; People v. Kingston, 101 N. Y. 82; North River Meadow Co. v. Christ Church, 22 N. J. L. 424.

<br>

### --- Page 0119 ---

662 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 4442.

however, in subsequent cases have held that the common-law writ is in no sense a special proceeding; and still later it reaffirmed the doctrine expressed in previous cases that costs were not allowable on a common-law writ, but held that the statutory writ was much broader, where it authorized a review and correction of the decisions and actions of inferior tribunals which entitled it to be denominated a special proceeding. Hence costs might be allowed. It has been urged that costs were allowable by reason of the analogy between writs of error and certiorari; but the answer has been that a writ of error only lies to a court proceeding according to the course of the common law, and certiorari only lies to a tribunal not proceeding according to the course of the common law.

Note.—In many states actions and special proceedings are defined by statute. Thus, an action is declared to be an ordinary proceeding in a court of justice by which a party prosecutes another party for the enforcement or protection of a right, the redress or prevention of a wrong, or the punishment of a public offense. Every other remedy is a special proceeding. It must follow that in those states where such a statute exists, other provisions relating to costs in special proceedings are directly applicable to certiorari.

Says Woodruff, J., in People v. Board of Police, 89 N. Y. 506: “It is quite clear that on a common-law certiorari costs were not recoverable by either party against the other. It was fitting that this should be so, as, in general, the people were the plaintiffs in certiorari, and some tribunal or body exercising powers judicial or quasi-judicial was defendant. In favor of both good faith was to be presumed, and the errors alleged, and, as the case may be, committed, were errors in law only. Where the relator, at whose instance the writ was sued out, was seeking to maintain some private right, property or privilege, there might be reason for providing that he should be liable for costs in case of affirmance. but as to the defendant there would still be the same ground of immunity in case of reversal. It is not according to our sense of propriety or expediency that public officers, boards or judicial tribunals should act under the peril of being charged with costs if they err in their judgment of the rules of law.”

By statute in some of the states the allowance of costs is within the discretion of the court. Thus, the statute of Maine, chapter 102, section 14, provides “that upon every application.

1 People v. Board of Police, 39 N. Y. 506; People v. McDonald, 69 N. Y. 369. 
2 People v. Commissioners, 76 N. Y. 64.

<br>

### --- Page 0120 ---

§ 4447.) CERTIORARI. 663

for certiorari and on the final adjudication thereof, the court may award costs against any party who appears and undertakes to maintain or object to the proceedings.” It was said: “By this section a limit is imposed upon the discretion of the court; costs cannot be awarded against a party who appears and does not defend against the proceeding. This is because a person who has acted in a judicial capacity ought not to be subjected to costs in cases where his errors are corrected without any opposition on his part. He stands in the position of a respondent in equity who puts in a disclaimer. In equity, the complainant having had probable cause to proceed against a respondent who disclaims, neither party recovers costs. There are courts which find authority in the nature of the proceeding, independent of statutes, to exercise a discretion in allowing costs in certain cases. Where the right to costs exists either by force of statute or otherwise, the question is limited to the amount and the party which is entitled thereto. Statutes in some instances provide that the costs shall be the same as is allowed upon appeal. The question is often present where the court has power to render a new judgment, and it is less favorable to the respondent than that of the tribunal below as to which party is entitled to costs. Usually upon appeal, the respondent, so long as the judgment is not reversed, recovers costs, and such by force of the statute would be the result upon certiorari. So, also, where the writ is dismissed, costs will be awarded against the petitioner. And it is held in some jurisdictions, where the court was without jurisdiction to issue the writ, that it had no power to award costs upon dismissal for such reason. The appellate court, on appeal from the judgment of the court of review, may, it seems, award costs of appeal in its discretion. The New York court of appeals, however, subsequently decided, upon direct consideration of the question,

1 Stetson v. County Commissioners, 72 Me. 17. Yerg. 186, apparently contrary.

2 Aller v. Shurts, 17 N. J. L. 188; Dye v. Noel, 85 Ill. 290. State v. Meyer, 40 N. J. L. 252. See, Deston v. Boyd, 21 Ark. 264; Taul v. Collingsworth, 2 Yerg. (Tenn.) 579.

3 Chance v. Haley, 6 Ind. 367; Norton v. Walker, 19 Tex. 102. See, however, People v. Wheeler, 21 N. Y. 82; People v. Van Alstyne, 3 Keyes, 35.

<br>

### --- Page 0121 ---

664 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 4447.

that costs upon appeal from proceedings upon a common-law writ were not allowable.

§ 4447. Review of the judgment of the court of review by the appellate court.— The question of the right to appeal from the judgment of the reviewing court is another question which is the occasion for a conflict of judicial opinion. The allowance of the writ being discretionary, this is urged as a reason against the right of appeal, as appeal does not lie to review discretionary acts. Yet, as we have seen, in some jurisdictions the propriety of granting the writ is determined upon hearing, and, if allowed, then thereafter the proceedings are of strict right; while in others the writ is allowed more as a matter of course, and the discretion is exercised at the hearing upon the return. The rule seems to be that, when the proceeding is final and affects a substantial right, the right of appeal exists, such as a judgment upon the hearing, whether it be in form that of affirmance or reversal, or dismissal of the writ or quashing the proceedings. But where the court refuses, in the exercise of discretion, to allow the writ, then that such order is not appealable, unless it may be in a clear case of an abuse of discretion. As it seems that in the latter class of jurisdictions above mentioned the discretion that may be exercised extends to the appellate court, no good reason appears why the right of appeal does not exist even where the court below has exercised its discretion, not because the appellate court may exercise its discretion, but on the ground that there may have been an abuse of discretion, which can be determined in no other way, as well as on the ground that such order of refusal does affect a substantial right, and for the purposes of appeal is a final order made in a special proceeding. Of course authorities may be found to the contrary, but there is conflict on every proposition; the distinction being made by some courts that where the writ is dismissed, then only the regularity of the proceeding is reviewable upon appeal. In New York it was held that

1 People v. Nelliston, 79 N. Y. 638. 2 See section 415, treating the question of discretion. Moede v. Stearns County, 43 Minn. 312; Knapp v. Hel- ler, 82 Wis. 467; Shafer v. Hogue, 70 Wis. 393; Morse v. Spees, 25 Wis. 543; Owens v. State, 27 Wis. 456. 3 Morse v. Spees, 25 Wis. 543.

<br>

### --- Page 0122 ---

§ 4447.] CERTIORARI. 665

an appeal would not lie from an order denying a motion to quash. Yet it was said that case was not an authority for the proposition that an order denying the motion to quash a certiorari irregularly or improperly issued in a case not reviewable on certiorari is not appealable, as when the proceedings sought to be reviewed were not of a judicial character.

Note.— There is a distinction to be observed between the right of appeal and the determination of questions upon appeal. Thus, there is a difference between saying that an appeal does not exist to review discretionary acts, and saying that upon appeal discretionary acts will not be reviewed. The right to appeal exists from the character of the proceedings as affecting a substantial right. What shall be considered and determined upon that appeal is another question.

Thus it was said by the New York court of appeals: “The writ was applied for under the provisions of the code of civil procedure, which by express provision makes its allowance discretionary with the court. Such an order is not reviewable by this court. If the court in making the order had refrained from exercising its discretion in passing upon the question presented, and had quashed the writ upon the ground of a want of power to issue it, or had granted it in a case not warranted by law, this court could properly have reviewed the questions presented by an appeal from such determination. But in a case where that court has exercised its discretion with respect to the allowance or denial of the writ, and has refused to grant it on the ground that it ought not, under all of the circumstances of the case, to have been issued, this court has no jurisdiction to review its determination, and so it has been repeatedly held.”

Note.— The court may not, as a rule of practice, review the exercise of discretion in inferior courts, but that it is a question of jurisdiction may well be doubted. It has jurisdiction to determine whether the court has exercised its discretion, and a wrong decision of that question, as that court has frequently held, does not affect its jurisdiction. Besides, precedents are numerous in that court and many others, that an abuse of discretion is not the exercise of discretion; that arbitrary action is sometimes an abuse of discretion, and that the appellate court, by reason of its superior powers, if upon no other ground, will correct such abuse where it has been exercised.

1 Jones v. People, 79 N. Y. 45.  
2 People v. Commissioners, 97 N. Y.  
3 People v. McCarthy, 102 N. Y. 630.

<br>

### --- Page 0123 ---

666 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 4447.

In a subsequent case (Matter of Corwin, 135 N. Y. 245) it was said with respect to the allowance of a common-law writ of certiorari: “The granting or refusal of which by the supreme court has always been held to be so far discretionary that an order of the court quashing the writ is not reviewable here, unless it affirmatively appears that the discretion of the court was not exercised in granting it.”

Note.— An act regulating the review of assessments, etc. (ch. 269, Laws 1880), is held to be independent of the writ of certiorari recognized by the code, and in such cases the writ is a matter of right and the court is without discretion.

Such was the rule in Alabama, but by statute appeals are expressly provided; and in Maryland, where no appeal is provided for, the judgment is final and conclusive. In Florida, Missouri, West Virginia, Wisconsin, Michigan, Illinois, and in most of the states, the right of appeal is recognized, and in Tennessee and California this right is confirmed by statute. In Illinois it is expressly held that the discretion of the court is subject to review, and in Virginia that the refusal to issue the writ, or the propriety of issuing it, may be reviewed in the supreme court of appeals by writ of error. The judgment of the appellate court is ordinarily either that of affirmance or reversal. I know of no reason why the appellate court has not the same power with respect to modification that it has with respect to other judgments at law.

Sec. 445. Definition and nature of the writ.— A most full and accurate definition of the nature of the remedy and office of the writ of prohibition is given by Blackstone (3 Com. 111). He says: “The other injury, which is that of encroachment of jurisdiction, calling one, coram non judice, to answer in a court that has no legal cognizance of the cause, is also a grievance

<br>

### --- Page 0125 ---

668 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 446, 447.]

for which the common law has provided a remedy by the writ of prohibition. A prohibition is a writ issuing properly out of the court of king’s bench, being the king’s prerogative writ; but for the furtherance of justice it may also be had in some cases out of the court of chancery, common pleas or exchequer, directed to the judge and parties to a suit in any inferior court, commanding them to cease from the prosecution thereof, upon suggestion that either the cause originally, or some collateral matter arising therein, does not belong to that jurisdiction, but to the cognizance of some other court.” Changing only the name of the court from which the writ issues, these words define with exactness the nature and extent of the remedy as known and applied in this country.

Sec. 446. Writ lies only where there is a want of jurisdiction.—The writ lies only where the subordinate tribunal has no jurisdiction at all in the premises, or where, having jurisdiction, some question arises which is not within the power of the court to decide. The use of the writ is not confined to those cases where the court has no jurisdiction originally of the subject-matter, but extends also to those cases where the court having such jurisdiction is about to exceed its powers, and to do that which it has no power to do at all. In other words, the writ will lie to confine tribunals within the limits of their powers.

Sec. 447. Excess of jurisdiction.—The writ has a proper but a restricted and limited office, and it cannot be enlarged so as to bring within its scope and operation questions merely collateral or incidental to its direct purpose, or more or less intimately connected with such purpose and object. It cannot be made a drag-net by means of which all controverted and litigated questions between individual suitors may be brought into court and tried and determined, for its office, as we repeat, is to prevent the exercise by a tribunal possessing judicial pow-

1 State ex rel. Kellogg v. Gary, 435; Smith v. Whitney, 116 U.S. 167; Judge, 33 Wis. 93. Ex parte Gordon, 104 U. S. 515.

2 State ex rel. De Puy v. Evans, 88 Wis. 255; Maurer v. Mitchell, 538 Cal. 289; Curtis v. Superior Court, 63 Cal. Ex parte Pennsylvania, 109 U. S. 174; Smith v. Whitney, 116 U.S. 167; Ex parte Gordon, 104 U.S. 115.

<br>

### --- Page 0126 ---

§ 448.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 669

Errors of jurisdiction over matters not within its cognizance or exceeding its jurisdiction in matters of which it has cognizance. It is a preventive rather than remedial process, and cannot take the place of a writ of error or other proceeding for the review of judicial action, or of a suit in equity to prevent or redress fraud. Hence, though a court may have jurisdiction of an action, prohibition will lie to forbid it to issue a writ not authorized by law. And though a county court has jurisdiction to canvass and certify the returns of a county-seat election, the writ will lie after they once have canvassed the returns, to prevent a recanvass and thus change the result first ascertained, on the ground that the latter act would be an unauthorized assumption of judicial power. It was stated by the New York court of appeals, upon review of English authorities, that those cases proved that the writ lies to prevent the exercise of any unauthorized power in a cause or proceeding of which the subordinate tribunal has jurisdiction, no less than when the entire cause is without its jurisdiction; from which it follows that the mere fact of jurisdiction of the subject-matter and of the person is not conclusive against the right to issue the writ.

Sec. 448. When the granting of the writ is discretionary. The writ is in some cases a matter of right, and in others discretionary. Where it appears that the court whose action is sought to be prohibited had clearly no jurisdiction of the cause originally, or of some collateral matter arising therein, a party who has objected to the jurisdiction at the outset, and has no other remedy, is entitled to a writ of prohibition as a matter of right. But where there is another legal remedy by appeal or otherwise, or where the question of the jurisdiction of the court is doubtful, or depends on facts which are not made matter of record, or where the application is made by a stranger, the granting or refusal of the writ is discretionary.

NOTE.—It is quite doubtful whether the state courts will be willing to adopt the latter declaration of the high court which made it. It is cer-

1 Thomson v. Tracy et al., 60 N. Y. 81; Hudson v. Judge of Superior Court, 42 Mich. 289.  
2 State ex rel. McCurdy v. Slaver (Mo.), 208 S. W. R. 718.  
3 State ex rel. Ellis v. Elkin (Mo.), 308 S. W. R. 333.  
4 Quimbo Appo v. People, 20 N. Y. 531.  
5 In re Rice, 155 U.S. 396.

<br>

### --- Page 0127 ---

670 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 448.

tainly at variance with what has been declared by courts almost everywhere to be the law. While it has been quite generally held that the granting of the writ was largely discretionary, yet it was assumed that such discretion could only be exercised where there was no other adequate remedy. It may be, notwithstanding the language used, which was repeated from another case, that the supreme court do not intend to be understood that there exists an adequate remedy, and the question of the jurisdiction of the court is doubtful, depending on facts extrinsic of the record, that the court has an unlimited discretion to grant the writ. Because in the same opinion in which the rule stated was delivered, it was stated, “a writ of prohibition is never to be issued unless it clearly appears that the inferior court is about to exceed its jurisdiction.” Smith v. Whitney, 116 U. S. 167, 176; High’s Extraordinary Remedies, secs. 767, 770; State ex rel. Dilworth v. Braun, 31 Wis. 606; In re Radl, 86 Wis. 645.

The court of appeals of New York is authority also for the position that the granting or refusal of the writ is a matter of discretion, seemingly taking a position in advance of that stated by the federal supreme court. Thus it is said: “The writ of prohibition is an extraordinary remedy and should be issued only in cases of extreme necessity, and not for grievances which may be redressed by ordinary proceedings at law or in equity, or by appeal, and is not demandable as matter of right, but of sound judicial discretion, to be granted or withheld according to the circumstances of each particular case.” Hence it was held that the order of the supreme court denying the writ was not reviewable. The court quite elaborately reviews the decisions of the English courts with respect to this question, and concludes that the modern or more recent doctrine of these courts is to the effect that the power to issue the writ is discretionary. The supreme court of the United States in the case cited, reasoning from the doctrine of the later English cases, reached the conclusion as stated, that such doctrine does not extend to those cases where the court has no jurisdiction of the suit or prosecution, and where objection has been made at the outset and the party has no other remedy. In those cases he is entitled to the writ as matter of right, and a refusal to grant it, where all the proceedings appear of record, may be reviewed on error.

People v. Westbrook, 89 N. Y. 152,

<br>

### --- Page 0128 ---

§ 449.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 671

Sec. 449. Writ only lies to restrain the exercise of judicial functions.—The object of a writ of prohibition is to prevent a court of peculiar, limited or inferior jurisdiction from assuming jurisdiction of a matter beyond its legal cognizance. It can only be issued to restrain the exercise of judicial functions. The use of the writ has been declared in some states by statute. Thus in California it is declared that “the writ of prohibition is the counterpart of the writ of mandamus. It arrests the proceedings of any tribunal, corporation, board or person, when such proceedings are without or in excess of the jurisdiction of such tribunal, corporation, board or person.” It was held that this provision of the code did not extend the office of the writ, or that it should issue in cases to which it could not have been resorted to prior to the statute. That the true construction of the statute was, that the corporations, boards or persons therein mentioned were such as were clothed with limited judicial powers, and where such bodies or persons were acting in excess of their powers, the writ, which was the same as at common law, would lie. The writ will not issue to arrest legislation pending before a body authorized by the constitution and laws to legislate with reference to matters of public interest. Nor can the legislature enlarge or extend the office of the writ so as to include ministerial functions. This rule extends to proceedings of inferior courts, if acting in a purely administrative capacity, such, for instance, as agent for a county for the purpose of removing the county seat to a new site, or the judge acting as a commissioner merely in taking testimony. Where, however, the question of the legality of the issuing of certain bonds was by an act of the legislature left to certain judges to determine, it was held that the writ would issue to restrain such judges from acting, on the ground that the act was void, as conferring legislative power on the judges. So the issue of the writ was held proper to restrain a circuit judge from sitting as pre-

1 Smith v. Whitney, 116 U.S. 167. Thresher, 62 Cal. 407; Camron v.
2 Maurer v. Mitchell, 53 Cal. 289. Kenfield, 57 Cal. 550.
3 Spring Valley W. W. Co. v. San Francisco Co., 52 Cal. 111.
4 Hobart v. Tillotson, 66 Cal. 210; Farmers’ Co-operative Union v.
5 State v. Justices, 41 Mo. 44.
6 State v. Spearing, 31 La. Ann. 122.
7 State v. Young, 29 Minn. 474,

<br>

### --- Page 0129 ---

872 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 450.

siding judge of a statutory inferior court under an unconstitutional law.

The statement that the writ only lies to restrain the exercise of judicial acts excludes those that are ministerial. To make the proper distinction between acts that are judicial and those that are ministerial only is not free from difficulty. The courts are not in accord upon this question with reference to particular acts. This has been discussed with reference to review upon certiorari. In Massachusetts it has been held that the writ would lie to restrain a board of aldermen of a city from laying out or widening a street when they were without authority in the premises. No question was raised as to the character of such an act, whether judicial or otherwise. So in Maine it was held that county commissioners would be restrained from proceeding to lay out a highway after their proceedings had been reversed. The ground upon which the writ issued was that the court, by virtue of its supervisory power over inferior courts for the prevention and correction of errors and abuses, where the law does not expressly provide a remedy, could thus issue the writ. It was thus evidently held that the act restrained was judicial. In California it was held that the collection of a tax by a town marshal was purely a ministerial act, and prohibition would not lie; and the same with respect to the acts of a comptroller in drawing warrants. It is also held in that state that the legislature is without authority to extend the use of the writ so as to include ministerial acts.

§ 450. Legislative bodies — How far subject to control by — While, as has been stated, the writ will not lie to restrain legislative action, yet, when bodies clothed with legislative powers only attempt to usurp judicial functions, or are clothed with judicial powers in some matters pertaining to their organization and existence as legislative bodies, and they attempt to proceed in excess of their judicial authority, prohibition will lie to restrain their action.

1 Ex parte Roundtree, 51 Ala. 42. 5 Camron v. Kenfield, 57 Cal. 550.  
2 Day v. Springfield, 102 Mass. 310. 6 Camron v. Kenfield, 57 Cal. 550.  
3 Harriman v. Waldo County, 53 Me. 83. 7 Spring Valley W. W. Co. v. San Francisco, 52 Cal. 111; State v. Columbia, 16 S. C. 412.  
4 La Conte v. Trustees, etc. 57 Cal. 269. 8 Speed v. Common Council, 98

<br>

### --- Page 0130 ---

§§ 451, 452. 673

PROHIBITION — WRIT OF.

Sec. 451. Removal of officers.— Hence, when the power to remove from office by such a body is not discretionary, but only for cause, the proceedings are judicial in their nature and may be reviewed on certiorari; and if the board or body proceed summarily, or in a manner not authorized by law, to remove an incumbent from office, it may be restrained by writ of prohibition. So prohibition will lie to restrain a common council from taking proceedings to remove an officer, where the power of removal, even for cause, is not inherent or vested in such body. And where a legislative body has such power, the power does not extend to remove for misconduct on the part of such officer prior to his election or appointment to such office. The misconduct for which an officer may be removed must be found in his acts and conduct in the office for which his removal is sought, and must constitute a legal cause for his removal, and affect the proper administration of the office.

Sec. 452. Administrative action.— The action or proceedings of such a body with respect to granting liquor licenses is administrative or ministerial, and not judicial, and therefore not subject to restraint by prohibition. So are the proceedings of the board of police justices of the city of New York with reference to the appointment of clerks and the proceedings of election commissioners. Yet, where election commissioners are proceeding to examine witnesses and hearing evidence to determine whether the precinct commissioners have returned votes that ought not to be counted, the writ will issue to restrain such unauthorized action.

Mich. 360; State v. Common Council (Minn.), 55 N. W. Rep. 118; McKee v. Town Council, 1 Rice (S. C.), 241; People v. District Court, 6 Colo. 534; Mealing v. City Council, Dudley (Ga.), 221; State v. Stockhouse, 14 S. C. 427; People v. Road Com’rs, 3 Hill (S. C.), 114.

1 State v. Common Council (Minn.), 55 N. W. Rep. 118; Speed v. Common Council, 98 Mich. 360.

2 Speed v. Common Council, 98 Mich. 369.

3 Speed v. Common Council, 98 Mich. 360; State v. Jersey City, 35 N. J. L. 536; Commonwealth v. Shaver, 3 Serg. & W. 288.

4 State v. Columbia, 16 S. C. 412; La Croix v. Fairfield County, 50 Conn. 321.

5 Norton v. Dowling, 46 How. Pr. 7.

6 People v. Board of Election Commissioners, 54 Cal. 404; Kemp v. Ventalett, 58 Ga. 419.

7 Braizie v. Fayette County, 25 W. Va. 213.

<br>

### --- Page 0131 ---

674 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 453, 454.]

Sec. 453. Executive officers.— The writ will not issue to restrain executive officers in the performance of their duties. The reason, by one court at least, has been given, that the judiciary have no right to invade the province of the other departments of the government, and the reason may have been applicable to that case. Yet it by no means follows that, when an executive clearly usurps power of a judicial nature, or exercises judicial powers, where he is clothed with such, in excess of the authority conferred, he is not amenable to the restraining power of the courts, at least to the same extent as officers of the administrative department. The writ will not lie against a governor to restrain him from granting a commission to a person claiming to be duly elected to a public office! There are reasons for this conclusion other than the one above stated. The matter is within his jurisdiction, a part of his executive duties, and the courts cannot restrain him in their execution. So it has been held that the writ will not lie against a mayor to prevent him from investigating charges against an inferior officer of the city. This is evident, if such were included in his duties. If, however, he had no authority in the premises, either inherent or otherwise, the writ would lie to the same extent as to a common council.

Sec. 454. Ministerial officers — Taxing officers — Sheriffs, etc.— The rule quite generally prevails that the writ will not issue to restrain ministerial officers, such as collectors of taxes, from proceeding to make collection. The reason is sometimes based upon the ground that such matter is within their jurisdiction, and mere errors or mistakes in the exercise of that jurisdiction are not subject to restraint by prohibition, though they might in some cases be reviewed by certiorari. In other cases it is based upon the ground that in such matter the officer is like a sheriff in the exercise of purely ministerial duties, and therefore the writ does not lie. A writ of prohibition does

1 Grier v. Taylor, 4 McCord, 206.  
2 Burch v. Hardwicke, 30 Grat. (Va.) 24; State ex rel. Kellogg v. Gary, 33 Cal. 81; Maurer v. Mitchell, 53 Cal. 289.  
3 Clayton v. Heidelberg, 9 Smed. & M. (Miss.) 623; La Conte v. Berkely, 57 Wis. 93; People v. Kern County, 269; People v. Supervisors of Queens Co., 1 Hill, 195.

<br>

### --- Page 0132 ---

§ 455.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 675  
not lie to a ministerial officer to stay the execution of process in his hands. It is directed to a court in which some action or legal proceeding is pending and to the party who prosecutes the suit, and commands the one not to hold and the other not to follow the plea. It stays both the court and the party from proceeding with the suit. The writ was framed for the purpose of keeping inferior courts within the limits of their own jurisdiction without encroaching upon other tribunals. It was said by a court, “there is not the slightest foundation in the books for saying that a prohibition may issue to a ministerial officer to stay the execution of process in his hands. If the relator has suffered or is in danger of suffering an injury, he is mistaken in supposing that we can grant the relief which he asks.” It was determined, however, in People v. Works, 7 Wend. 486, that prohibition would lie to restrain a collector from levying a tax, where the purpose was unlawful and beyond the power to impose, being to improve property not owned by the town. The question of ministerial and judicial functions does not appear to have been discussed — simply that of jurisdiction.

Sec. 455. Boards of review.—Boards of review exercise judicial functions, and it follows that when they attempt to exceed their jurisdiction they may be restrained by prohibition, unless there is available an adequate remedy by certiorari, injunction or other means. Where a personal-property tax was assessed and levied against an individual, and, not being paid, a supplementary proceeding authorized by statute was commenced to examine such person with reference to his property before a county judge, and application was made for a writ of prohibition to restrain such proceedings, based, among others, upon the ground that the party assessed was not a resident of the taxing district, and therefore the assessment was void, it was held these grounds were not sufficient to authorize the writ. It was stated that if such proceedings before the court were judicial in character then the court had jurisdiction, which was sufficient ground for denial of the writ. If they were ministerial merely, then of course the writ could not issue.

People v. Supervisors of Queens State ex rel. Kellogg v. Gary, 33 Co., 1 Hill, 195, Wis, 93.

<br>

### --- Page 0133 ---

676 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 456, 457.]

It was stated by another court that the writ would not lie to prevent supervisors from collecting a tax, unless the proceedings were absolutely without or in excess of the jurisdiction of the board. While this was the statement, it will hardly justify the conclusion that if they were without or in excess of the jurisdiction that the writ should issue, as this court in subsequent cases have held the writ would not lie, as such duties “were ministerial.” Where a sheriff has seized goods upon attachment, and judgment thereafter is rendered and a sale ordered, prohibition will not issue to restrain proceedings on the part of such officer, on the ground that the court did not acquire jurisdiction either of the subject-matter or the person. Such an officer is, in such proceeding, in the exercise of ministerial duties, and therefore the writ will not lie. 

Sec. 456. Jurisdiction — Question of.— The definition of the term “jurisdiction” has been given, and the general rules which apply to distinguish it from error have been fully discussed in other parts of this work. It only remains to call attention here to those distinctions which have been made upon application for a hearing upon the writ of prohibition. It is proper to state in this connection that the writ of prohibition cannot be made to serve the purpose of a writ of error or certiorari to correct the mistakes of subordinate courts in deciding any question of law or fact within their jurisdiction, nor to prevent acts already done. 

Sec. 457. Illustrations of the application of the rule.— Where the question was whether the writ should issue by reason of a defect in a criminal warrant or because of an alleged former jeopardy, the warrant being confessedly bad, it was said: “To allow such a writ in such a case is to sanction its allowance in every case where the warrant is void upon its face, or where the complaint, information or indictment is insufficient. 

1 People v. Kern County, 47 Cal. 81.  4 Smith v. Whitney, 116 U. S. 167;  
2 La Conte v. Berkely, 57 Cal. 269.  Ex parte Ferry Co., 104 U. S. 519; Ex  
See also Talbott v. Dent, 9 B. Mon. (Ky.) 527.  
5 Hull v. Superior Court, 63 Cal.  
7 Dolson v. Westheimer, 36 Pac. 179  
Rep. (Wy.) 626.

<br>

### --- Page 0134 ---

§ 458.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. OTT

That would be a complete revolution in criminal procedure. So this court has repeatedly held, that under our statutes, such writ issues only to restrain the acts of a court or other inferior tribunal exercising some judicial power which it has no legal authority to exercise at all. Where the inferior tribunal has jurisdiction of the subject-matter, and the defendant is duly served with process or voluntarily appears, a writ of prohibition will not be granted. Where application was made to restrain a district court sitting in an admiralty proceeding, in a case begun therein against the owner of a vessel to recover damages for the drowning of certain persons in consequence of a collision, and it being urged that damages were not recoverable for such wrongs, it was said: “This question is one which a court of admiralty may properly decide. Having jurisdiction in respect to the collision, it would seem necessarily to follow, the court had jurisdiction to hear and decide what liability the vessel had incurred thereby.” A vessel was libeled for pilot fees, the master having refused the services of a pilot when by the laws of Delaware they should, as was alleged, have been accepted. The claim was resisted on the ground that the United States district court in Pennsylvania had no jurisdiction. The district court allowed the claim, whereupon the attorney-general of Pennsylvania applied for a writ of prohibition directing the court below to refrain from further proceedings in the case. It was held on the authority of Ex parte Hagar, 104 U.S. 520, that, as the court had jurisdiction of the vessel sued and the subject-matter of the suit, it could not be restrained by a writ of prohibition from deciding all questions properly arising in the suit. The real question was whether the Delaware statute was valid.

Sec. 458. Rule further illustrated.—The collector of the port of New York seized a yacht, purchased abroad by a Vanderbilt, claiming that it was subject to the payment of duty. The owner filed a libel in the district court against the vessel and collector, alleging among other things that the seizure was wrongful. The collector made application for a writ of prohi-

1 State ex rel. De Puy v. Evans, 88 Wis. 255.
2 Ex parte Pennsylvania, 109 U.S. 174.
3 Ex parte Gordon, 104 U.S. 115.

<br>

### --- Page 0135 ---

678 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 459.

bition to prohibit him from further holding the plea which had been filed in the suit. The effect was to determine the question of the jurisdiction of the court in the premises. It was held that the libel presented for the determination of the district court, as the subject-matter of the suit, the question whether the yacht was an imported article, within the meaning of the customs revenue laws. The matter was sub judice in the district court. The district court has jurisdiction to determine the question, because it has jurisdiction of the vessel by attachment and of the collector by monition, and for the supreme court to decide in the first instance and in the proceeding, on application for a writ of prohibition, the question whether the yacht was an imported article and subject to duty, would be to decide that question as a matter of original jurisdiction and not of appellate jurisdiction, while, as a question of original jurisdiction, it is duly pending before the district court on pleadings which put that very question in issue. As the district court has jurisdiction, we will not prohibit it from proceeding in the exercise of such jurisdiction. A writ of prohibition is not intended to take the place of exceptions to the libel for insufficiency, and will issue only in case of want of jurisdiction either of the parties or the subject-matter of the proceeding.

§ 459. Contempt proceedings,— The rule is the same with respect to contempt proceedings as prevails with respect to other proceedings. There is nothing growing out of their summary character that requires, or to which is applied, an exception to the general rule. The only question is that of jurisdiction. Whether the matter alleged does or does not constitute a contempt is purely a matter for the determination of the court before which the proceedings are pending, in the first instance. If the court has jurisdiction of contempts, and the matter is alleged as in contempt of that court, and jurisdiction of the person is obtained, then the court possesses all of the elements of jurisdiction to authorize it to proceed and determine all other questions in the case, including the sufficiency of the complaint or charges, and the questions of law relevant thereto. It may

1 In re Morrison, 147 U. S. 14; In re Ion, 105 U.S. 451; In re Engles, 146 Fassett, 142 U. S. 479; Ex parte Slay-U.S. 357.

<br>

### --- Page 0136 ---

§ 460.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 679

be assumed that prohibition will not issue to restrain where habeas corpus would not lie to release.

Sec. 460. Whether acts or words alleged are contempts is not a question of jurisdiction. Where a subordinate court held that certain matters alleged constituted a contempt, which order was reversed upon an appeal, and thereafter in other proceedings the question was present as to the jurisdiction of the court, it was said: “A simple question of law was thus presented to the court, as to whether all the elements constituting the offense of contempt appeared on the application for the commitment. Whether they did or not in no sense constitutes a jurisdictional question. The court concededly had jurisdiction of the parties and the subject-matter of the application, and we think authority to determine whether a contempt had been committed or not; and the question for its consideration was whether the facts of the case brought it within the statutory definition of a contempt. An erroneous decision of that question in no sense affected the jurisdiction of the court over the subject-matter of the application. The disobedience of its order by the plaintiff gave the court jurisdiction of the subject-matter, and called upon it to determine whether a contempt had been committed or not. The right to adjudicate upon this question did not depend upon the fact whether the plaintiff was guilty of a contempt, but whether a case had been made calling for an adjudication upon that question.” The learned court, after reviewing many decisions, further state: “The rule to be deduced from these authorities seems to be that when a court is called upon to adjudicate upon doubtful questions of law, or to determine as to inferences to be drawn from circumstances reasonably susceptible of different interpretations or meanings, and calling for the exercise of the judicial function in their determination, its decision thereon does not render an order or process based upon it, although afterward vacated or set aside as erroneous, void, or subject the party procuring it to an action for damages thereby inflicted.

1 Fischer v. Langbien, 103 N. Y. 84; Williams v. Smith, 108 Eng. C. L.

Yates v. Lansing, 5 Johns. 281. 596; Harman v. Brotherson, 1 Denio,

2 Fischer v. Langbien, 103 N. Y. 84; 537; Landt v. Hills, 19 Barb, 283, Marks v. Townsend, 97 N. Y. 590;

<br>

### --- Page 0137 ---

680 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 461.

Where the jurisdiction of the court is made to depend upon the existence of some fact of which there is an entire absence of proof, it has no authority to act in the premises, and if it nevertheless proceeds and entertains jurisdiction of the proceeding, all of its acts are void and afford no justification to parties instituting them as against parties injuriously affected thereby. But if the facts presented to the court call upon it for the exercise of judgment and reason, upon evidence which might in its consideration affect different minds differently, a judicial question is presented, which, however decided, does not render either party, or the court making it, liable for the consequences of its action. This is substantially the test that has been recognized by practically all courts, and specially declared even in more emphatic terms by the supreme court of Wisconsin. That court declared, where release of the prisoner committed for contempt was sought by habeas corpus, that there was no question but that the court had jurisdiction of the cause of action and of the parties. If, then, it had power to make the order of commitment in any supposable circumstances which might arise in the progress of the cause, then the order is valid until reversed, however erroneous it may be in the particular circumstances. And again, that the writ of prohibition only lies where the court exercising judicial power has no legal authority to exercise it at all. And again, if it were conceded that a corporation could not be the object of a criminal libel, the decision of a court holding the contrary does not present a jurisdictional defect. The court had jurisdiction of criminal libels, and whether the indictment or information charged such an offense was within the jurisdiction of the court to determine.

§ 461. Disobedience of void order.— The rule that disobedience of a void order cannot be contempt is sometimes invoked to sustain the right to habeas corpus or writ of prohibition, and that rule extended to include orders that are voidable or may have been declared erroneous, and courts have sometimes erred in failing to make the proper distinction. A void order

1 In re Rosenberg, 90 Wis. 581. 3 Hauser et al. v. State, 33 Wis. 678. 
2 State ex rel. Du Puy v. Evans, 88 Wis. 255.

<br>

### --- Page 0138 ---

§§ 462, 463.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 681

is, as we have seen, one the court had no power to make; not one where the court, having the power to make an order, does not make the proper one,— power being distinguished from judgment. Hence, if a court should undertake to adjudge one in contempt for violating the order of another court, such order would be absolutely void, as without the power of the court; or should order garnishees to sell property and turn the proceeds into court, where no such power was conferred upon the court, such order would be absolutely void for the same reason. This is far different from a case where the court has the power to order property sold, but the evidence before the court is insufficient to justify the order.

Sec. 462. Distinction between void and voidable orders.— It was stated by the Louisiana court that a writ of prohibition is not a writ of right, and should only issue when the court to be prohibited, having no jurisdiction at all, ratione material and ratione persone, clearly usurps jurisdictional power; and that the writ should not issue to prohibit a district court to punish for contempt a party defendant in an injunction suit, on the ground that the court had no jurisdiction of the suit. Sometimes it is said that unless the court had the power to make the particular order it is without jurisdiction. The intent and meaning is, where such statement appears, that unless the court had the power to make an order of that class, kind or character, it is acting in excess of its jurisdiction. It could not be questioned but that, if the court made an order directing an act to be done upon insufficient proof, the error is not jurisdictional. If, on the contrary, the court had the power to impose a fine only, and should order imprisonment, the order would be in excess of the power and absolutely void. These questions have been fully illustrated in preceding chapters, and are merely referred to here as illustrative of the particular branch of the subject under discussion.

Sec. 463. California — Rule in.— There are some decisions made by the California court not strictly in accord with the doctrine herein expressed or with the doctrine prevailing in

1 People v. Judge, 27 Cal. 151. 3 State ex rel. Follett v. Rightor, 32. 
2 Brown v. Moore, 61 Cal. 482. La. Ann, 1182,

<br>

### --- Page 0139 ---

682 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 463.

Practically all the other states. At the outset, the premises assumed are contrary to the principles of law as elsewhere expressed. They state that “it is essential to the validity of proceedings in contempt, subjecting a party to fine and imprisonment, that they show a case in point of jurisdiction within the provisions of the law by which such proceedings are authorized, for mere presumptions and intendments are not to be indulged in in their support.” Further, that the statute requires an affidavit to set the power of the court in motion. If this is absent the court is without power. Then the argument is made that an affidavit which fails to set out the substantive facts required is the same as no affidavit, and therefore the court is without power. In other words, jurisdiction is made to depend upon the sufficiency of the proof. It is immaterial that the court is called upon to exercise its judgment upon the sufficiency of the proof. The proof must be in fact sufficient to stand the test upon review to confer jurisdiction. The same court in an earlier case announced the doctrine that it is the right and duty of the supreme court on habeas corpus to review the decisions of inferior courts in cases of contempt as well as in others. That with respect to disobedience of writs or orders of the courts or judges, and in refusing to answer questions as a witness, the contempt can only consist in disobeying legal writs or orders and in refusing to answer legal questions. The question what are legal writs, orders and questions is one of law simply, and is therefore often of the most difficult and complex character. Then, after quoting from the habeas corpus act, in effect that a prisoner may be discharged “when the process, though in proper form, has been issued in a case not allowed by law,” proceeds to declare that this provision applies to process issued in case of contempt. That in case of a witness committed for refusal to answer, the questions propounded, to which answer was refused, must be set out in full in the commitment, so that the appellate or reviewing court can determine whether they were legal or not, and, from their own determination, thus determine whether the party was or was not in contempt.

Comment seems unnecessary. The simple statement that the doctrine thus expressed is not in accord with that prevailing.

1 Batcheldor v. Moore, 42 Cal. 412.  
2 Ex parte Rowe, 7 Cal. 181.

<br>

### --- Page 0140 ---

§§ 464, 465.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 683

in other jurisdictions, and is foreign to all the principles upon which the question of jurisdiction is made to depend, is sufficient. If such be the rule, habeas corpus and prohibition in that state simply perform the office of a writ of error or appeal by which the mistakes of judgment of subordinate courts are corrected.

Sec. 464. Iowa—Rule in.—One other court which I will mention also made a departure from the general rule, and held that it was not competent in a habeas corpus proceeding to review and reverse an order of imprisonment for contempt, unless it can be determined as a matter of law that the act constituting the alleged contempt was not a contempt. That the decision of a court that an act is a contempt is a decision as to its jurisdiction and may be questioned in any collateral proceeding. Here lies the fallacy of the entire assumption on the part of the court. If the court correctly decides the question of law in the case, then it has jurisdiction; if it comes to a wrong conclusion, then it is without jurisdiction,—its jurisdiction being made to depend upon the result of its judgment rather than upon its power to judge upon the question. As well might the court say, if an indictment as a matter of law does not charge an offense, and the court so decides it, it has jurisdiction. If the court reaches the conclusion that an offense is charged, then it is without jurisdiction.

Sec. 465. Courts-martial.—The doctrine is well settled that the acts of a court-martial within the scope of its jurisdiction and duty cannot be controlled or reviewed in the civil courts by writs of prohibition or otherwise. Unless it appears upon the face of the proceedings that the court has no jurisdiction of any part of the subject-matter of the charges, it is not a case for prohibition. Under every system of military law for the government of either land or naval forces, the jurisdiction of courts-martial extends to the trial and punishment of acts of military or naval officers which tend to bring disgrace and reproach upon the service of which they are members, whether

1 State ex rel. Whitcomb v. Seaton, parte Reed, 100 U. S. 13; Smith v. Whitney, 61 Iowa, 563.  
2 Dynes v. Hoover, 20 How. 65; Ex parte Washburn v. Phillips, 2 Met. 299; Smith v. Whitney, 116 U. S. 167.

<br>

### --- Page 0141 ---

684 SPECIAL REMEDIES, [§ 465.

those acts are done in the performance of military duties or in a civil position or in a social or private business. “The jurisdiction of courts-martial under the articles for the government of the navy established by congress is not limited to the crimes defined or specified in those articles, but extends to any offense which, by a fair deduction from the definition, congress meant to subject to punishment, being one of a minor degree, of kindred character, which has already been recognized to be such by the practice of courts-martial in the army and navy service of nations, and by those functionaries in different nations to whom has been confided a revising power over the sentences of courts-martial; or which, though not included in terms or by construction within the definition, came within a comprehensive enactment, such as the thirty-second article of the rules for the government of the navy; which means that courts-martial have jurisdiction of such crimes as are not specified, but which have been recognized to be crimes and offenses by the usages of the navy of all nations, and that they shall be punished according to the laws and the customs of the sea.” Therefore prohibition will not lie to restrain the proceedings of a court-martial where an officer of the navy is on trial before it charged with making contracts or payments in violation of law, in disregard of the interests of the government and to promote the interests of contractors. Nor will it lie where the accused is charged before a court-martial with conduct prejudicial to good order and military discipline, where the specification shows that he is alleged to have committed a homicide, on the ground that he has been tried for that offense and acquitted in the civil courts. A plea of that character would go to the merits, not to the jurisdiction of the court. Where the application for the writ is made at the outset after charges filed, before the court has proceeded with its investigation, unless it appear upon the face of the proceedings that the court has no jurisdiction of any part of the subject-matter of these charges, no case is presented for a prohibition. Nor will the writ issue to restrain the collection of fines imposed by such a

1 Smith v. Whitney, 116 U.S. 167.  
2 United States v. Maney, 61 Fed. Rep. 140.  
3 Dynes v. Hoover, 20 How. 82.  
4 Smith v. Whitney, 116 U.S. 167.  
5 Washburn v. Phillips et al., 2 Met. 296.

<br>

### --- Page 0142 ---

§ 466.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 685

court where the subject-matter is within its jurisdiction, and its power with respect to imposing such fine is inherent in or conferred upon such court, and it has acquired jurisdiction of the person.

Sec. 466. Another adequate remedy available — Effect.— At the commencement of this chapter the rule announced by the federal supreme court was stated, which in fact recognized the discretionary power of the court under certain conditions to issue the writ where the party had another remedy. I have not found any case where the writ has been issued by that court where such conditions existed (that is, where there was another adequate remedy), and am inclined to the conclusion that the statement of the rule in the respect referred to was largely influenced by decisions of the English courts. The federal court has often placed the denial of the writ upon the ground that the party had another adequate remedy. Thus in Ex parte Gordon, where the condition was that there was no liability on the part of a British vessel for damages on account of the drowning of certain persons, the court held that the admiralty court had jurisdiction of the vessel and the subject-matter, the collision; that an appeal lay from its decree to the circuit court and from there to the supreme court, and this was a reason why the writ should not issue; and in Ex parte Ferry Co., where the question was the same, only the vessel was not foreign, it was held that as the party had the right of appeal to the circuit court, though not to the supreme court because of the insufficiency of the amount, prohibition would not lie. That it could not be made to perform the office of a writ of error, and the result would be the same in case the right of appeal was not given. And in another case it was said, in reference to an intimation in the opinion of the state court that the relator was not entitled to the writ because he had other remedies of which he might have availed himself, “this was a ground broad enough to sustain the judgment, irrespective of the decision of any federal question, if such arose.”

1 State v. Edwards, 1 McMull. 215.  
2 Ex parte Gordon, 104 U. S. 515.  
3 Yesler v. Washington Harbor Line Com’rs, 146 U. S. 646.  
4 Ex parte Ferry Co., 104 U. S. 519.  
See also Ex parte Hagar, 104 U.S. 520.

<br>

### --- Page 0143 ---

686 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 467, 468.

Sec. 467. Rule generally in state courts.—In other courts the doctrine is well settled that, where a remedy by appeal or otherwise may be had to correct an error of law or practice, the writ will not lie, and the rule extends to a court of concurrent jurisdiction proceeding with respect to the same subject-matter. In such case the inferior court, or the tribunal of limited jurisdiction, may be set right by appeal only. Where, however, the statute has imposed restrictions as to the circumstances under which such inferior court, or judge thereof, may act in matters otherwise within its jurisdiction, and these restrictions are disregarded, the New York courts hold that the party may have a remedy by prohibition; but I understand this statement to mean nothing more than that proceedings in excess of jurisdiction will be restrained. Thus, it was held that the writ ought not to issue to restrain courts at special term from proceeding, upon certiorari, to review certain proceedings, where the grounds alleged were errors in matters of practice; that error or mistake in practice affords no foundation for the writ, unless it involves the doing of something contrary to the general law of the land; that there was an adequate remedy by appeal in such cases. Again, where a person charged with bastardy had been tried before a justice, who adjudged there was not probable cause for believing the accused was the father of the child, and subsequently he was brought before another justice upon the same charge, it was held that the accused could introduce such prior adjudication as conclusive evidence in his favor, though not pleaded, and therefore prohibition would not lie.

Sec. 468. Illustrations of the application of the rule.—If, after acquiring jurisdiction of the parties and the subject-matter, a superior court should order judgment for one of the par-

People ex rel. Mayor v. Nichols, 79 N.Y. 582; Appo v. People, 20 N.Y. 531; State ex rel. Dilworth v. Braun, 79 N.Y. 582.

People ex rel. Mayor v. Nichols, 79 N.Y. 582.

State ex rel. Dilworth v. Braun, 79 N.Y. 582.

People v. Circuit Court, 11 Mich. 393.

State v. Withrow, 108 Mo. 1188.

People ex rel. Mayor v. Nichols, 79 N.Y. 582.

State ex rel. Rogers v. Burton, 11 Wis. 50; People v. Circuit Court, 11 Mich. 393; State ex rel. Dilworth v. Braun, 79 N.Y. 582.

State v. Withrow, 108 Mo. 1188.

31 Wis. 600; State ex rel. Rogers v. Burton, 11 Wis. 50.

<br>

### --- Page 0144 ---

§ 469.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 687

ties without a trial, such judgment would not be without or in excess of the jurisdiction of the court, although erroneous. In such case prohibition would not lie. The remedy would be by appeal! So the writ will not lie where a motion is pending before a court to set aside and vacate an information on the ground that the evidence before the committing magistrate showed the commission of a higher offense, to prohibit the court from examining and considering such evidence. Any error committed in the decision of a motion can be disposed of upon appeal. Such a writ cannot be issued to prescribe what a court shall or shall not consider in a matter before it and within its jurisdiction, any more than a writ of mandate can be used to compel a court how or what to decide.² Nor will it lie to restrain proceedings in a court which proceeds to determine actions against a debtor, where insolvency proceedings are pending in another court which has ordered a stay of all proceedings. The party has an adequate remedy by appeal if the former court disregarded the order, and such order can be enforced by the court where made.³ Nor to prevent the court from proceeding with the trial of an information against the accused on the ground that the magistrate before whom the preliminary examination took place did not examine on oath or otherwise the prosecutor or any other witness. In such case the accused has another adequate remedy. The matter alleged did not affect the jurisdiction of the court! Nor to restrain proceedings being taken for the incorporation of a village under a statute on the ground that such law is unconstitutional. The grievance, if any, may be redressed by ordinary proceedings at law or in equity.⁴

Sec. 469. Rule not applicable where court is proceeding against an express prohibition of a statute.—A court that is proceeding in the trial of a cause against an express prohibi-

1 Clark v. Superior Court, 55 Cal. 199. See also Tomboy Gold Min. Co. v. District Court, 23 Colo. 441.

2 Wreden v. Superior Court, 55 Cal. 504.

3 Bandy v. Ransom et al., 54 Cal. 87.

4 Murphy v. Superior Court, 58 Cal. 520.

5 In re Schumaker (Wis.), 63 N. W. Rep. 1050. See also State v. Rost, 49 La. Ann. 1051. See, however, Conn. River R. Co. v. County Com’rs, 127 Mass. 50; Sweet v. Hurlbut, 51 Barb. 312.

<br>

### --- Page 0145 ---

688 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 470, 471.

tion of a statute, thus exceeding its jurisdiction, will be restrained by prohibition, though the aggrieved party may have a remedy by appeal. Hence where a statute provided that all proceedings against an insolvent debtor must be stayed upon his application, pending the examination of the court in insolvency (except in certain cases), a court which refuses to so stay proceedings on the application of such party will be restrained by prohibition, though it may have, in the form of an order, a permit so to do, from the court in which the insolvency proceedings are pending. It was stated by one court that a writ of prohibition will not be withheld because there may exist other remedies, unless such remedies are equally effective and convenient. This statement of the rule is in advance of that held generally by courts. The better rule, and that sustained by authority, is that the writ will not be withheld where other remedies exist, unless they are speedy and adequate.

Sec. 470. Where judge disqualified.— When a judge is disqualified, by reason of interest in the subject-matter of the litigation or for any other cause, prohibition will lie to restrain him from proceeding in the action. Though there would exist a right of review upon appeal, yet under such circumstances such remedy would not be a speedy and adequate remedy.

Sec. 471. Remedy must be speedy and adequate.— The California statute states that writs of certiorari and prohibition may issue where there is not a plain, speedy and adequate remedy in the ordinary course of law. This, however, is no more, in so far as it applies to writs of prohibition, than a declaration of the common law. Thus, it was held that the writ would not be withheld to restrain a proceeding to condemn land for railroad purposes, where the law under which the proceedings were taken was unconstitutional and void in not providing for compensation, because of the fact that the remedy by petition for writ of certiorari was open to

1 Hayne v. Justice’s Court, 82 Cal. 284.  
North Bloomfield Gravel Mining Co. v. Keyser, 58 Cal. 315.

2 Ellis v. Elkin (Mo.), 30 S. W. Rep. 333,

<br>

### --- Page 0146 ---

§§ 472, 473.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 689

the land-owner after final judgment. That fact affords no sufficient reason why the petitioner should be subjected to the trouble, expense and delay of a trial before a tribunal which has no jurisdiction of the case, and to whose jurisdiction the petitioner has objected at the outset of the proceedings! So the writ will issue to restrain an unwarranted interference with the judgment of the appellate court on the part of the trial court; also to restrain the unlawful exercise of jurisdiction by one court, where another court has acquired jurisdiction of the subject-matter. As to courts of concurrent jurisdiction the writ will not lie: a party has a remedy by appeal.

Sec. 472. Judgments appealed from.—The writ will issue to restrain subordinate courts from proceeding to execute their judgments where an appeal has been properly taken. But where, by reason of the insufficiency of the bond or for other causes apparent on the record, the enforcement of the judgment is not stayed, then the party is not entitled to the writ. The same rule applies where, upon an appeal from the decision of a county board or other inferior tribunal, its judgment has been reversed, to the extent that the writ will lie to prevent it from enforcing its own judgment, regardless of the decision or judgment of the appellate tribunal. The writ, however, will not lie to prevent a court from proceeding with the trial of a cause after a motion has been made and denied for a change of venue and appealed from, though such appeal is still pending. The jurisdiction of the court to proceed is not taken away by such proceedings.

Sec. 473. Inferior courts — Splitting demands.—The writ has frequently been issued to restrain proceedings of inferior courts whose jurisdiction was limited by the amount in con-

1 Conn. River R. Co. v. County Com’rs, 127 Mass. 50. See also Sweet v. Hurlburt, 51 Barb. 312.

2 State v. Superior Court, 8 Wash. 591, 36 Pac. Rep. 443.

3 State ex rel. Merriam v. Ross (Mo.), 25 S. W. Rep. 947.

4 State v. Witherow (Mo.), 18 S. W. Rep. 41.

State v. Judge, 24 La. Ann. 598; State v. Judge, 22 La. Ann. 115.

Sweet State v. Judge, 21 La. Ann. 735; Fite v. Black, 85 Ga. 413.

Harriman v. Waldo County, Me. 83 Cal. 584. People ex rel. Scannell v. Whitney,

<br>

### --- Page 0147 ---

690 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 474, 475.

Troversy, where it was made to appear that the aggregate amount of the claims or demands was in excess of such jurisdiction, and the party had divided his claims or demands and brought more than one action, each for an amount within such jurisdiction. In such cases it is clear that the court is without jurisdiction, it not being within the power of the party to create a jurisdiction in the courts. And the rule is not changed by the fact that the claim is evidenced by several promissory notes, each of an amount within the jurisdiction of the court, and though judgment has been rendered but not paid. This rule applies where several items of personal property are pledged to secure a loan to the extent of restraining the pledgor (who has not paid the loan) from bringing a separate action in trover for each item, where the damages in each case would be of such a sum only as to render the judgment final. The rule and the reasoning for it apply as well to a claim which is in excess of the jurisdiction of the court, to the extent that the party will not be permitted voluntarily to reduce the claim to an amount within the jurisdiction of such court.

Sec. 474. Improper service of process.—So a justice may be restrained from proceeding in an action where the defendant is improperly served with process out of his own county while in attendance upon court, and also where he is proceeding without authority of law to abate a nuisance. It would seem that the doctrine stated by the California court, supra, that, where such a court proceeds in a cause against an express statute, prohibition will lie notwithstanding there may be a remedy by appeal, applies in many jurisdictions to a court clearly usurping a power, and comes also within the rule stated by the federal court, supra, with respect to discretionary writs.

Sec. 475. Removal of causes—State court proceeding notwithstanding.—There has always been some controversy with respect to the effect of the filing of a proper affidavit and bond.

1 Hudson v. Lowry, 2 Va. Cases, 42; 4 Ramsay v. Court of Wardens, 2
Girling v. Aldas, 2 Keb. 617; Bullard v. Thorpe, 66 Vt. 509.
People v. Flausberg, 74 Hun, 130.
Hudson v. Lowry, 2 Va. Cases, 42; South Carolina R. Co. v. Ellis, 40
Bullard v. Thorpe, 66 Vt. 599; Ga. 87.

<br>

### --- Page 0148 ---

§ 475. PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 

in the state court for removal of a cause to the federal court — whether the effect of such proceeding was to deprive the state court of all jurisdiction in the premises, whether it merely suspended such jurisdiction, or whether it required affirmative action on the part of the state court, in the form of an order, or that the court be judicially notified of such proceeding as a court before its jurisdiction could be said to be taken away. Circuit courts of the United States have reached different conclusions with respect to the subject. In Insurance Co. v. Pechner, 95 U.S. 188, the supreme court of the United States stated that where a state court had once acquired jurisdiction it might proceed until it was judicially informed that its power over the case had been suspended. This statement does not settle the question, because the court quite properly might hold that the court takes judicial cognizance of its own records — that which is properly filed in the court with the proper officer, the clerk. It appears, however, that the controversy is closed by a recent decision of the supreme court of the United States. It is there held that in cases under section 643 of the Revised Statutes the jurisdiction of the federal court attaches by notice to the state court from the clerk of the circuit court of the petition there filed; that in cases under section 641 the filing of the petition in the state court is of itself notice to that court, and the case in legal effect is removed. Much of the controversy is due to an early adjudication that no order of the state court for the removal of the cause is necessary; that the right of the defendant is not dependent upon the action of the state court; that the federal court may take jurisdiction without such an order. It is equally true that the rule is that if it appears to the federal court, either that the cause was one not lawfully removable, or the act by which the removal was attempted came too late, or there was a failure in compliance with essential requisites required by the law to effect such removal, the federal court will remand the cause to the state court from whence it came; and from this it is argued that the jurisdiction of the state court in any event is but suspended, or that proceedings therein are stayed. Some courts hold that

1 Virginia v. Paul, 148 U.S. 107.

<br>

### --- Page 0149 ---

692 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 475.

Prohibition will not issue to restrain the state court from proceeding with the cause where that court has not made any order in the premises and has not been judicially notified of the removal by at least an application direct to the court for an order, which has been refused, and that, upon an application for a writ of prohibition, the superior court will examine into the proceedings to the extent of determining whether the cause is one removable under the law, as well as whether the proceedings for removal have been seasonably and properly taken. The writ was refused by the Alabama court upon the ground that the removal act provided an adequate remedy by certiorari, and in another case upon the ground that it did not appear that the case was one which could properly be removed from a state to a federal court. We have seen that the federal courts cannot enjoin proceedings in the state courts, except those taken under the bankrupt law;* and hence the writ of prohibition will not issue from such courts to restrain state courts from proceeding after a cause has been properly removed. If the state court proceeds, the remedy is by writ of error, after final judgment from the highest court of the state, and not by prohibition or punishment for contempt.® If a party is forced to trial he loses none of his rights by defending against the action in the state courts. A judgment thus taken in the state court, though unlawfully and improperly taken, is valid until reversed.⁷ Where, however, a plaintiff obtaining such a judgment sends it into another state to be enforced, the circuit court of the United States into which the cause has been removed may enjoin the plaintiff from proceeding until it hears the case; and if, after hearing, it annuls the decree of the state court and dismisses as wanting in equity the bill in the suit in which the decree is made, the injunction may be made perpetual.⁸

1 Southern Pacific R. Co. v. Superior Court, 63 Cal. 607.

2 Ex parte Mobile & Ohio R. Co., 63 Ala. 349.

* Ex parte Grimball, 8 Cent. Law J. 151.

4 See, § 82 and cases cited in note.

5 Chesapeake & O. R. Co. v. White, 111 U. S. 184; Kanouse v. Martin, 14 How. 23.

® National Steamship Co. v. Tugman, 106 U.S. 118; Meyer v. Delaware Const. Co., 100 U. S. 457.

7 Johnson v. Brewers’ Ins. Co., 51 Wis. 570.

8 French v. Hay, 22 Wall. 250,

<br>

### --- Page 0150 ---

§§ 476-478.] PROHIBITION — WRIT OF. 1693

Sec. 476. Venue—Court proceeding where right to change of, established.—Where the provisions of the statute are to the effect that a change of venue may be had, where an action is brought in the wrong county, upon compliance with certain conditions, prohibition will issue to restrain the proceedings of the court which refuses to order the change after the proper demand has been made and the other requisite conditions have been complied with. So it will lie to restrain proceedings in an action to recover real estate brought in the wrong county where such provisions do not exist.

Sec. 477. New trial, granting of—Interest of judge.—The writ will lie to prevent the granting of a new trial after expiration of the trial term, and to restrain a justice of the peace from granting a new trial after the lapse of thirty days from the rendition of the judgment. (In this particular state, it seems, a justice has such power within thirty days.) Also in New York to restrain the court of oyer and terminer from granting a new trial upon the merits after a conviction for felony. The principal question of such right was elaborately discussed, and the conclusion reached that the courts had no such power. The writ lies to a judge of a subordinate court to prevent him from hearing a cause where he has a personal interest in the subject-matter, though the court over which he presides has jurisdiction of the cause.

Sec. 478. Suggestion first made in court below.—The Supreme Court of the United States, in stating the rule respecting the issue of the writ, included as one of the essential elements that the party must have made objection at the outset, in the court where the proceedings were pending, of the want of jurisdiction. The rule has been stated that, before a writ of prohibition will be granted to a court, it must have determined adversely to the petitioner on a motion or suggestion.

1 State v. Superior Court, 5 Wash. St. 518.  
2 Burroughs v. Taylor (Va.), 17 S. E. Rep. 745.  
3 Granger’s Bank v. Superior Court (Cal.), 83 Pac. Rep. 1095.  
4 Appo v. People, 20 N. Y. 531.  
5 North Bloomfield G. M. Co. v. Keyser, 58 Cal. 310.  
6 State v. Walls (Mo.), 20 S. W. Rep. 883.  
7 In re Rice, 155 U.S. 396.

<br>

### --- Page 0151 ---

694 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 479, 480.]

founded on the ground of a want of jurisdiction in the court. Such was the rule at common law. A plea was necessary, and a refusal to entertain the plea. Hence, where an injunction has been ordered contrary to the express provisions of a statute, prohibition will not issue, unless a motion has been made to dissolve the injunction and refused.

Sec. 479. Title to office.—Prohibition will not issue to test the title to an office. There is, in cases where the right is disputed, an adequate remedy by quo warranto. If it appear that there is an office de facto to be filled, and the incumbent was ostensibly appointed or elected to fill such office, then he becomes a de facto officer, whose proceedings will not be restrained, and this though it should be made to appear that the officer or body exercising the power of appointing actually did not possess it. The California court, however, hold that the legality of a grand jury may be tested by writ of prohibition, on the ground that the court is without jurisdiction, where it proceeds to try one accused of crime upon indictment not found by a lawful jury, and that the remedy by appeal, where one is given, is not a speedy and adequate remedy; and involved in the question is the legality of the appointment of the person who summons such jury, whether he is a proper officer or person duly selected or appointed under the law.

Sec. 480. Stage of proceedings when writ will issue. The courts of England in several cases discussed the question whether the writ would lie after judgment and sentence, and it seems that the rule was established that, where the jurisdictional defect appeared upon the face of the proceedings, then the writ would issue in a proper case, even after judgment or sentence. Where such jurisdictional defect was not thus ap-

1 State v. District Court, 39 Pac. Rep. 749 (Wyo.); State v. Judge, 44 La. Ann. 1083; Ex parte McMeechen, 12 Ark. 70; Ex parte Hamilton, 51 Ala. 62; State v. Judge, 29 La. Ann. 806; State v. Judge, 42 Mich. 239; Southern Pacific R. Co. v. Superior Court, 59 Cal. 471.

2 Edmundson v. Walker, Carth. 166.

3 Ex parte McMeechen, 12 Ark. 70. See also Ex parte City of Little Rock, 26 Ark. 52.

4 In re Radl, 86 Wis. 645; Hull v. Superior Court, 63 Cal. 174; State ex rel. v. McMartin, 42 Minn. 30; Epperson v. Rice (Ala.), 15 So. Rep. 484.
