Sec. 635. General principles. — We have seen in the preceding chapters that mandamus, when addressed to a public officer, is the exercise of original jurisdiction on the part of the court issuing the writ, and therefore cannot be issued by courts having appellate jurisdiction only. The general rules and principles which have been stated to be applicable where the writ is applied for when addressed to inferior courts are those which apply and govern where the application is for a writ addressed to a public officer.

<br>

### --- Page 0318 ---

§§ 636-638.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 861

Sec. 636. Executive officers.— Thus, the court will not interfere by mandamus with the executive officers of the government, whether national or state, in the exercise of their ordinary official duties; but when they refuse to act in a case at all, or when by special statute or otherwise a mere ministerial duty is imposed upon them, that is, a service which they are bound to perform without further question, then if they refuse to act or perform such duty a mandamus may be issued to compel them. The rule is stated somewhat stronger by the United States court, in substance, that the courts will not interfere by mandamus with such executive officers in the exercise of their ordinary official duties, even where those duties require interpretation of the law.

Sec. 637. Writ only issues to officers in being having the power and whose duty it is to perform the act.— The writ, however, will issue only where it appears that there is some officer in being having the power and whose duty it is to perform the act commanded by the court. Thus, the writ will not be awarded to compel county officers to do any act which they are not authorized to do by the laws of the state from which they derive their powers; nor can more be required of a public officer than the law has made it his duty to do; nor to compel such an officer to do a particular thing which his superior in authority has lawfully ordered him not to do.

Sec. 638. Discretion of public officers will not be controlled.— The rule with respect to public officers is that while a mandamus is an appropriate remedy to enforce the performance of a ministerial duty, it will not be awarded to compel an act in respect to which the officer may exercise judgment or discretion. Nor will the courts interfere with the exercise

1 United States v. Black, 128 U.S. 40; Butterworth v. Hoe, 112 U.S. 50; 18 Wall. 71; United States v. Clark, 102 U.S. 378; Alger v. Seaver, 188 Mass. 331; State v. County Judge, 7 Iowa, 425.

2 Carroll County v. United States, 40; United States v. Schurz, 102 U.S. 378; County, 95 U.S. 769; United States v. Macon County, 99 U.S. 582.

3 Ex parte Rowland, 104 U.S. 604.

4 United States v. Black, 128 U.S. 6; Butterworth v. Hoe, 112 U.S. 50.

5 People v. Leonard, 74 N.Y. 448; United States v. Guthrie, 17 How.

6 State v. Beloit, 21 Wis. 282.

<br>

### --- Page 0319 ---

862 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 639, 640.]

of such judgment and discretion when once exercised; the rule being the same in this respect as in respect to inferior courts or judicial officers. Nor will the writ lie where the party has another adequate legal remedy.

Sec. 639. Federal officers.— The difficulty of determining in a particular case whether the writ will lie against an officer is not so much in ascertaining what principles or rules are applicable, as they are reasonably certain and definite, but to determine from the facts whether or not such facts are within the principles of law which are applicable to the subject. With respect to federal officers we have seen that the supreme court of the District of Columbia is the only court from which such a writ can issue; the supreme, circuit and district courts of the United States having no original jurisdiction, and the state courts being entirely without jurisdiction.

Sec. 640. Secretary of the interior — Patents.— The writ has been applied for in several instances to compel the issuing of a patent for land, and the rule has been uniformly applied that where anything was left to be done which required the exercise of judgment or discretion on the part of the particular officer, the writ would not issue. Where, however, the proceedings had reached that stage where the act required was purely ministerial and the mere performance of a plain duty, the writ would issue. Thus, in United States v. Schurz, 102 U. S. 378, all the proceedings had been gone through, the right of the applicant had been affirmed, the patent had been made out in the land office, signed by the president, sealed with the land office seal, countersigned by the recorder of the land office, recorded in the proper book, and transmitted to the local land office for delivery. Delivery, however, was refused because instructions had been received from the commissioner to return the patent. The court held that this plea was insufficient; that the title had passed to the applicant and he was en-

284; Board of Liquidation v. Mc- v. Commonwealth, 113 Pa. St. 229;

Comb, 2 Otto, 541; Berryman v. Cicotte v. Wayne Co., 59 Mich. 502.

Perkins, 55 Cal. 483. 2 United States v. Seaman, 17 How.

1 People ex rel. Equitable Life Ins. 225,

Co. v. Chapin, 103 N. Y. 635; Dechert

<br>

### --- Page 0320 ---

§ 641.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 63

titled to his patent, subject to any equities which other parties might have to the land, or to a proceeding for setting the patent aside; that the duty of the commissioner or secretary of the interior had become a mere ministerial duty to deliver the instrument. Under existing laws patents for land are required to be signed by the president in person, or in his name, by a secretary under his direction, and they are to be countersigned by the recorder of the general land office. Such patents cannot be issued and delivered to any party without the signature of the president, and no proceeding to compel either the commissioner of the land office or the secretary of the interior to issue such a patent can be sustained.

§ 641. Patents continued.—The writ will not issue to compel the commissioner of the general land office to cause a patent to be prepared, signed, countersigned, recorded and issued where any question is left to be determined which calls for the exercise of discretion. Hence, where it appeared that there were questions of law and fact which were to be determined, some depending upon parol proof yet to be obtained, the writ was withheld. Nor will the writ be granted in such a case where it is reasonable to presume that there are persons at the time in possession under another title, and who therefore should have an opportunity to defend. A commissioner of patents, in awarding or refusing a patent to an applicant, acts in a quasi-judicial capacity. But this is only true with respect to his determination whether the applicant is or is not entitled to a patent for his invention. He cannot be controlled by mandamus in respect to such duties as are in their nature judicial. But after determining that a patent shall issue, the subsequent acts in preparing the patent for the signature of the secretary of the interior, and in countersigning it, are ministerial merely, which in case of refusal to perform will be enforced by mandamus. Nor will the commissioner be excused in such a case on the ground that the secretary of the interior

See also United States v. Stone, 23 Wall. 525.  
United States v. Edmunds, 5 Wall. 563.  
Secretary v. McGarrahan, 9 Wall. 298.  
Butterworth v. Hoe, 112 U.S. 50.

<br>

### --- Page 0321 ---

86+ SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 642, 643.

had reversed the decision of the commissioner and held that the relator was not entitled to the patent. The secretary of the interior under existing laws had no power of review or revision, in the particular case recited, over such determination. The supervision which the head of a department may exercise over his subordinates in matters administrative and executive, in the absence of express statutory authority, does not extend to matters in which the subordinate is directed by statute to act judicially. The writ will not issue to compel the commissioner to examine an application, where he has already done so, and has decided against the application.

Sec. 642. Secretary of state—Delivery of commission.—It was early held that mandamus was the appropriate remedy to compel the secretary of state to deliver commissions to certain persons who had been duly appointed to office, and whose appointments had been confirmed, where such secretary wrongfully withheld them. It was, however, decided that the supreme court of the United States was without jurisdiction to award the writ.

Sec. 643. Postmaster-general—Payment of an award.—Where by an act of congress the solicitor of the treasury was authorized and directed to settle and adjust certain claims presented on account of carrying the mails, and the postmaster-general was directed to credit the relators with whatever sum or sums of money, if any, such solicitor should so decide to be due to them, and pursuant to such authority such solicitor did make out and communicate to the postmaster-general his decision and award, by which a certain amount was found to be due the relators, and the postmaster-general carried to the credit of the relators a part only of said sum, refusing to credit the balance, it was held that under the act in question he had no discretion; that his duty in respect to making such credit was purely ministerial, and that mandamus would lie.

1 Butterworth v. Hoe, 112 U.S. 50.  
2 Marbury v. Madison, 1 Cranch, 137.  
3 Commissioner of Patents v. Whiteley, 4 Wall. 522.  
4 Kendall v. United States, 12 Pet. 834.

<br>

### --- Page 0322 ---

§§ 644, 644a. MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 865

Sec. 644. Secretary of the Treasury — Payment of claims.  
There is no power in the courts to command a withdrawal of money from the United States Treasury to be applied in satisfaction of disputed or controverted claims against the United States. Hence it was held that mandamus would not lie commanding the Secretary of the Treasury to pay to a judge of a territory his salary for the unexpired term, after he had been removed and another person appointed. It appears that the legality of the claim had been passed upon by the Secretary and other officers, and the decision was adverse. The writ was refused to compel the Secretary of the Treasury to credit on the books of the Treasury and pay a sum adjudged to be due the defendant in a suit where the United States was plaintiff. Such judgment for a set-off was held not obligatory against the United States — in fact was not a judgment; consequently no duty rested upon the Secretary to pay it. It required an appropriation of money to pay the demand, and the Secretary was not authorized to pay it in the absence of an appropriation for that purpose. It was also held in another case that the writ would not lie to compel the Secretary of the Treasury to pay to an administrator appointed in the District of Columbia, if an inhabitant of one of the states of the Union, the amount of a draft payable to the intestate at the Treasury out of an appropriation made by Congress, and held by such administrator. The reason for this ruling was that the appointment of the administrator in the District of Columbia was illegal. He should have been appointed in the state of the domicile of the intestate at the time of his death. After Congress had withdrawn the appropriation for refunding to states expenses incurred in raising volunteers, mandamus would not lie to compel the Secretary of the Treasury to deliver a warrant to one of the states for the payment of such expenses, where demand was not made prior to such withdrawal.

Sec. 644a. Secretary of the Navy — Payment of pensions. —  
It was held that mandamus would not lie to compel the pay-

1 United States v. Guthrie, 17 How. 284.  
2 Reeside v. Walker, 11 How. 272.  
3 Wyman v. Halstead, 109 U.S. 654, 4 Kentucky v. Boutwell, 18 Wall. 526.

<br>

### --- Page 0323 ---

866 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 645, 646.]

ment of a pension by the secretary of the navy (where the fund was under his control), though it appeared affirmatively on the application that, if the plaintiff was entitled thereto, the amount demanded was correct, and also that the pension fund was ample. The writ was refused on the ground that the court could not control executive officers in the exercise of the ordinary duties of their office; that the duty of determining whether the applicant was entitled to the pension claimed was judicial in character.

Sec. 645. Superintendent of public printing — Delivery of documents.—The writ was refused where it was applied for to compel the superintendent of public printing of the houses of congress to place a document in the hands of the printer of the senate instead of the hands of the printer of the house of representatives, on the ground that it involved the exercise of discretion and judgment to determine in the particular case which printer under the law was entitled to the document.¹

Sec. 646. State officers — Governor — Whether the writ lies in any case.— Courts of different jurisdictions have disagreed upon the proposition whether the writ can lie against the governor of a state in any case. The argument against the right is based generally upon the ground of the tripartite system of our government, that each department is distinct, and not subject to the control of another. To this general ground may be added another mentioned by some courts, which is that for disobedience of the mandate of the court the governor is subject like other persons to imprisonment; and as he is charged with the execution of the laws, it would contravene the policy of the law if he should be made subject to the mandate of the judiciary. Theoretically the doctrine thus announced appears to be plausible, but in the opinion of the writer rests upon no solid foundation of either principle, reason or argument. Some of the courts which hold this doctrine are consistent in that it is made applicable to all executive officers, including the secretary of state, treasury, and others of like character, while others

¹ Decatur v. Paulding, 14 Pet. 479.  
² United States v. Seaman, 17 How. 225.  
See also Brashear v. Mason, 6 How. 92.

<br>

### --- Page 0324 ---

8 647.) MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 867

seem to confine its application to the governor alone. The fallacy of the argument consists in the statement that the three departments of the government are independent of each other. They are independent in so far as they proceed within their legitimate province and perform the duties that the law requires; yet it has never been held that the executive was the sole judge of what duties the law imposes upon him, or the manner in which duties shall be exercised. The final arbiter in cases of dispute is the judiciary, and to this extent at least the executive department may be said to be dependent upon and subordinate to the judiciary. If a governor or head of a department commits an illegal act under color of his office, by which an individual sustains an injury, it cannot be pretended that his office alone exempts him from being sued in the ordinary mode of proceedings, and of being compelled to obey the judgment of the law. How then can his office exempt him from this particular mode (mandamus) of deciding on the legality of his conduct, if the case be such as would, were any other individual the party complained of, authorize the process?!

Sec. 647. Governor — Question further discussed.— The argument, however, goes to the extent that, conceding that it is within the power of the courts to determine the nature and extent of the duties devolved upon and the authority to be exercised by executive officers, it does not follow that the power rests in the court to coerce such officers in the performance of their duties; that the remedy for maladministration lies in proceedings by impeachment; and this is sound to a certain extent. It is not the office of the person to whom the writ of mandamus is directed, but the nature of the thing to be done, by which the propriety of issuing a mandamus is to be determined. So far as the powers vested in the three departments of the government are derived from the constitution, they may be said to be independent of each other, and it is extremely doubtful, at least, if the courts could interfere and control to any extent an executive officer in the performance of these duties. But beyond all that, all are subject to regulations by law touch-

Marbury v. Madison, 1 Cranch, 137.  
Marbury v. Madison, 1 Cranch, 187.

<br>

### --- Page 0325 ---

868 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 647.

ing the discharge of duties required to be performed. The executive power is vested in a governor, and so far as his powers are derived from the constitution he is beyond the reach of any department except in the mode prescribed by the constitution, through the impeaching power. Those matters within the political power of such an officer, as the authority to grant reprieves and pardons; to convene the legislature in extraordinary session; to appoint to office; to accord or withhold the executive approval to legislative bills, and the like, are among the constitutional powers thus vested by the constitution. But the legislature may impose additional duties, and such as they may deem proper, upon such an executive officer, not repugnant to the rights and duties imposed by the constitution, and in such cases the duty and responsibility grow out of, and are subject to, the duty thus enjoined, and are of a mere ministerial character. In such case he is regarded, as to the particular act, not as the executive, but as merely a ministerial officer, delegated to perform a mere ministerial duty which might have devolved upon another officer of the state.

Note.—Justice Field in the opinion rendered in McCauley v. Brooks, 16 Cal. 39, uses the following language: “The fourth article of the constitution reads as follows: ‘The powers of the government of the state of California shall be divided into three separate departments, the legislative, the executive, and the judicial, and no person charged with the exercise of powers properly belonging to one of these departments shall exercise any functions appertaining to either of the others, except in cases hereinafter expressly directed or permitted.’ There is nothing in this distribution of powers which places either department above the law or makes either independent of the other. It simply provides that there shall be three separate departments, and it is only in a restricted sense that they are independent of each other. There is no such thing as absolute independence. Where discretion is vested in terms, or is necessarily implied from the nature of the duties to be performed, they are independent of each other, but in no other case. Where discretion exists, the power of each is absolute; but there is no discretion where rights are vested under the constitution or by existing laws. The legislature may pass such laws as it may judge expedient, subject only to the prohibition of the constitution. If it oversteps those limits, and attempts to impair the obligation of contracts or to pass ex post facto laws, or grant special acts of incorporation for other than municipal purposes, the

1 Kendall v. United States, 12 Pet. 2 State v. Chase, 5 Ohio St. 528; Cotton v. Ellis, 7 Jones (N.C.), 550. 834; Harpending v. Haight, 39 Cal. 189.

<br>

### --- Page 0326 ---

§ 647.) MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 869

judiciary will set aside its legislation and protect the right it has assailed. Within certain limits it is independent. When it passes over those limits its power for good or evil is gone. The duty of the judiciary is to pronounce upon the validity of the laws passed by the legislature, to construe their language, and enforce the rights acquired thereunder. Its judgment in those matters can only be controlled by its intelligence and conscience. From the nature of its duties it must be free from coercion. But it is not independent of the legislature in numerous matters materially affecting its action and usefulness. The legislature fixes the places where courts shall be held; determines the number of their terms; and in the regulation of proceedings in civil and criminal cases provides the manner in which such suits shall be brought, prosecutions conducted, appeals taken, and all the vast machinery by which rights are asserted and wrongs redressed. In all these matters, with certain limited exceptions, the judiciary is a dependent department. To the executive department a large and important class of duties is intrusted, in the performance of which the officers are subject to no control. The governor, the head of the department, can recommend such measures as in his judgment will promote the public interest; he can approve or disapprove of such legislation as in his opinion may advance or injure the public welfare; he can exercise his discretion in numerous appointments to office; he can grant such reprieves and pardons for all offenses after conviction, except for treason and in cases of impeachment, as he may think proper; and call out the militia when he considers that proceeding necessary to suppress insurrection or repel invasion. The manner in which he shall exercise these duties rests in his sole discretion. In these matters he is independent of the other departments. But other numerous duties assigned to him arise from legislation, in which he may never have participated, or in relation to which he possessed only a qualified negative, and in the performance of which duties he has no discretion, but is subject, like every other citizen, to the law. In the distribution of powers the constitution only contemplates that different persons shall administer the different departments; that is, for example, that the governor or other member of the executive department shall not at the same time be a judge or member of the legislature. “When we speak,” says Story, “of a separation of the three great departments of a government, and maintain that that separation is indispensable to public liberty, we are to understand the maxim in a limited sense. It is not meant to affirm that they must be kept wholly and entirely separate and distinct, and have no common link of connection or dependence the one upon the other in the slightest degree. The true meaning is that the whole power of one of these departments shall not be exercised by the same hands which possess the whole power of either of the other departments, as that such exercise of the whole would subvert the principles of a free constitution. (Com. on the Const., vol. 1, sec. 525.) The truth is, no officer, however high, is above the law; and when duties are imposed upon him in regard to which he has no discretion, and in the execution of which individuals have a direct pecuniary interest, and there is no other plain, speedy and adequate remedy, he can be required to perform those duties by the compulsory process of mandamus.

<br>

### --- Page 0327 ---

870 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 648.

Sec. 648. Governor—Question further discussed.—In the case of State v. Governor, 5 Ohio St. 528, application was made to the governor to issue his proclamation, setting forth that a banking association organized as a branch of the state bank of that state was authorized to commence and carry on the business of banking, as he was required to do by statute, when he was satisfied that the law for the incorporation of the association had been in all respects complied with; and the question was raised and considered by the court as to its power to control by mandamus the action of the governor. “Can the chief executive officer of the state,” said the court, “be directed or controlled in his official action by proceedings in mandamus? It is claimed on the part of the defense that inasmuch as the government is by the constitution divided into three separate and co-ordinate departments, the legislative, the executive, and the judicial, and inasmuch as each department has the right to judge of the constitution and the laws for itself, and each officer is responsible for an abuse or usurpation in the mode pointed out in the constitution, it necessarily follows that each department must be supreme within the scope of its powers, and neither subject to the control of the other for the manner in which it performs, or its failure to perform, either its legal or constitutional duties. This argument is founded on theory rather than reality. That each of these co-ordinate departments has duties to perform in which it is not subject to the controlling or directing authority of either of the others must be conceded. But this independence arises, not from the grade of the officer performing the duties, but from the nature of the authority exercised. Under our system of government no officer is placed above the restraining authority of the law, which is truly said to be universal in its behests—all paying it homage, the least as feeling its care, and the greatest as not exempt from its power. And it is only where the law has authorized it that the restraining power of one of these co-ordinate departments can be brought to operate as a check upon one of the others. The judicial power cannot interpose and direct in regard to the performance of an official act which rests in the discretion of any officer, whether executive, legislative or judicial. . . . While the authority of the governor

<br>

### --- Page 0328 ---

§ 649.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 871  
is supreme in the exercise of his political and executive functions which depend on the exercise of his own judgment or discretion, the authority of the judiciary of the state is supreme in the determination of all legal questions involved in any matter brought judicially before it."

§ 649. Governor — Question further discussed.—It is to be regretted that the courts of the several states could not reason alike upon a proposition of so great importance and reach the same conclusion. Uniformity of the law in the several states upon questions involving the construction of provisions of the fundamental law is an object much to be desired. We find many courts disagree with the reasoning of, and the conclusions reached by, those whose judgment we have stated. Among these may be classed Arkansas, Minnesota, Florida, Illinois, Louisiana, Maine, Michigan, Mississippi, Missouri, New Jersey, Rhode Island, Tennessee and Texas. They hold to the doctrine that mandamus will not lie to the chief executive officer of a state in any case, and make no distinction with respect to ministerial or political duties—those expressly commanded by law and those which rest in discretion,—and base their conclusion upon the principle heretofore discussed, that such an officer, being the head of one of the three departments of government, is not subject to interference or control in respect to any duty by either of the other departments. While Michigan has been classed in the list of states holding this doctrine, later expression of its court would seem to warrant its

1 See also Bonner v. State, 7 Ga. 481; Page v. Hardin, 8 B. Mon. (Ky.) 649; State v. Comptroller, 18 Wend. 659; Board of Liquidation v. McComb, 92 U. S. 522; McGruder v. Swann, 25 Md. 173; State v. Kirkwood, 14 Iowa, 162; State v. Doyle, 40 Wis. 175; Cotton v. Ellis, 7 Jones (N.C.), 545; Tennessee, etc. R. Co. v. Moore, 36 Ala. 871; Groome v. Gwinn, 43 Md. 572; Gray v. State, 72 Ind. 567; State v. Blasdel, 4 Nev. 241; State v. Adams, 19 Nev. 370; Martin v. Ingham, 38 Kan, 641; State v. Warmouth, 22 La. Ann. 1; In re Dennett, 32 Me. 508; Sutherland v. Governor, 29 Mich. 320; Rice v. Governor, 19 Minn. 103; Vicksburg & M. R. Co. v. Lowry, 61 Miss. 162; State v. Governor, 39 Mo. 388; State v. Governor, 25 N. J. L. 331; Mauran v. Smith, 8 Baxt. 192 (R. I.); Houston R. Co. v. Randolph, 24 Tex. 317; Bisbee v. Drew, 17 Fla. 67; People v. Bissell, 19 Ill. 229.

<br>

### --- Page 0329 ---

872 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 649.

being placed among the states holding the contrary doctrine, for we find the expression made by that court, “that if the relator is given any right by statute, no executive officer can deprive him of it, and when it is a specific right mandamus will enforce it.”

During the year 1898 the court of appeals of New York had this important question under consideration, and adopted the logic of Judge Cooley in the case of Sutherland v. Governor, 29 Mich. 320, and reached the same conclusion, in effect, that mandamus will not lie to the governor of a state in any case. It was stated by Judge Cooley, in the case referred to, where the writ was applied for to compel the governor to perform the duty imposed upon him by statute of certifying as to the completion of certain work: “It is not claimed on the part of the relators that this court or any other has jurisdiction to require and compel the performance by the governor of his political duties, or the duties devolved upon him as a component part of the legislature. It is conceded that these, under the constitution and the laws, are to be exercised according to his own judgment and on his own sense of official responsibility, and that from his decision to act or decline to act there can be no appeal to the courts. Nor is it pretended that where any executive act whatsoever is manifestly submitted to the governor’s judgment or discretion it can be coerced by judicial writ. What is claimed is, that where the act is purely ministerial, and the right of the citizen to have it performed is absolute, the governor, no more than any other officer, is above the laws; and the obligation of the courts, on a proper application, to require him to obey the laws, is the same that exists in any other case where an official duty has been disregarded. There is no clear and palpable line of distinction between those duties of the governor which are political and those which are to be considered ministerial merely; and if we should undertake to draw one, and to declare that in all cases falling on one side of the line the governor was subject to judicial process, and in all falling on the other he was independent of it, we should open the door to an endless train of litigation. . . .

Isle Royale, etc. Corporation v. Osman, 76 Mich. 162,

<br>

### --- Page 0330 ---

§ 649. MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS.

However desirable a power in the judiciary to interfere in such cases might seem from the standpoint of interested parties, it is manifest that harmony of action between the executive and judicial departments would be directly threatened, and that the exercise of such power could only be justified on most imperative reasons. When duties are imposed on the governor, whatever be their grade, importance or nature, we doubt the right of the court to say that this or that duty might properly have been imposed upon a secretary of state, or a sheriff of a county or other inferior officer, and that inasmuch as in case it had been so imposed there would have been a judicial remedy for neglect to perform it, therefore there must be the like remedy when the governor himself is guilty of a similar neglect. The apportionment of power, authority and duty to the governor is either made by the people in the constitution or by the legislature in making laws under it; and the courts, when the apportionment has been made, would be presumptuous if they should assume to declare that a particular duty assigned to the governor is not essentially executive, but is of such inferior grade and importance as properly to pertain to some inferior office, and consequently, for the purposes of their jurisdiction, the courts may treat it precisely as if an inferior officer had been required to perform it. To do this would be not only to question the wisdom of the constitution or the law, but also to assert a right to make the governor the passive instrument of the judiciary in executing its mandates within the sphere of his own duties. Were the courts to go so far they would break away from those checks and balances of government which were meant to be checks of co-operation and not of antagonism or mastery, and would concentrate in their own hands something at least of the power which the people, either directly or by the action of their representatives, decided to intrust to other departments of government. We have now presented the theory advanced by the adherents of each side of the proposition. The New York court presented, however, another consideration which seemed to it to be of conclusive importance. They say the only way in which a mandamus can be enforced is by the commitment of the party who refuses to obey its commands, as for contempt. They have no power

<br>

### --- Page 0331 ---

SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$649.

over his person. He may be impeached, but there is no other way in which he may be deprived of his executive office.

It thus appears that the doctrine of these courts is that there is a sanctity thrown about the office that exempts the incumbent from all responsibility. The fact that he may be impeached and removed is the only means of redress for injuries resulting from refusal to perform a clear and specific duty. He may, by virtue of his office, be in possession of important public documents which it is his duty to deliver to another custodian, and important rights and interests of either a public or private character may depend upon the performance of duty in this respect, and which may be impaired or lost by delay. It is evident that the mere impeachment of the officer would in no manner secure the public right or preserve the public interests. The able discussion of this question by Justice Field which we have quoted seems conclusive; if not so, it certainly outweighs the argument advanced by the courts which adhere to the opposite view, and certainly is more in accord with the spirit of our institutions, which recognize the great principles that the source of all power is in the people, and that no man, whatever his position, is above or beyond the law. The New York court concedes that the writ may run to the lieutenant-governor and the speaker of the assembly during the recess of the legislature. They say that during the session of the legislature, they as members thereof are not subject to arrest, and it may be that courts during that time would not have the power to enforce their mandates against them; but after the adjournment of the legislature and the lapse of time given by the statute in which they are exempted from arrest, we think their obedience to the writ may be compelled by the courts. True, under the provision of the constitution they in turn may succeed to executive power, upon the happening of certain events; but, until they respectively become vested with the powers of the governor, they form no part of the co-ordinate branches of the government, except, as we have already stated, when the legislature is in session. It seems to the writer that the foregoing statement and concession on the part of the learned court overthrows and destroys the whole fabric of their

1 People v. Morgan, 156 N. Y. 136,

<br>

### --- Page 0332 ---

§ 649.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 875

argument with respect to the governor. The lieutenant-governor and speaker of the house are not, when not performing the constitutional duties of their respective offices, of the coordinate departments of the government. Such is the logic of the New York court. The logic must extend to the governor, and he is not when not performing such duties. Hence, where specific duties are imposed upon the governor, outside and beyond those which properly pertain to his duties as governor, but which he assumes, the issue of mandamus to compel their performance cannot be an interference by the courts with executive duties which pertain to his office and to the executive department of the government. They are of the same nature as though imposed upon and assumed by another. The writ in the case referred to was applied for to compel the governor, together with the lieutenant-governor and speaker of the assembly, as trustees of public buildings, to reinstate the relator in an employment in a public building, on the ground that, being a veteran, he had been removed in violation of a statute of that state; and the majority of the court held, as has been stated, that the writ would not lie to the governor even as to acts in his capacity as a trustee of public buildings. There was a vigorous dissent by Justice O’Brien. He cites many authorities to sustain his position, and his reasoning certainly commends itself to those who believe that it was never the intention of the framers of the fundamental law to place any officer above the law. He says: “I am not willing to indorse the principle that the maxim which tells us that the king is the fountain of justice and mercy and can do no wrong has any application to the elected servants of the people of the state. . Nor can I admit for a moment that the judicial power of the state is so feeble as to be unable to reach with its process, in the enforcement of its legal judgments and decrees, every citizen within its territory, from the governor to the humblest workman, and one as well as the other. The notion that the right of the citizen cannot be declared and enforced against a ministerial board of which the executive happens to be a member, because he may call out the military and naval forces of the state to resist the judgment of the court, is too trivial for serious consideration. It is the duty of the court to

<br>

### --- Page 0333 ---

876 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 649.

declare what the law is, without fear or favor, and let consequences take care of themselves. Courts cannot with self-respect frame their judgments upon the view that some power may refuse to submit to the mandate of the law, or may resist it.” Continuing, he states: “It is only a short time since a court fined all members of a board composed of all the state officers for disobeying a mandamus, and this court affirmed their action.”

The majority of the court announced another proposition which the writer thinks is not defensible. It seems that the personnel of the board had changed since the proceedings were instituted, and it was held that the writ could not go against them; that they were not parties, and had not been served with notice. It has been stated in another part of this work, when treating of the effect of judgments and upon whom they were binding, that judgments against officers in matters pertaining to the office were binding upon their successors. This principle has been supposed to be beyond controversy when acting as a board, and no court has more emphatically declared it than the court whose ruling is under discussion. In a prior case it was stated that "where a permanent official body created by statute is clothed with power and charged with the duty to do certain acts, without being limited as to time, it is an existing authority, capable of performing the duty at any time, if a former determination has been reversed or set aside. In such a case the courts have the power, and it is their duty, at any time to review the determination, when called upon, although the individuals who made it have ceased to be officers, and the record of their proceedings has passed into custody of some other authority." The supreme court of the United States awarded a mandamus against a board, of which the governor of a state was a member, and, referring to the power of the court, stated: “But it has been well settled that when a plain official duty requiring no exercise of discretion is to be performed, and performance is refused, any person who will sus-

1 People ex rel. Platt v. Rice, 114 Wend. 56; People v. Champion, 16 N.Y. 249. Johns. 60; Thompson v. United States,

2 People v. Gibson et al., 121 N. Y. 103 U.S. 60; State v. Madison, 15 Wis. 551. See also People v. Collins, 19 30; State v. Gates, 82 Wis. 210.

<br>

### --- Page 0334 ---

§§ 650, 651. MANDAMUS To PUBLIC OFFICERS. 877

tain personal injury by such refusal may have a mandamus to compel its performance. The court of appeals of New York had before reviewed the action of the same board of trustees, but it is said the point was not raised. I have given much space to the discussion of this question, deeming that its importance demands it.

Sec. 650. Governor — Application of the rule — The writ has been recognized as the appropriate remedy to compel the chief executive of a state to issue commissions to officers duly returned elected to office; to proceed to the canvass of returns of an election, where such duty devolved upon him; to draw his warrant upon the treasurer to a railway company; to draw his warrant upon the treasurer for the payment of salaries due to state officers; to sign and execute a patent for lands; to authenticate as a statute a bill in his possession, which had been duly passed and had become a law by not being returned by him within the time prescribed by law.

Sec. 651. Executive officers of state. — The rule prevailing in some states, which exempts the governor, is applied by their courts generally to all executive officers of state. Some of the courts which deny the right to issue the writ against the executive consistently hold that the exemption extends to all officers who comprise the executive department. Hence it will not lie against the governor, lieutenant-governor, secretary of state, auditor, treasurer, attorney-general, or any others, by whatever title. The commissioner of the state land office was held by the supreme court of Texas within the executive department of the state, in such a sense that mandamus would not lie against him to compel the delivery of certificates for land donated by the state in aid of a railroad company.

7 Tennessee & Coosa R. Co. v. Moore, 40 Tex. 537; Galveston B. & C. R. Co. v. Gross, 47 Tex. 428.

### --- Page 0335 ---

878 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 652.

Sec. 652. Contrary doctrine prevails in other states—Illustrations.—Other courts hold that the heads of departments, such as secretary of state, state treasurer and other elective officers, and their subordinates, are subject to the mandate of the writ in proper cases. Such, we have seen, is the doctrine of the supreme court of the United States. The great difficulty in such cases as in others is in drawing the distinction between discretionary and ministerial duties. A secretary of state was by writ compelled to revoke the license to do business in the state granted to an insurance company, the company having forfeited its right or privilege by removing an action against it in the federal court in contravention of a condition upon which the license was based not to do so. Such duty was held to be ministerial only. After such license was revoked it was held that mandamus would not lie to compel the secretary to issue a new license, not, however, upon the ground that such an act involved the exercise of discretion which could not be controlled, but upon the ground that, the court having issued its mandate, it was powerless to grant any relief. The writ has been allowed to compel such an officer to audit and allow a claim against the state, such duty being imposed by law; also to compel the board of state auditors to take action and advertise under a law for proposals for publishing the reports of the supreme court; also to compel the secretary of state to deliver the returns of an election for governor to the speaker of the house of representatives; also to compel the state treasurer to pay a demand. The writ was refused to compel a secretary of state to issue patents to state lands on the ground that such duty devolved upon the governor, and also where it was sought to compel such an officer

1 Isle Royale, etc., Corporation v. Osman, 76 Mich. 162; Ayres v. State Auditors, 42 Mich. 422; State v. Ryan, 2 Nev. App. 303; State v. Wrotnowski, 17 La. Ann. 156; State v. Barker, 4 Kan. 379; State v. Hayne, 8 S.C. 367; State v. Secretary of State, 33 Mo. 293; State v. Warner, 55 Wis. 271.

2 Ante, sec. 639 et seq.

3 State v. Doyle, 40 Wis. 175.

4 State v. Doyle, 40 Wis. 220.

5 State v. Warner, 55 Wis. 271.

6 Ayers v. State Auditors, 42 Mich. 422.

7 State v. Hayne, 8 Rich. (N.S.) 367.

8 People v. Treasurer, 87 Mich. 351.

9 Crane v. Secretary of State, 51 Mich. 195. See also State v. Harvey, 11 Ill. 33.

<br>

### --- Page 0336 ---

§§ 653, 654.] | MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 879

to count votes, where the office in question was already filled by a person holding by virtue of a commission issued to him under the election.

Sec. 653. State boards.—It is not questioned, except possibly in two or three states, that the writ issues to state boards to compel them to proceed within the scope of their legitimate powers, when they have refused, and where the duty required is ministerial merely. The writ issues to compel performance of the particular act or duty. They cannot be controlled, however, in those matters which rest in judgment or discretion, or where discretion is to be exercised. Thus, the writ will lie to compel land commissioners to make and certify transcript of proceedings for the purpose of an appeal; also to compel a board of auditors to advertise for proposals, under a law where such duty is imposed upon them; also to compel the board of public lands of a state to accept the highest bid for the leasing of certain school lands, if it is clear there is an abuse of discretion, and the sum is the full rental value of the lands; otherwise the writ will be denied.

Sec. 654. State and other auditing officers.—Auditing officers form no exception to the general rule. If the act or duty required involves the exercise of discretion, that discretion will not be controlled in the manner of its exercise, or disturbed when once exercised. Where, however, the act or duty required is directed by law and no discretion is involved, and there exists no other adequate remedy, then its performance may be enforced by mandamus. Where the duty of passing upon and determining the validity of claims against the state

1 State v. Rodman, 43 Mo. 256. See 4 Ayres v. State Auditors, 42 Mich.
contrary, under Title of Right and 422.

Title to Office. 5 State v. Scott, 17 Neb. 686.

2 People v. Illinois Dental Examiners, 110 Ill. 180; Shober v. Cochrane, 53 Md. 544; Ayres v. State Auditors, 42 Mich. 422. See, however, People v. Board of State Auditors, 32 Mich. 191.

La Chance v. Auditor-General (Mich.), 43 N. W. Rep. 1005; Peters v. Auditor, 33 Gratt. (Va.) 368; McDougal v. Bell, 4 Cal. 177; Weston v. Dane, 51 Me. 461.

3 Ex parte Lusk, 82 Ala. 519; Matter of Gardner, 68 N. Y. 467.

<br>

### --- Page 0337 ---

880 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 654.

Devolves upon such officer, then, as such duty requires the exercise of discretion, mandamus will not lie to compel him to audit the claim, but will lie to compel him to act upon the claim. He will also be compelled to proceed and audit according to law, and at the sum fixed by law, in cases where the law fixes the amount and leaves to the officer no discretion in that respect; and if it be made to appear that, through mistake or otherwise, he has audited the claim at a sum less than that fixed by law, he will be compelled to audit the claim at the required sum. Thus, where the law fixes the fees of commissioners of highways at two dollars per day, and the relator presented to the board of auditors a bill, duly verified, for twenty-seven days’ services as a highway commissioner, specifying by date each day and the particular service or duty performed, and charging the statutory fee, and such board, without disallowing any particular item, arbitrarily allowed the claim at the gross sum of $34, it was held that mandamus would issue directing the board to convene and audit the account. It was said that the only open question before the auditors was the number of days actually and necessarily spent by the relator in the performance of his duties. The statute fixed the compensation. The board did not determine the question before them. It was their duty to pass specifically upon each separate item of the claim presented. Where the law fixes the salary of a police surgeon at the sum of $2,250, and the salary of the relator when employed as such was fixed by the board of police at the sum of $1,500, and he had performed the duties for the term of two years, drawing the salary fixed by the police board, and he sought by mandamus to compel such board to draw their requisition upon the comptroller requiring him to pay over to the treasurer of said city the balance that was due him for such services, it was held that a board of officers having the power of appointment to an

Morton v. Comptroller, 48, C. 430; People ex rel. Equitable Ins. Co. v. Chapin, 103 N. Y. 625.  
People v. Chapin, 105 N. Y. 309; Johnson v. Reynolds, 44 Ala. 586;  
People v. Elmira Auditors, 82 N. Y. 80; People v. Town Auditors of Elmira, 82 N. Y. 80; People v. Board of Supervisors, 45 N. Y. 196; People v. Auditor, 2 Colo. 97.

<br>

### --- Page 0338 ---

§ 654.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 881

office cannot reduce the amount fixed by law as the salary of said office, or make a binding contract with their appointee to perform the duties of the office for a less sum, and that it was the duty of the board to draw a requisition for the amount, the performance of which could be compelled by mandamus.

Charter provisions sometimes are to the effect that no action shall be maintained upon any claim against a city until it shall have been presented to the common council and disallowed. That the only remedy in such case is that of appeal from such decision. It is clear, and so it has been held, that mandamus will lie to compel the proper board to act; yet such remedy is not exclusive where the board unreasonably delays. The claimant has an election of remedies,— either mandamus or an action at law upon his claim. Other provisions quite frequently are to the effect that no money shall be drawn from the treasury except on an order signed by the mayor and clerk; and from this it has often been urged that mandamus to compel the proper officers to issue the order is the only remedy where a claim has been allowed by the proper board; such remedy being available in many jurisdictions where, by the regulations of a municipal corporation, money in the treasury is appropriated to the payment of claims which have been audited and allowed, as hereinbefore stated. Such remedy, however, is not exclusive. The claimant may, if he sees fit, resort to a civil action against the municipality to recover the debt. Where a city had compromised a claim by allowing a less sum than demanded, which was accepted by the claimant making a demand for an order for the amount allowed, it was held that he might maintain an action at law upon such contract thus created, though mandamus would properly lie, and that the provisions of the city charter providing for the presentation of claims and appeal from a disallowance were not applicable. In fact, the claim had been presented, audited and allowed, though at a less sum. The purposes of the statute had been fully met and satisfied.

1 People v. Board of Police, 75 N.Y. 388.
2 Sharp v. City of Mauston, 92 Wis, 629.
3 Kraft v. City of Madison, 98 Wis. 252.
4 Same case.

<br>

### --- Page 0339 ---

882 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$$ 655, 656.

Sec. 655. Warrants for salaries—Issue of by state and other officers.—The act of issuing warrants for salaries of state officers is merely ministerial. The compensation or salary is fixed by law, and an auditor may be compelled to perform this duty by mandamus. In fact it is held the appropriate remedy, and without reference to whether the party may have another remedy. Recent cases in the New York courts are to the effect that mandamus does not lie to compel the payment of salaries; that such claims are merely common-law demands for money, and the remedy is by an action at law the same as for the collection of any other debt.

Sec. 656. Warrants for awards and other purposes — Issue of.—With respect to the payment of awards and other claims, or drawing warrants therefor, it has often been urged that mandamus would not lie because the relator had an adequate remedy at law to recover the money consideration. Yet in many instances, where the award is certain, depending upon no contingency or claim, and the amount has been definitely determined, so that it is not subject to controversy, it has been held that the existence of a right of action would not operate to bar proceedings by mandamus upon the ground generally that it would cause unnecessary delay and expense, and such an action was not necessary to determine a right which was closed and beyond further controversy. Thus it was held that, while a party injured by the laying out of a road might have an action against the municipality for the amount awarded him by a jury duly appointed for that purpose, the court would compel the proper officers to audit his account for the damages awarded, and take steps for the collection of the same. Among other reasons stated, it was said that, in case the relator should bring his action against the municipality and recover judgment, he would have to proceed by mandamus to levy a tax to pay it. In another case, where the county commissioners having accepted, as completed according to contract, a road.

1 Speed v. Common Council, 98 Mich. 360; Bryan v. Meloney, 13 Cal. 621; Danley v. Whiteley, 14 Ark. 687; People v. Board of Education, 148 N. Y. 766; People v. Roosevelt, 48 N. Y. Supp. 1043.

2 People v. Secretary of State, 58 Ill. 90.

3 State v. Wilson et al, 17 Wis. 687.

<br>

### --- Page 0340 ---

§ 657.) MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 883

improvement ordered by them to be made in pursuance of a statute authorizing payment to be made in bonds of the county, to be issued to the contractor on completion of the improvement and acceptance thereof, and the commissioners had refused to deliver to the contractor the bonds to which he was entitled, that mandamus would lie to compel them to comply with the law in performing the duty; that, having thus accepted the road, their discretion had been exercised and exhausted; that redress could not be had by appeal or by action! Where lands had been legally and regularly condemned by a city for a street, and the city auditor directed by ordinance to issue his warrant on the treasurer to pay therefor upon the receipt of proper vouchers from the city solicitor, whose duty it was to furnish the vouchers, and he refused to furnish them for the reason that in his opinion the condemnation proceedings were irregular and invalid, it was held mandamus would issue to compel him to perform that duty; that, on receiving the vouchers, the duty of issuing the warrants on the treasurer was simply ministerial, and he in like manner would be compelled to perform the duty by mandamus?

Sc. 657. Duty in some instances ministerial, in others not.— In fact, it has been distinctly held that mandamus is the proper remedy against an auditor who refuses to issue a warrant when directed to do so by the board of supervisors; that an action on an auditor’s bond is not a plain, speedy and adequate remedy. In another jurisdiction it has been held that mandamus will not issue to compel a state auditor to audit a claim where the party has an adequate remedy by appeal. It has also been held that where the claim is payable only out of a fund specifically appropriated, and such fund has been exhausted, mandamus will not lie to compel the auditor to issue a warrant or the treasurer to pay it. Where, however, a statute provides that in all cases where the law recognizes a claim for money against the state and no appropriation has been made by law to pay the same, the auditor shall audit and

1. Commissioners v. Hunt, 33 Ohio St. 169.  
2. Ryan v. Hoffman, 26 Ohio St. 109.  
3. Babcock v. Goodrich, 47 Cal. 488.  
4. State v. Babcock, 22 Neb. 38.  
5. State v. Porter, 89 Ind. 260.  

<br>

### --- Page 0341 ---

8st SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 657.

settle it and issue a certificate of the amount if demanded, the issue of the certificate may be compelled; but mandamus will not lie to compel its payment. Stenographic reporters appointed pursuant to a statute, which fixes their compensation, may compel by mandamus the state auditor to issue a warrant for payment thereof. So, where an act fixes the compensation of code commissioners and provides, upon the filing of one of the completed codes with a designated officer, the auditor shall draw his warrant for the pro rata salaries of the commissioners, mandamus will issue to compel the auditor to issue such a warrant. The terms of the act constitute an appropriation of the money required. The court will not review on such a proceeding the alleged improper inclusion by the commissioners or board of unauthorized items of damage in their awards.

Where materials were furnished to a city under contract, which provided, among other things, that the certificate of the engineer, approved by the commissioner of public works, shall be conclusive upon the contractor, and the express purpose of this provision was to prevent all disputes and litigation between the parties, the certificate of the engineer was held to be conclusive between the parties. Such having been declared to be its effect, it was further held that the comptroller’s duties in approving and issuing the warrant for the payment of such claim were merely ministerial. That it was not the intention of the statute requiring the comptroller to approve claims against the city to give him any judicial power in regard to a claim, where, by the agreement under which it arose, the action of the engineer and commissioner was to be final and conclusive.

In another case the relator entered into a contract with a city to construct a sewer. The contract provided that no payment shall be made until the cost of the work shall have been assessed upon and collected from the taxpayers liable. The

1 Files v. State, 42 Ark. 233; State v. Clinton, 28 La. Ann. 47, see also Rep. 1092.  
Gilbert v. Moody (Idaho), 25 Pac. Rep. 1092.  
Livingston v. McCarty, 20 Pac. Rep. (Kan.) 478, see also State v. Kenney (Mont.), 26 Pac. Rep. 999.  
People v. Haws, 23 How. Pr. 107; People v. Connolly, 2 Abb. Pr. (N. S.) 315; People v. Green, 1 Hem. L. 6.  
People v. Fitch, 147 N. Y. 356.  
Matter of Freel, 148 N. Y. 165.

<br>

### --- Page 0342 ---

§ 657.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 835

Sewer was completed, accepted and used by the city, and its common council adopted a resolution directing the city assessors to assess the costs upon the abutting property. This was done, but only a portion of the assessment was collected, and nothing further was done to enforce it. The common council, by resolution, declared its intention to issue improvement bonds, as authorized by the city charter, to pay for the work, but this was not done. The contract provided that all plans, explanations and directions necessary to carry out the work should be given to the city engineer, and upon its completion and final acceptance by the engineer he was required to make up a final account and return the same to the common council, whereupon it was provided that that body should receive and, when satisfactory, approve the same. It appeared indisputably that the engineer gave the plans and directions as required, and that the contractor followed the directions. The engineer returned the amount of the work, etc., and certified the amount to be correct and that the work had been performed according to the contract. The common council ratified the acceptance. The contractor applied for a peremptory writ of mandamus requiring the mayor and common council to complete the collection of the assessment or to issue the bonds. It was alleged on the part of such officers that the return was given under a mistaken idea of the facts and that the certificate was false and incorrect. It was held that neither the city nor its officers were in a situation to attack the return and certificate in the mandamus proceeding. The city was not a party to the proceeding; and, being bound by the return and certificate, its ministerial officers were also bound thereby. Under these certificates any evidence introduced tending to show the return and certificate incorrect was incompetent; that the appellants, acting as ministerial officers, stood, as to the money already raised for the relator by taxation and in their hands, as do town railroad commissioners who have received money raised by tax to pay interest on town bonds; and it has been held that the commissioners could not draw in question the validity of the bonds, even if the town had authorized the defense by resolution of town meeting. It was further adjudged that the

1 Citing First Nat. Bank v. Wheeler, 72 N. Y. 201.

<br>

### --- Page 0343 ---

886 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 658.

Relator was entitled to a mandamus requiring the city authorities to complete the collection of the assessment or to issue the bonds. It is broadly stated by the California court that, if illegal claims are allowed by a board of supervisors against the county, it is the duty of the auditor to refuse to draw warrants therefor, and when the warrants are drawn it is the duty of the treasurer to refuse to pay them. This doctrine implies that such officers have a judicial discretion which they may exercise in respect to such duties. I hardly think it is intended to state such a rule; but, on the contrary, that such an officer, if he refuses, must take his chances. If payment is sought by mandamus; if, as matter of law, the claim is illegal and beyond the power of the board to allow, then the court will not enforce it. Where a county board had employed an attorney to act as counsel for the board, and his bill for services was allowed, but the auditor refused to draw a warrant, contending that the action of the board was illegal, it being the duty of the district attorney to act as its legal adviser, the court refused to award the writ.

Sec. 658. Payment of awards and other claims.—Where a work of public necessity is done under an invalid contract or even voluntarily, without the authority of any public officer, and the legislature, or other public body clothed with similar power, appropriates money to pay for it, a disbursing officer cannot refuse to apply the money to the purpose for which it was appropriated, on the ground that the state or municipality was not originally under any legal obligation to make payment, or that the legislative body was not sufficiently informed of the facts. The only question for such officer is whether the appropriation was for the purpose claimed; when this is ascertained his duty is ministerial only. Where, however, it is sought to compel the payment of an award of appraisers or other special tribunal created by statute with limited powers, the question of their jurisdiction is always open and may be raised by the

1 People v. Mayor and Common Council of Syracuse, 144 N. Y. 63.  
2 Linden v. Case et al., 46 Cal. 172; Merriam v. Barnum, 116 Cal. 619.  
3 Merriam v. Barnum, 116 Cal. 619.  
4 People v. Schuyler, 79 N. Y. 189.

<br>

### --- Page 0344 ---

§ 659. MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 887

respondent in such proceedings. For instance, it may be shown that the award covers items or matters that were not within their jurisdiction to determine, the duty not having been conferred upon them. If power was conferred to ascertain and determine the damage sustained by reason of land being appropriated for the deposit of shale and stone, it cannot extend to a determination of the damages sustained by reason of the destruction by fire of fences and timber on the land. Nor is it an answer to such lack of jurisdiction to say that the legislative body (where the claim is against the state or a public corporation) has, since the making of the award, made appropriations of money to pay awards in general. It must appear that it was the intention of such legislative body to include the particular award in such appropriation.^1

§ 659. Payment of awards and other claims continued.

Also it has been held that in mandamus proceedings against a town treasurer to compel the payment of an order drawn by the town clerk on a claim allowed by the town board, it is competent for him to allege that the order was erroneously issued, that the claim is fraudulent, and that in fact the town was not indebted upon the claim, as justifying his refusal to pay. In such a case the writ will not issue, but the petitioner will be remitted to his action at law to establish therein the validity of his claim. The rule declared by the court was that mandamus would not issue against a municipal corporation to compel the payment of a claim unless in a clear case of indebtednes.^2 A similar rule was declared by another court. It was said it is the duty of a county treasurer under ordinary circumstances to pay the warrants drawn according to law by the board of county commissioners, when he has funds in his hands for that purpose. If, however, he has reasonable grounds to question the legality of the warrant or the power of the commissioners to draw the same, he is justified in refusing to pay such warrant until the validity of the same is established by the judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction.^3

1 People v. Schuyler, 69 N. Y. 242.
2 Simmons v. Davis (R. I.), 25 Atl W. Rep. 721.
3 Evans v. Bradley (S. Dak.), 55 N. Rep. 691.

<br>

### --- Page 0345 ---

88S SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 660.

Src. 660. Payment of awards and other claims — Duty ordinarily ministerial. — It has frequently been held that the mere act of payment of a claim which has been duly examined, audited and allowed by the proper officers of a municipal corporation is purely ministerial, which, if true, would preclude the disbursing officer upon whom the warrant is drawn from exercising any judgment upon the question of the invalidity of the claim for which the warrant was drawn, or passing in review upon the judgment of the proper auditing officers or board, and thus place his judgment against theirs. It may be true that, where he is advised that clearly a fraud was perpetrated upon such officer or board which induced the allowance of the claim and the issuing of the order, he would be justified in refusing to pay, as well as where the claim was clearly without authority or sanction of law, as in such case it would be without the jurisdiction of such auditing person or body. And those matters could be urged as a defense in mandamus proceedings against him to compel him to pay the warrant where the issue was properly made by answer or return to an alternative writ, and the court would determine these questions. But to say that such an officer may arbitrarily refuse payment, and thus override the judgment of the auditing power, is certainly going a great ways toward depriving such tribunals of the discretionary power with which they are supposed to be clothed, and compelling persons who have lawful demands against the municipality about which there is no dispute to resort to the courts, at an expense to the municipality, to establish that which is beyond dispute. The disbursing officer would not be a party to such suit. The body ordinarily in case of claims against towns and counties which have declared the claim valid would control the proceedings on the part of the municipality. There is much of force in the doctrine declared by the New York court, in effect, that no action will lie against the municipality upon a claim that has been presented, ex-

1 Thomas v. Town of Mason, 39 W. Va. 526; Ireland v. Hunnel (Iowa), 57 N. W. Rep. 715; Cloud v. Town of Sumas (Wash.), 87 Pac. Rep. 305; Baker v. Johnson, 41 Me. 15; People v. Mahoney, 30 Mich. 100; Hendricks v. Johnson, 45 Miss. 644; Potts v. Va. 526; Abernethy v. Town of Medical Lake (Wash.), 37 Pac. Rep. 806; Beeney v. Irwin (Colo. App.), 39 Pac. Rep. 900.

2 People v. Wendell, 71 N. Y. 171.

<br>

### --- Page 0346 ---

§ 661.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 889

amined and allowed, and for the payment of which provision has been made; and it is upon this ground that mandamus has been allowed against the officers whose duties are to examine, audit and allow claims. Where there is a plain and imperative duty on the part of a public official to pay over money which has come into his hands for such specific purpose, the doctrine of the California court is that mandamus will lie to compel such payment; and it necessarily follows from the law of that state as declared that there is no other plain, speedy and adequate remedy. Thus, where the superintendent of streets had collected money in his official capacity, a part of which was for services rendered by the city engineer, it was held that mandamus would lie to compel him to pay it to the party entitled thereto.

Sec. 661. Right of action will not preclude the writ.— The objection is always present that mandamus will not lie where the party has an adequate remedy by law, and it is often urged that the party may bring an action in the courts upon his warrant. This is true in some of the states; but, as was urged and decided by the Wisconsin court, such a remedy necessarily does not bring the party any nearer a conclusion. The remedy by mandamus at the present day is to a great extent in the nature of a civil action, and all questions urged against the validity of a claim that has been audited can be urged and tried in such proceeding. There is no necessity for resorting to an action at law; the alternative writ may be used to inaugurate the proceeding. It is true that in some states it is held that an action against the municipality may be brought upon warrants or orders that have been issued, and such a practice, especially in Wisconsin, has prevailed, whereby municipalities have been subjected to great and unnecessary cost and expense. The judgment, however, when obtained must be collected through the agency of the municipal authorities. As execution will not issue, mandamus must still be resorted to to compel the levy of a tax to pay the judgment, if a levy will not volun-

1 Martin v. Supervisors, 29 N. Y. 645.
2 Fitzhugh v. Ashworth (Cal), 51 Pac. Rep. 635.
3 State v. Wilson, 17 Wis. 687.

<br>

### --- Page 0347 ---

890 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 661a.

tarily be made. It is apparent, therefore, that an action at law is not a plain, speedy and adequate remedy in such cases, and it has been so directly held. Thus, where a board of supervisors, with authority under general laws to erect a county jail and provide the means to pay for the same, contracted for the erection of such a structure, and directed the auditor to issue a county warrant for an amount due upon a contract for building the same, it was held that the auditor had no discretion, but must issue the warrant, and mandamus would issue to compel him to perform that duty; that he could not justify his refusal upon the ground that there was no money in the treasury, nor could he set up his judgment in opposition to others in respect to whether the warrant ought to issue upon any ground, which it was the province of such others to determine. In one state at least, however, it seems to have been decided that mandamus will not lie to compel the levy of a tax for the payment of warrants of a similar character, prior to suit brought and judgment obtained upon them.?

§ 661a. Warrants drawn on special fund.— Where a warrant has been drawn upon a special fund for a particular indebtedness, mandamus is the only remedy to enforce its payment. The reason is that, being payable out of a special fund, no action at law lies to enforce it. It has been held, however, that the treasurer of a city was so far subject to the direction of the city council that he would not be compelled by mandamus to pay warrants which the city had directed him not to pay. Nor could a school board be compelled to make a special levy for the payment of orders issued in violation of a constitutional provision forbidding the incurring of any indebtedness on the part of the board of education, without first submitting all questions relating thereto to a vote of the people.

1 Babcock v. Goodrich, 47 Cal. 488. 4 State v. Cook, 43 Neb. 318.

2 State v. Trustees, 61 Mo. 155. Compare State v. Treasurer, 43 Mo. 288.

3 State v. Justices, 48 Mo. 475; Commissioners v. Hunt, 33 Ohio St. 169.

5 Dempsey v. Board of Education, 205. W. Va. R. 811.
