CHAPTER 32.

MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS — Continued.

Sec. Src.
662. Clerks of court — Their duties largely ministerial. | 676. Duty in respect to writ of restitution.
663. Executions — Issue of. | 677. Registers of conveyances and documents.
664. Action for damages may not be an adequate remedy. | 678. County clerks.
665. Other duties. | 679. Public officers generally — Rights and duties of — Entitled to that which pertains to the office — Insignia, books and papers.
666. Delivery of books and papers — Reporting fees. | 680. Officer retiring must deliver records, etc., to successor — Title to the office cannot be tried — Certificate of election conclusive as to the right to receive the records.
667. Certificates of election — Issue of — Restoration to office. | 681. Application of the rule — Incidents.
668. Prohibiting recording votes cast under an invalid law — Testing constitutionality of the law. | 682. Writ only lies to compel performance of acts incident to some office or position of trust, not to private persons.
669. Inspection of papers and records. | 683. Records of public officers open to inspection.
670. Approval of bonds.
671. District attorneys — Duties largely discretionary.
672. Sheriffs — Duties largely executive.
673. Executing conveyance of land.
674. Amending return.
675. Sale of property.

Src. 662. Clerks of court — Their duties largely ministerial.— The duties prescribed by law to be performed by clerks of court are largely ministerial in character, though in some cases they are judicial. The power of the courts is ample to compel a clerk to perform such duties as are purely ministerial in case of refusal, where there exists no other adequate remedy at law. The difficulty encountered with respect to such officer and his duties lies mainly in determining whether there exists in the particular case a remedy at law, and if it is one that is adequate.

Sec. 663. Executions — Issue of.— It appears to be the rule established in some of the states that mandamus will not lie to

<br>

### --- Page 0349 ---

$92 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 664,

compel a clerk of court to issue an execution on a judgment for money only. The reason given in some cases is that the party has a plain, speedy and adequate remedy by an action on his official bond, and in others that he can apply to the court for an order directing the clerk to perform his official duties. While the cases cited from California are not expressly overruled, yet in a case decided in 1894 (Garonette v. Haley, 104 Cal. 479) it was held that mandamus was the appropriate remedy to compel the clerk to issue an execution upon a money judgment. Where, however, it is the duty of the clerk to issue writs or executions for the enforcement of judgments, not purely for a specific sum of money, then mandamus will lie. The reason given is that in such cases a suit upon his official bond for damages resulting from a non-performance of that duty would be wholly inadequate. Where, however, a statute provides that, on the performance of his duty, the clerk shall be under the direction of the court, mandamus will not lie to compel the clerk to issue an order of sale upon a decree of foreclosure of a mortgage, where no application has been made to the court to direct the clerk to issue such order.

Sro. 664. Action for damages may not be an adequate remedy. In 23 Wend. 461, it was said “that although as a general rule a mandamus will not lie where the relator has another remedy, it is not universally true where the writ is sought against ministerial officers. Notwithstanding they may be liable in an action on the case for a neglect of duty, they may be compelled by mandamus to exercise their functions according to law.” It is true that in a subsequent case the New York court of appeals repudiated this exception as abstractly stated; yet we have seen that such court and others state the doctrine generally to be, that an action for damages against an officer for neglect of duty is not equally convenient, beneficial and effective as the proceeding by mandate, since it would not compel him to do the specific act which 

1 Goodwin v. Glazer, 10 Cal. 333; Attorney-General v. Lum, 2 Wis. 507. Fulton v. Hanna, 40 Cal. 278, See sec. 581.  
2 Pickett v. Owen, 66 Iowa, 485.  
3 State v. Moores (Neb.), 45 N. W. Rep. 278.  
4 Carpenter v. Loucks, 28 Cal. 69;  
5 People v. Supervisors, 11 N. Y. 563.

<br>

### --- Page 0350 ---

§ 665. MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 893

the law requires. It is equally clear that such remedy is not equally speedy, nor should the party be compelled at cost and expense to pursue such a remedy and subject himself to the uncertainties involved in an action against sureties, who may or may not be responsible or liable. With respect to the case cited, as decided by the Iowa court, the decision was placed upon the provisions of a statute, and there existed a speedy remedy by motion.

§ 665. Other duties.—The writ lies to compel the clerk to perform other duties prescribed by law where the right is clear, such as furnishing copies of the records of the court, recording papers, furnishing transcripts, and the like. The clerk when required to make a transcript of appeal cannot question the correctness of the court’s minutes, nor plead the nullity of the appeal, for instance, on the ground that the judge did not sign the minutes on the last day of the term; yet the writ will not lie to compel the furnishing of a transcript when the writ of error has not been issued; nor in a case where the record could not be used on account of the time having expired, or other impediment exists that prevents under the law the case being heard.

Note.—It would seem, however, that the clerk is invested with no such discretion, and the court would not determine upon the return any such collateral question. The duty of the clerk is clear and mandatory.

Where the clerk has tendered a transcript on appeal which the party refuses to accept on the ground that it is imperfect, it has been held that mandamus would not lie to compel him to furnish another until after the question of the imperfections in the one tendered has been determined. No reason can be urged why such matters may not be determined in the mandamus proceedings.

1 Babcock v. Goodrich, 47 Cal. 488; McCullom, 83 Ky. 361; State v. Mayo, People v. Supervisors, 11 N. Y. 563; 48 La. Ann. 367.  
People v. Mead, 24 N. Y. 114; People v. Hawkins, 46 N. Y. 9.  
State v. Mayo, 42 La. Ann. 640.  
Ex parte Ralston, 119 U. S. 613.  
2 Moore v. Muse, 47 Tex. 210; State v. Megher, 57 Vt. 898; Wulftange v.  
5 Roberts v. Small, 63 Ga. 613.  
6 State v. Clark, 46 La. Ann. 1289,

<br>

### --- Page 0351 ---

894 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 666-669.]

Sec. 666. Delivery of books and papers—Reporting fees.  
Mandamus is the appropriate remedy to compel the delivery by a clerk of the records of his office and official seal to a person showing himself prima facie entitled to them; also to compel him, even after the expiration of his term of office, to report all the fees of his office, and to pay what by law is required thereof into the county treasury.

Sec. 667. Certificate of election—Issue of—Restoration to office.—The writ lies to compel the clerk to issue a certificate of election to a person whom the proof shows is clearly entitled thereto, where such duty is imposed by law. Nor can he refuse to perform this duty upon the ground that he has, without direction from a court, issued a certificate to another, and that such other has been regularly commissioned by the governor. (For cases relating to title of office see subsequent chapter.) It is also the proper remedy to restore a clerk who has been illegally removed from office by the appointment of another person.

Sec. 668. Prohibiting recording votes where the election law is invalid.—Testing constitutionality of law.—The writ will lie to prevent a clerk and county judge from recording a vote, such being their duty under valid laws, where the act under which the election was held is, in fact, unconstitutional. The constitutionality of a law may be determined upon such a proceeding. Thus, where a local-option law was submitted to the electors of a county for adoption, it was held that the question of the constitutionality of such law could thus be tested, and the writ would issue to prevent the recording of the vote where the law was determined to be void.

Sec. 669. Inspection of papers and records.—Mandamus will lie to compel an inspection of public records, subject to reasonable conditions. After a public trial or hearing and a determination of the cause entered upon the records of a court,

1 Driscoll v. Jones (S. D.), 44 N. W. 3  
People v. Matterson, 17 Ill. 167; Rep. 726. People v. Rives, 27 Ill. 242.  
2 State v. Shearer, 29 Neb. 477, 45 N. W. Rep. 784. See State v. Schofield, 41 Mo. 38,  
3 Dew vy. Judges, 3 Hen. & M. (Va.) 1.  
5 Gayle v. County Court, 88 Ky, 61.

<br>

### --- Page 0352 ---

§ 670. MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 895

Such record has then become a public record which any person has the right to inspect. A distinction, however, is drawn between papers which are filed with the clerk preliminary to a hearing and public trial, and those which relate to the case after judgment has been rendered. The former are not open to inspection, and therefore mandamus will not lie to compel the clerk of the court to permit persons other than the parties to inspect such papers. It is further held that, independent of the doctrine thus stated, parties to a suit may, under the direction of the court, lawfully withhold the records and papers in a case, and prevent any statement in regard thereto being made public, until they are made public by the consent of the parties or by proceedings in open court. The publication of a judgment or decree is privileged, and affords no basis for an action of libel. This is not so where one publishes the statements or charges made in the pleadings before their truthfulness has been determined by the judgment or decree of the court.

Sec. 670. Approval of bonds.—The duty of a clerk with respect to the approval of bonds is at least quasi-judicial in character. Mandamus, therefore, will not lie to compel him to approve a bond, where he refuses upon the ground that the sureties are insufficient. Yet I apprehend the rule to be, that if the clerk arbitrarily refuses without sufficient cause to approve a bond, where it clearly appears that the sureties are amply sufficient, and such refusal plainly appears to be an abuse of discretion, the party having no other adequate remedy, the writ will issue. Where the clerk is vested with no discretion as to the approval of the bond, as where the law prescribes its conditions, and the sufficiency of the sureties is determined by acts on the part of the sureties definitely required, and the duty of the clerk is made imperative, then, in case of refusal to

1 Cowley v. Pulsifer, 187 Mass. 3938;  
2 Schmedding v. May (Mich.), 48 N. W. Rep. 201.  
3 Cowley v. Pulsifer, 137 Mass. 892;  
4 Mobile Mutual Ins. Co. v. Cleveland, 76 Ala. 321; Swan v. Gray, 44 Miss. 393.  
5 Ex parte Thompson, 52 Ala. 98; Swan v. Gray, 44 Miss. 393.

<br>

### --- Page 0353 ---

896 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 671, 672

Approve the bond, the writ will issue. Where a clerk refuses to act or determine the questions that may be subject to his discretion, then the writ will lie to compel him to act and to determine such question, but not to compel his approval. So also, where he bases his refusal upon a ground that is clearly insufficient in law, the writ will lie. We have seen (ante) that a judge must in his answer to an alternative writ state definitely his reasons for refusing to settle or sign a bill of exceptions, and I assume the same rule applies to a clerk who has refused to approve a bond.

Sec. 671. District attorneys — Duties largely discretion.— District attorneys, by virtue of the character of their duties, ordinarily are vested with a discretion in their exercise that cannot be controlled by proceedings in mandamus. Where an act empowered the district attorney of a county and made it his duty to institute suit in the name of the county against any person or persons to whom money has been actually paid under order of the board of supervisors, without authority of law, to recover the money so paid and damages for its use, it was held that the district attorney was vested with a discretion in determining whether or not in a particular instance he should bring an action under that section, which a court could not control by mandamus. Where the code of a state provided that an action may be maintained on the information of the prosecuting attorney against one usurping an office, it was held that a discretion was thus vested in such officer whether he ought or would institute such a prosecution which was beyond control by mandamus.

Sec. 672. Sheriffs — Duties largely executive.— The ordinary duties which attach to the office of sheriff are executive, though like most other officers of a state or municipality there are many duties thus imposed that are to some extent judicial, and others that are purely ministerial in character. The rule being well settled that the performance of ministerial acts only will

1 Gullick v. New, 14 Ind. 93; People v. Fletcher, 2 Scam. 482.
2 Mobile Mut. Ins. Co. v. Cleveland, 76 Ala. 32.
3 Boym v. Ryan, 100 Cal. 265.
4 Everding v. McGuire, 23 Oreg. 15.

<br>

### --- Page 0354 ---

§§ 673, 674.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 897

be compelled, though such officer may be compelled to proceed to the performance of duties of a judicial character, the inquiry is limited to determine those of the former character.

Sec. 673. Executing conveyance of land.—It is the duty of the sheriff to execute a conveyance of land sold on execution to the purchaser, and this act being one that is purely ministerial in character, he will in case of refusal be compelled to perform the act, and this though he had issued a prior deed to another claiming to be the purchaser; and it has been held that upon such proceeding the court may determine the question of the right of the relator. Yet if it appear that the sale thus made is invalid by reason of non-compliance with the provisions of law or otherwise, as that several distinct parcels were sold for an aggregate price, his duty being to offer each tract separately, then, as the relator’s right is not clear, the writ may be refused. And if it is made to appear that the title to a tract of land is in the name of the wife of the judgment debtor, mandamus will not issue to compel him to levy upon such property, for the same reason, that the relator’s right is not clear. Another reason has been given in effect that the aggrieved party has a right of action against the officer if he unjustly or improperly refuses to execute the writ. In many states, as we have seen, this reason is not sufficient, and such a remedy is not deemed to be adequate. In others it is adequate where the judgment involved is one for the payment of money only.

Sec. 674. Amending return.—While a sheriff may of his own will correct his return so as to conform to the facts in proceedings for the sale of land for taxes, yet it seems he cannot be compelled to do so by mandamus, where he insists that the return is according to the facts. Hence he cannot be compelled to deliver a deed to a purchaser at a tax sale, containing recitals which would be in contradiction of those stated in the return. Where such an officer has levied an attachment upon exempt property, and the defendants have filed an inventory of all their property as required by a statute, and they claim

1 People v. Fleming, 4 Denio, 137.  
2 Winters v. Heirs of Buford, 6 Cold.  
3 State v. Craft, 17 Fla. 722.  
4 Hewell v. Lane, 53 Cal. 213, 328.

<br>

### --- Page 0355 ---

898 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 675, 676]

the property to be exempt, it then becomes his duty to proceed to appraise such property, and if he refuses mandamus will compel him to do so.1

Sec. 675. Sale of property.— A sheriff has a discretion within certain limits which he may exercise with respect to the selling of property upon execution or order, judgment or decree of the court, and hence as to such matters as are within that discretion he is not subject to control by mandamus. Hence where he has a discretion which he may exercise in the selection of a paper to publish notice of sale, he cannot be compelled to select for such purpose a paper designated by the judgment creditor, upon the ground that it has a wider circulation than the one in which he proposes to publish, and also the cost of publication is less. It is said that in performing such duties he acts under the orders of the court, and is answerable to it for abuse of his powers, and that relief should be sought in an application to the court.2 This may be true in Nebraska, but I am of the opinion that the discretion of the officer could not be controlled unless the facts were of such character as to clearly show an abuse of discretion. Nor will mandamus lie to compel a sheriff, on the relation of one accused of crime, to compel him to bring such person before a commissioner of the latter’s own choosing, to be admitted to bail, and more especially where it appears that the sheriff has good reason to believe such commissioner disqualified, and there are other officers qualified to assume jurisdiction of the proceedings.3

Sec. 676. Duty in respect to writ of restitution.— The verdict in proceedings for forcible entry and detainer against defendants or any of them, where there is more than one, is conclusive that the plaintiff at the time of the entry was in the peaceful and actual possession of the premises, and which is incompatible with the lawful possession of another, claiming under one of said defendants. Hence the sheriff cannot excuse his failure to enforce the writ of restitution on the ground that

1 State v. Wilson (Neb.), 48 N. W. Rep. 147.
2 Elder v. Garner (Mich.), 55 N. W. Rep. 460.
3 State v. Holliday, 85 Neb. 327.

<br>

### --- Page 0356 ---

§ 677.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 899

persons are in possession claiming under one of such parties, whom the judgment did not determine was guilty of the trespass, but will be compelled by mandamus to execute the writ. Neither a remedy by criminal prosecution nor by action on the case for neglect of duty will supersede that by mandamus, since it cannot compel a specific act to be done, and therefore is not adequate.

Src. 677. Registers of conveyances and documents.—It is said that registers of deeds and those performing similar duties, by whatsoever name designated, are ministerial officers and therefore are subject to the writ. The fact that they are classed as ministerial officers does not of itself have the effect to subject them to the writ, any more than the mere fact that a judge is a judicial officer exempts him from the writ. We look at the character of the act to be done rather than that of the mere designation of the office or officer, and determine from that the application of the remedy by mandamus. As has been repeated many times, if the act is ministerial merely, then the officer, whether he be classed as a judicial officer or otherwise, will be subject to the writ. If the act be judicial, then he is exempt from its control. In most matters the duties of such registers are ministerial, involving no discretion. They are plainly prescribed by law. Thus, it being their clear duty to record an instrument left them for that purpose, if they refuse they may be compelled by mandamus to perform the act. So the writ will issue to compel such an officer to enter upon the record a satisfaction of mortgage. If the instrument which he is required to record is not executed in the manner required by law so as to entitle it to record, then it would seem that he might properly refuse to record it, and to a certain extent he is called upon to act judicially in determining that question; but only where the defect is apparent upon mere inspection can he refuse. Such an officer can only be required to record instruments which he receives or which are tendered to him in his official capacity for that purpose. If a deed is

Freemont v. Crippen, 10 Cal. 212. Silver v. People, 45 Ill. 225; People v.

Ex parte Goodell, 14 Johns. 325; Miner, 35 Barb. 466.

People v. Miner, 37 Barb. 466.

<br>

### --- Page 0357 ---

900 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 678, 679.

delivered to him in escrow, the grantee cannot require him to place it upon record, and especially where the grantor objects. The rights of the parties in respect thereto must first be determined in an equitable or other proceeding before the instrument is entitled to be recorded. The records of such an officer are public, and therefore the public at all reasonable times are entitled to examine them and make abstracts from them, and if the officer refuse to permit such inspection a proper case is presented for granting of the writ to compel him. The rule extends to town clerks, city clerks, and those who have similar duties to perform with respect to filing or recording papers. A town clerk may be compelled to record the proceedings of a town meeting and to amend and correct his records to correspond with the facts.

§ 678. County clerks.— The duties imposed by law upon county clerks are not the same in the several states. In some of the Eastern States he is the clerk of the court and also register of deeds. In the Western States he is clothed with important duties relating to the redemption of lands delinquent for the non-payment of taxes, and the execution of deeds of lands thus sold. It is made his duty generally to sign and issue county orders for claims allowed by the county board. This is a purely ministerial act. The execution of a tax deed to the holder of a tax certificate who has complied with the law is also a ministerial act; and if he has executed a deed that is defective upon a valid certificate of sale, he may be compelled by mandamus to execute one that is without defect. Where the law provides for the making of a report of the fees received by him by virtue of his office, this act may be compelled.

§ 679. Public officers generally — Rights and duties of — Entitled to receive that which pertains to the office.— There are certain duties which are incident to public offices that devolve upon all who occupy them. The incumbent of an office

1 People v. Curtis, 41 Mich. 723.  
2 Silver v. People, 45 Ill. 225; State v. Meadows, 1 Kan. 90; State v. Hobart, 12 Nev. 408.  
3 Hill v. Goodwin, 56 N. H. 44.  
4 State v. Richter, 37 Wis. 275.  
5 State v. Winn, 19 Wis. 804; State v. Hundhausen, 23 Wis. 508.  
6 State v. Winn, 19 Wis. 304.  
7 State v. Whittemore, 12 Neb. 252.

<br>

### --- Page 0358 ---

§ 680. MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 904

is entitled to have the care and control of that which pertains to the office and is essential to the due performance of his duties, which the law expressly or impliedly confides to his custody and care. There are cases, as we shall see, where the person, though technically not an actual incumbent, that is, not actually in possession of the office, but who is clearly entitled thereto, is entitled to demand and secure that which belongs to it. It is true mandamus is not the proper proceeding by which to try the right or title to an office, quo warranto being the appropriate proceeding, yet it is not every dispute over the right that excludes the remedy by mandamus to compel delivery to one clearly entitled to the possession of that which inures to the office. This subject has been fully discussed in other chapters. Mandamus is the appropriate remedy to compel the delivery of books, papers, records and other materials incident to an office to the custody of the person clearly entitled thereto, and especially applicable where the term of an officer has expired. The writ in such case will issue to compel him to make delivery of all books, records, etc., pertaining to the office to his successor.

Sec. 680. Retiring officer— Must deliver records, etc., to successor.— He cannot be heard to say that the one claiming to be his successor was not duly elected, or, if a candidate himself for re-election, that he was duly elected, where such a successor shows that he was declared elected by the proper board of canvassers. He cannot avoid the effect of the decision of the board of canvassers by simply holding on to the office and claiming that their decision was erroneous, or the election was illegal, or that fraud was practiced. If he was a candidate for re-election he must himself commence proceedings in quo warranto against the party claiming the office, and must surrender the official papers, records and moneys, if any, to the person duly declared elected, upon his having duly qualified, and if not.

1 See chapter on Quo Warranto; also chapter on Election and Title to Office. Trustees, 78 Ind. 269; People v. Kilduff, 15 Ill. 492; Burr v. Norton, 25 Conn. 103; Peters v. Auditor, 33 Gratt. (Va.) 368.

2 Walter v. Belding, 21 Vt. 658; Stone v. Small, 54 Vt. 498; Atherton v. Sherwood, 15 Minn. 221; Frisbee v.

3 People v. Head, 25 Ill. 325.

<br>

### --- Page 0359 ---

902 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 681, 682.

a candidate he must do the same. There may be cases where it has been said that the writ will not issue upon the application of one whose title to the office is uncertain and doubtful, but I am unable to conceive of a case where such is the fact. The certificate of election is conclusive in mandamus. There can be no doubt the case might present such a state of facts as to create grave doubts in proceedings by quo warranto, and if such were the case the party not holding the certificate is compelled to resort to such proceedings. The right or title cannot be determined in proceedings by mandamus; nor do I think that a court would be justified in refusing the writ upon the ground that the granting of it is in some respects discretionary. It has been held, however, that the writ would not issue to compel a county clerk to deliver assessment books to one claiming to be duly elected an assessor when he had already delivered the books to another who was de facto an assessor.

Sec. 681. Application of the rule—Incidents.—The rule has been applied to restore to a sheriff possession of the county jail, and to a retiring mayor of a city to compel him to deliver to his successor the common seal of the city; also to a county clerk compelling him to transfer certain records to a new county formed from territory of another, where the law has specially imposed upon him that duty, and to an auditor of public accounts compelling him to deliver to the commissioner copies of the land and property books for his district.

Sec. 682. Writ only lies to compel performance of acts incident to some office or position of trust, not to private persons.—It should be kept in mind, however, that the writ does not lie to compel private persons, or persons acting in a private and individual capacity, to perform particular acts or duties. The remedy against such persons for misconduct is

1 Supervisors of La Pointe v. O’Malley, 46 Wis. 35; People v. Miller, 16 Mich. 56; People v. Callaghan, 83 Ill. 128; Crowell v. Lambert, 10 Minn. 369; People v. Stevens, 5 Hill, 616.
2 State v. Dusman, 39 N. J. L, 677.
3 People v. Lieb, 85 Ill. 484.
4 Felts v. Norton, 25 Conn. 103.
5 People v. Kilduff, 15 Ill. 492.
6 Hooten v. McKinney, 5 Neb. 194.
7 Peters v. Auditors, 33 Gratt. (Va.) 368.

<br>

### --- Page 0360 ---

§ 683.] MANDAMUS TO PUBLIC OFFICERS. 903

ample in proceedings at law or in equity. The writ only issues to compel the performance of an act which is incident to some office or position of trust where the law imposes a clear duty in respect thereto. Thus, where it appears that the books, papers and official records belonging or pertaining to a public office are held by a private person not claiming to hold them by virtue of any right growing out of or incident to the trust, the writ will not issue to compel their surrender, but the party claiming them will be remitted to proceedings at law or in equity; and this doctrine has been applied where a person was employed to make a survey of the public roads of a county and plat them in a suitable book, to the extent that the writ would not lie to compel him to deliver possession of the book. He was in no sense an officer, but a mere agent. The remedy at law was considered ample.

Sec. 683. Records open to inspection.—As a rule the records of a public office are so far public that they are open to inspection at all reasonable times and under reasonable restrictions, and this right of inspection includes the right to make copies or extracts therefrom.

1 Hussey v. Hamilton, 5 Kan. 462; Bart, 12 Nev. 408; Brewer v. Watson, 2 Silver v. People, 45 Ill. 225; State v. Meadows, 1 Kan. 90; State v. Hobart, 61 Ala. 310.

Sec. 684. County seat — Question of removal tested by mandamus.— The decisions of the courts are quite generally to the effect that mandamus is the appropriate remedy to test the question whether the county seat of a county has been legally removed, and that the proper method is to apply for a writ to compel a county officer to hold his office at the county seat. The principle involved is not unlike that which prevails with respect to compelling the delivery of official records by an incumbent to his successor, who appears by the return of the canvassers to have been duly elected, and who has qualified. Yet the inquiry is far more extended. In the former the return of the canvassing board is conclusive, the result being that the incumbent must resort to proceedings in the nature of quo warranto to determine the right, while in the latter the right itself is determined in the mandamus proceedings, there being no other adequate remedy. Hence the whole question involving the validity of the election is open to inquiry. In many of

1 State v. Saxton, 11 Wis. 27; s.c., ers v. State, 61 Ind. 379; Light v.

14 Wis. 123; Calaveras County v. State, 14 Kan. 489.

Brockway, 80 Cal. 826; Commission-

2 State v. Barton, 51 Pac. Rep. 218,

<br>

### --- Page 0363 ---

906 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 685, 686.]

the states the proceeding is regulated by statute, whereby the issues involved are submitted to a jury. In some of the states it has been held that where a statutory method is provided it excludes proceedings by mandamus, and in one at least the writ has been refused where it appeared there were fatal defects in the preliminary proceedings. The supreme court of Nebraska, however, holds that in such a proceeding the court will not go behind the face of the returns and determine the ultimate right.

SEC. 685. Right and title to office—How far determined by mandamus.—It is beyond dispute that the mere question of the title to a public office, in cases where the element of expulsion or removal is not involved, as between two or more claimants, must be determined in quo warranto and not by mandamus. Yet it does not follow in all cases that the writ will not lie, where there is a clear right to the office. We shall see, first, that it is the appropriate remedy to restore one who has been wrongfully removed. Also it is the appropriate remedy to compel one holding over to surrender to one duly declared elected to the office the books and official records.

SEC. 686. Admission to office.—It is the appropriate remedy to compel a municipal board to admit a person duly elected as a member of such board, where there is no adverse incumbent. It was held, however, where the charter of a city provided that the common council should convene and examine the certificates of election filed with the clerk, and forthwith determine, declare and certify who were duly elected at such election, and a further provision that the common council should be the judge of the election and qualification of its own members, that the duties of the new council were judicial and not ministerial, and that mandamus would not lie to compel them.

1 State v. Stockwell, 7 Kan. 98. Lane, 55 N. Y. 217; People v. Gardner, 68 N. Y. 467; Morris v. People, 3 Denio, 396; People v. Stevens, 5 Hill, 616.

2 People v. Hamilton County, 3 Neb. 244.

3 State v. Roper (Neb.); 65 N. W. Rep. 80.

4 People v. Common Council, 18 Mich. 838; Meredith v. Board of Supervisors, 50 Cal. 438; People v. New York, 3 Johns. Cas. 79; People v.

5 State v. Davis, 50 Mo. App. 447.

6 State v. Rahway, 33 N. J. L. 111; Angell & Ames on Corp., sec. 703; Putnam v. Langley, 183 Mass. 204.

<br>

### --- Page 0364 ---

§ 686.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 

to seat a person returned as alderman-elect by the inspectors of election. In so far as the duties of such council pertain to examining the certificates, I cannot conclude that they partake of a judicial character, more than the act of state canvassers in examining returns from county canvassers. Federal and state legislatures are invested with the authority to determine the election and qualification of their members by the constitution itself, and such exclusive authority is in some instances vested in municipal bodies. If authority can thus be placed, it certainly must be reasonably exercised, and not arbitrarily; but I apprehend that, if one was duly elected to a municipal office, or had a certificate in due form from the inspectors, such municipal body would be compelled by mandamus to seat him, either where there was no other incumbent or where there had been no proceedings taken nor proposed by such body to determine who in fact was actually elected to such office. This, if for no other reason, on the ground that there exists no other remedy, and of clear abuse of discretion. It is held, however, by the Michigan court that the common council of a city may exercise such power, but that the power is exhausted when a member has once been seated; that they have no authority where disqualification occurs (such as removal from the ward) afterwards. Other courts have upheld the exercise of such power, and to the extent that, where a municipal body has refused to admit one having the prima facie right as a member thereof, their action is not subject to review by mandamus; that the writ will only lie to the extent of compelling the particular body to determine the question of the election and qualification of the person presenting such prima facie right. In fact the writ lies in all cases where quo warranto does not lie, as well as where such proceeding does not afford an adequate remedy, unless the power to determine the right is vested elsewhere than in the courts.

1 Haloran v. Carter, 13 N. Y. S. 214.  
2 Doran v. De Long et al., 48 Mich. 552.  
3 Supervisors v. Minturn, 4 W. Va. 300.  
4 State v. Common Council, 4 W. Va. 800; Peabody v. School Committee, 115 Mass. 388; Mayor v. Rainwater, 47 Miss. 547.  
5 Regina v. Leeds, 11 A. & E. 512; Rex v. Sawyer, 10 B. & C. 486; Willcock, 378, pl. 87; Harwood v. Marshall, 9 Md. 83.  

<br>

### --- Page 0365 ---

908 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 687-689.]

Sec. 687. Incidental questions affecting the right.—Mandamus, however, is an appropriate remedy to determine questions relating to the right to a public office which may be preliminary to the contest to determine the actual right and title in proceedings by quo warranto.

Sec. 688. Election—Compelling.—Mandamus will lie to compel the proper officers to give notice of an election, even where the charter day has passed. If such officers neglect their duty at the proper time from any cause, the obligation still rests upon them to call an election at the earliest opportunity. So it will lie in the name of the state on the relation of a voter to compel the holding of a special election to fill a vacancy where such duty is enjoined by law, and the writ may be applied for where there is unreasonable delay.¹

Sec. 689. Nominations for office.—The duty of ballot commissioners to place upon the ballot the name of a candidate legally certified to the board is ministerial. Where the secretary of state refuses to certify the nomination of a de facto political party as entitled to a place on the official ballot, mandamus will lie to compel such certification, if, owing to the shortness of time before the election, the remedy by petition, according to the provisions of a legislative act of the particular state, appears inadequate.² But where the law provides that the decision of election supervisors shall be final, then the courts are without jurisdiction; and where under a law which provides that, in case district supervisors cannot agree or arrive at a decision, the matter shall be submitted to the state supervisor of elections, who shall summarily decide the question thus submitted to him, and his decision shall be final, his decision is beyond review by the courts, but the district supervisors will be compelled by mandamus to comply with it. While the courts have no jurisdiction of the questions thus submitted, yet after they have been decided and duties become

¹ State v. Young, 65 S. Dak. 406; People v. Fairbury, 22 Minn. 218; People v. Carr, 86 N. Y. 512.
² Marcum v. Ballot Commissioners, 42 W. Va. 263; State v. Rahway, 83 N. J. L. 111; People v. Common Council, 77 N. Y. 503; Lamb v. Lynd, 44 Pa. St. 336; People v. Town, 51 Minn. 149; People v. McGaffey, 23 Colo. 156.

<br>

### --- Page 0366 ---

§ 689.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 909

thus fixed, the duty of the board is to obey the order, and mandamus lies to compel obedience to duty.

The law has been declared to be in Wisconsin, Ohio, and possibly another state, that persons who had signed their names to a petition (for instance, as in Wisconsin, for submission of the question of removal of a county seat) might withdraw their names at the last moment by a request to the supervisors so to do, and thus defeat the submission. It is evident that it is thus left within the power of an adverse board, or of individuals acting in concert with them, to perpetrate a fraud upon those who desire the question to be submitted. In the argument of that case the writer called the attention of the Wisconsin court to the fact that such a rule, if sustained, would operate as a fraud, which it did in that case, and made the query whether a nominee for office, having filed his petition, duly signed with the requisite number of electors, could be deprived of his right as such by the action of such electors in requesting that their names be withdrawn, at a time when it was too late to get other signers in their place. It followed that if the logic was good in one case it was in the other. The writer's attention is now called to a decision of the supreme court of Maryland with respect to that identical question, and be it said to its credit that it found ample reason in the law for preventing the consummation of such frauds. They say: “The contention is at war with the whole policy of the law, which intrusts to those ministerial officers no discretion, but imposes upon them an imperative duty to place on the ballot the names of the persons nominated in any one of the three modes designated in the act of 1896. There is no provision in the statute permitting a voter, who has signed a nominating certificate, to erase or withdraw his name therefrom after that certificate has been filed with the supervisors. If the legislature had intended that such an authority should be exercised by the voter, it would undoubtedly have been stated in terms sufficiently clear to admit of no debate. The withdrawal paper was consequently a mere nullity. It should not have been received or considered. It in no way affected the right of the candidates nominated in

1 Chapman v. Miller et al., 52 Ohio St. 164

<br>

### --- Page 0367 ---

910 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 690, 691.

and by the certificate to have their names go upon the ballots.” The duties of supervisors or those who stand in their place, with reference to making up official ballots, was stated by the Maryland court. After reciting several of the provisions of the statute, the court said: “It is manifest it was not the purpose to invest the supervisors with any discretion as to what names should be placed upon the official ballot. It will not for a moment be contended that the supervisors can reject any of the nominations certified to them by the secretary of state, because in their opinion there were no vacancies in the offices for which such nominations were made; nor is there any authority to be found anywhere in the statute investing them with power to act differently in respect to nominations certified from a convention or a primary meeting. Where the nominations come to the supervisors from the proper sources, certified in the proper manner as the law provides, it is their plain duty to place the names certified to on the ballots, and all ballots shall contain every such name.”

Sec. 690. Election of officers — Appointment of.— Where the law provides that members of a board of supervisors of election who belong to one political party shall select as an election clerk one of the three persons whose names are submitted by the members of the other leading party, entitled to be represented by such clerk, this duty may be enforced by mandamus.

Sec. 691. Boards of canvassers.— The duties of boards of canvassers of election returns are mainly ministerial in character, though some of the duties imposed upon them may be termed quasi-judicial. It has been stated that their duties respecting the determination whether the papers received purporting to be election returns are such in fact, and duly and properly authenticated as required by law, partake of a quasi-judicial character. Yet I apprehend these are pure questions of law, and the action of the board in receiving or refusing to

1 Sterling et al. v. Bones et al, 39 Atl. Rep. 424. People v. Hilliard, 29 Ill. 422; State  
2 Wells v. Monroe, 38 Atl. Rep. 987, v. County Judge, 7 Iowa, 186.  
3 Sudler v. Lankford, 82 Md. 142, 

<br>

### --- Page 0368 ---

§ 692.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 911

receive such returns is subject to control by mandamus in cases where their authenticity does not depend upon extraneous evidence, and the court will determine as a matter of law whether the return was such as ought to have been received! In many of the states the law requires the canvassing board, in cases where no return has been received from a precinct, or if one has been received not properly authenticated, to send a messenger and procure a return, or an amended return, as the case may be. Such a law to a great extent supersedes the effect of certain decisions wherein it was held that, if the return was not authenticated in such a manner as to constitute a return, the canvassing board could reject it, as they were not bound to know or ascertain that it was a return emanating from the proper source. It was held, where the statute did not otherwise provide, that a paper purporting to be a return, merely tested by the signature of the person who was town clerk, but which did not bear his official title as such, was properly rejected by the board, it being stated by the court that “the board of examiners had no official knowledge and were not required to ascertain or know that the person signing his name as ‘Jas. N. Tilton’ was in fact such town clerk. The board of examiners were not required by law to receive, examine or treat any paper which did not appear upon its face to be such a return.”

Szc. 692. Duty of ascertaining number of votes is ministerial.—Among the duties that are ministerial are those which relate to the determination of the number of votes cast for each particular person or proposition, and to declare and certify which person or which proposition has received the highest number of votes cast. And mandamus lies in all cases to compel them not only to proceed to the performance of this duty, but also to prosecute it to the end; and wherever it is made to appear that they have not completed their duties in conformity to law, as in excluding certain returns or in other ways, by

lLuce v. Mayhew et al., 18 Gray, 2 Luce v. Mayhew et al. 13 Gray, 83; State v. County Judge, 7 Iowa, 83. See also State v. County Judge, 186; People v. Cook, + Selden, 67, 89; 7 Iowa, 186. Bacon v. Commissioners, 26 Me. 491.

<br>

### --- Page 0369 ---

912 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 692.

which the number of votes cast is not determined, mandamus will lie to compel them to include such returns and to certify the whole vote cast. All questions relating to the validity of the election are to be determined by legal tribunals, no such discretion being vested in the board of canvassers. This duty is not evaded by an adjournment of the meeting. If not fully performed, the writ will lie to compel the board to reconvene and proceed with the performance of such duty. The New York court declares that when the statement or return of election district inspectors states a less number of votes for certain candidates than that shown by the unquestioned tally sheet, the board of county canvassers may be required by mandamus, on the petition of the candidates prejudiced by the return, to exercise the power conferred by the statutes of that state to summon the inspectors to correct their return, and also, independently of the election law, the inspectors may be required by mandamus to convene and make a correct return, and the county board of canvassers directed to canvass the corrected return. It is true that in that state, by statute, the tally sheets must be preserved and may be referred to in making the canvass; yet the decision of the court was not based upon such fact, but, as they say, the writ lies independently of the statute. The supreme court of Michigan reach a result directly contrary to that announced by the New York court of appeals. They held that when inspectors of election have completed their count and executed and delivered their returns to the proper officer, their legal powers end, and any attempt on their part to change or modify such returns in any particular, involving other than a mere clerical duty which is patent on the face of the papers, is clearly beyond their powers. There the board of inspectors, discovering their mistake, made a second or amended return to the board of canvassers, which the latter accepted and included in their canvass. Mandamus was applied for and issued to compel such canvassing board to reconvene and make their

1 Coll v. Board of Canvassers, 83 Mich. 386; State v. County Judge, 7 Iowa, 186; People v. Cook, 4 Selden, 67, 89; Bacon v. Commissioners, 26 Me. 491; People v. Kilduff, 15 Ill. 492; Patton v. People, 63 Ill. App. 617. 
2 Lewis v. Commissioners, 16 Kan. 102; State v. Berg, 76 Mo. 136; State v. McFadden, 46 Neb. 668. 
3 Matter of Stewart, 155 N. Y. 545.

<br>

### --- Page 0370 ---

§ 693. RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS, 913

canvass upon the returns as originally made! The doctrine of this court, as very emphatically expressed in a later case, is that the duty of the board of canvassers is purely clerical, to foot up the returns and declare the result and issue certificates accordingly, and the performance of this duty will be compelled by mandamus, even after the board has declared that a candidate was elected and thereafter adjourned, when it appears that the duty was not performed in the manner required by law.2

Sec. 693. Writ will not lie where election not authorized or not held at proper time. — The writ, however, may be refused where it appears that the election was clearly illegal, as, for instance, not being authorized by law in the particular municipality, or not having been held at the proper time.3 But such exception to the rule does not extend to proceedings involved in the manner of conducting the election, or to questions involved in the election, if the election be held at the time and place prescribed by law.4 For example, the board of canvassers have no right to reject a return because it did not show that the election officers were sworn.5 It is to be remembered that proceedings by mandamus in such case are not to place the relator into the office claimed, but they are simply to compel the board of canvassers to perform a plain duty required by law, leaving, as we have said, the question of the ultimate right and title to be determined by a competent tribunal in other proceedings specially provided to determine that question.6 Thus the writ will lie to compel the board of state canvassers to determine in accordance with law which one of the candidates at an election in the state for the office of representative in congress is entitled to a certificate of election. The power of determining the right to such an office is vested by the constitution of the United States exclusively in the house of representatives itself, and the court has no power to go behind the returns and determine which candidate was

1 Roemer v. Board of Canvassers, 90 Mich. 27.
2 Coll v. Board of Canvassers, 83 Mich. 367.
3 State v. County Judge, 7 Iowa, 186.
4 Luce v. Mayhew, 18 Gray, 83; State v. County Judge, 7 Iowa, 186.
5 State v. Robinson, 1 Kan. 17.
6 Bradfield v. Wart et al., 36 Iowa, 291; Prescott v. Gonsor, 84 Iowa, 175.

<br>

### --- Page 0371 ---

914 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 694, 695.]

elected. The mere fact that the office is a federal one does not preclude the power of the state court.

Sec. 694. State board can only act upon returns received. What has been stated with respect to determining and certifying the whole number of votes cast has special reference to local boards of canvassers and not to state boards or officers. The duties of the latter are specially prescribed by law, and they can only act upon such returns as are properly made to them by the respective county boards. They have no power, unless directed by statute, to send for additional returns in case those returned are defective, nor can they include in their determination returns from towns and precincts which have been unlawfully rejected by a county board, though it appear upon the face of the return made by such a board of canvassers that it does not include all the votes cast in the county from which it was made, but that a portion of the voters have been illegally disfranchised by such county canvassers. The court can, however, upon mandamus, determine whether the return made to the state board of the votes cast in any county should be included by the state board in their canvass and statement of the votes cast for said office.

Sec. 695. Defective ballots—Initials of Christian names. The question has been before the courts of the duty of canvassing boards with respect to counting or excluding votes which contain the initial letter of the Christian name of the candidate only, and the power of the courts to revise or supersede the action of such board with respect to such matter. It is conceded that in quo warranto proceedings the question is open to be determined by extrinsic evidence that the votes so cast were intended to be cast for the candidate whose full name would correspond with the initial; but it is denied in some cases that the canvassers have the power to determine for themselves what the intent of the voter was, or to determine whether the person voted for by the initial Christian name was the same person with a full name commencing with the same initial letter.

State v. Board of Canvassers, 36 Wis. 498; State v. Board of Canvassers, 17 Fla. 9.

State v. State Canvassers, 36 Wis.

<br>

### --- Page 0372 ---

§ 696.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 915

That the most they can do is to return the vote as actually cast, though it treat the ballots, where some bear the full Christian name and others the initial letter only, as cast for different persons; and if the opposing candidate upon the face of the returns has more votes to his credit than the person expressed by the full name or the person expressed by the initial name only, though the two latter combined would make a plurality, they must give the certificate to the former. That the board having thus done their full duty, and as the purpose of mandamus is confined to compelling the performance of duty, the writ will not lie to compel the canvassers to count the ballots containing the initial letter of the Christian name with those containing the full name of the candidate and award to him the certificate of election!

§ 696. Subject discussed — Courts not agreed.—It may be stated that the supreme court of Ohio are not in harmony with the courts of Maine and Massachusetts with respect to the foregoing proposition. They concur to the extent that the duties of the canvassing officers are purely ministerial in their nature. But they say, “the performance of ministerial duties requires the exercise of intelligence, sense and judgment. A sheriff is bound to ascertain the identity of the person described in process, and to serve the right person. Nor would the fact that there were several persons of the same name relieve him from the performance of the duty; nor the fact that the Christian name of the party named in the process was expressed by initials only.” It appeared that some ballots expressed the name of a candidate for congress “Henry L. Morey,” while others expressed the name of “H. L. Morey.” The court say further: “If the person receiving the certificate did not receive the plurality of votes as shown by the returns, fairly and reasonably understood, the defendants have not performed the duty enjoined upon them. If H. L. Morey and Henry L. Morey designate the same person, as appears from the returns read, in the light of such facts of public notoriety connected with

¹ Clark v. Board of Examiners, 126 N. Y. 45; People v. Smith, 45 N. Y. Mass. 282; Opinion of Justices, 64 Me. 772; People v. Cicotte, 16 Mich. 283. See also People v. Pease, 27 Me. 596.

<br>

### --- Page 0373 ---

916 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 697.

The election as everyone takes notice of, the defendants have performed their duty correctly in giving the certificate to Henry L. Morey.” The court approves of the rule stated by Judge Cooley in his work on Constitutional Limitations, p. 623, as follows: “The action of such board is to be carefully confined to an examination of the papers before them, and a determination of the result therefrom in the light of such facts of public notoriety connected with the election as everyone takes notice of, and which may enable them to apply such ballots as are in any respect imperfect to the proper candidates or officers for which they are intended, provided the intent is sufficiently indicated by the ballot in connection with such facts, so that extraneous evidence is not necessary for this purpose.” The court also mentions a distinction existing between the case cited from Massachusetts and the one under consideration, to wit, that in the former the writ was applied for to compel the board to count votes which they had rejected, while in the latter it was applied for to compel the board to exclude votes which it had counted; but it is quite evident that this distinction was of but little weight in reaching their conclusions. Mention was also made that it was not disputed on the argument that Henry L. Morey and H. L. Morey were in fact the same person.

Src. 697. Board may be compelled to perform its duty though it has awarded a certificate to another.—It is the doctrine of some courts that though the canvassing board, upon canvassing a portion of the returns only, issues a certificate of election to the person appearing thus to be elected, and he has qualified and entered upon the discharge of his duties, such an action on their part will not preclude the court by its writ of mandamus from compelling such board to re-assemble and canvass all the returns correctly, and issue a certificate of election to another, if by such canvass it appears he received a plurality of the votes cast. The supreme court of Wisconsin hold otherwise, in effect that, where a person declared by the inspectors of election to have been duly elected to an office has qualified and

1State v. Foster, 83 Ohio St. 599. 2 State v. Howe, 28 Neb. 618; Smith v. Lawrence, 49 N. W. Rep. (S. Dak.) 7.

<br>

### --- Page 0374 ---

§ 698. RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE—ELECTIONS. 

entered upon its duties, mandamus will not lie to compel the inspectors to declare another person elected; that this would, in effect, be determining the title to the office, which should be tried on quo warranto.

Sxo. 698. Rule where duty has been once performed.—It has been stated that some courts hold to the extent that the writ will not lie to compel canvassers to amend their returns after they have once performed that duty and the matter has passed beyond their jurisdiction, even though they have erred in rejecting votes which should have been received. I apprehend, however, that the doctrine herein stated, in effect that where the canvassers have made an incomplete canvass by unlawfully rejecting certain returns, and have adjourned, they may be compelled to reassemble and complete their duty, where no one, as the result of their action, is in the possession of the office, is the doctrine best supported by reason and authority. The fact that the officers have given the certificate to another will not prevent their being compelled to certify the election of one who, upon the face of the returns, is entitled thereto. It was held, however, that a peremptory writ would not issue to compel a county clerk to issue to relator a certificate of his election to the office of prosecuting attorney, and to certify the vote to the secretary of state, when it appeared from the return to the alternative writ that he had duly canvassed the vote and issued a certificate of election to another to whom the governor had issued a commission, and where the relator had instituted a proceeding which was then pending to contest the right to such office. Where the law provides that the judges of election shall certify to the governor the number of votes received by each candidate for each office, who thereupon is to issue a commission to the person elected, and the governor, acting upon such return, issues a commission to a person thus appearing to be elected, mandamus. 

1 State v. Sullivan, 88 Wis. 416. See 3 Ellis v. Commissioners, 2 Gray, also People v. Supervisors, 12 Barb. 370; Clark v. McKenzie, 7 Bush, 523; State v. Rodman, 43 Mo. 256. State v. Lawrence, 3 Kan. 95; People v. Rivers, 27 Ill. 242. 
2 High’s Ex. Rem., sec. 57, citing People v. Supervisors, 12 Barb. 217; State v. Rodman, 43 Mo. 256. 
4 State v. Smith (Mo.), 15 S. W. Rep. 614.

<br>

### --- Page 0375 ---

9is SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 699-701.

will not lie to compel him to issue a commission to another who claims to have been duly elected. He must in such case pursue his remedy by other appropriate proceedings."

§ 699. Duties that are judicial.— There are some duties involved in canvassing votes that are judicial in their nature, which call for the exercise of judgment. It often occurs that certain erasures or interlineations are made or intended which call for the judgment of the inspectors as to the actual intent of the voter. Where such is the case the judgment thus exercised is conclusive, and cannot be reviewed or revised in proceedings by mandamus. In some states the law expressly confers upon state canvassers judicial power with respect to their duties in determining all contested election cases. The decision of such officers as to who has received the greatest number of votes, and their act in issuing a certificate of election, is conclusive, and cannot be reviewed by mandamus. The right of appeal in some states is given by statute from the board of county canvassers to the state board. In such cases the state board acts judicially. An equal division of the numbers of such board has the effect to affirm the action of the county canvassers.

§ 700. Writ will not lie until default.— The writ, however, will not lie until there has been an actual default on the part of the canvassers in the performance of their duties, or unless it is clearly manifest that they do not intend to proceed or correct the erroneous or unlawful proceedings which have already taken place.

§ 701. Questions of eligibility not determined.— Questions involving the eligibility of a person to occupy an office are not to be determined by mandamus or by canvassers. Such questions are now more properly determined by proceedings in quo warranto.

1 Brown v. Bragonier (Md.), 29 Atl. Rep. 7.
2 Ex parte Elliott, 83 S.C. 602, 12 S.E. Rep. 423.
3 State v. Deane, 23 Fla. 121, 6 So. Rep. 698.
4 Ex parte Ivey (Fla.), 8 So. Rep. 427.
5 Maverick Oil Co. v. Hanson (N.H.), 29 Atl. Rep. 461.
6 Ex parte Scarborough, 348 C.13.
7 Stevens v. Carter (Oreg.), 40 Pac. Rep. 1074.
