### --- Page 0376 ---

§§ 702, 703.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 919

Sec. 702. Possession of office.— While, as we have seen, mandamus does not lie to determine the title to an office, yet it is an appropriate proceeding to obtain the possession of an office, or, what is the same, the books and records pertaining thereto, where the relator has received the certificate of election, or been duly declared elected by the board of canvassers; and the result is the same whether the certificate was voluntarily given or compelled by mandamus. When the result of the canvass of votes cast at a legal election, as declared by the board of canvassers, shows the election of a person to an office, and he qualifies within the time and in the manner prescribed by law, he is entitled to the office as against every other person laying claim thereto, until the result so declared is set aside by the judgment of some competent court in a direct proceeding for that purpose; and every other person who assumes to exercise the duties of that office (as by holding over after the expiration of the former term, though a candidate for re-election, and claiming to have been lawfully elected) must be treated as a mere intruder or usurper, in any proceeding against him to compel him to deliver up the books and papers pertaining thereto, or to recover moneys or other property in his hands which he is required by law to deliver over to his successor in office. Such incumbent in order to retain possession must himself, by proceedings in mandamus, obtain the certificate of election, or by proceedings in quo warranto instituted by himself have been declared to have the title to such office. He cannot, by holding over, force the person having the certificate to institute such proceedings.

Sec. 708. Peremptory writ will not issue upon motion where right not clear or disputed.— It is held in New York that a peremptory writ of mandamus will not issue upon motion to determine the validity of a relator’s claim to an office to which he alleges he has been elected, where there is a serious question in regard thereto and another person is holding and exercising the functions of the office;* and also the general

1 Supervisors of La Pointe v. O’Malley, 46 Wis. 35. See also Huffman v. Mills, 39 Kan. 577, 18 Pac. Rep. 516.

See also People v. Lane, 55 N. Y. 217; People v. Goetting, 133 N. Y. 569; People v. Lewis, 146 N. Y. 60.

2 Matter of Gardner, 68 N. Y. 467.

<br>

### --- Page 0377 ---

920 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 704.

doctrine is stated that upon a motion for a peremptory writ, where opposing affidavits are heard which are in conflict with the averments in the moving affidavits, the question as to the right to a writ must be determined upon the assumption that the averments in the opposing affidavits are true. It should be remembered that the court refers to a motion or application made for a peremptory writ, and not where the proceedings are instituted by means of an alterative writ. There can be no issue tried in the former proceeding, while in the latter issues may be formed upon the writ and return.

Src. 704. Certificate not conclusive of the right.—The misleading feature that attaches to the opinion in People v. Lewis, 146 N. Y. 60, is the quotation made from Mechem in his work upon Public Officers and Offices, sec. 478, apparently with approval, as follows: “The proceeding by quo warranto is the proper and appropriate remedy for determining the title to a public office and of ascertaining who is entitled to hold it, of obtaining possession of an office to which one has been legally elected and has become duly qualified to hold, and also removing an incumbent who has usurped it, or who claims it by an invalid election, or who illegally claims to hold it after the expiration of his term.” It has been demonstrated by a line of authorities heretofore cited that the propositions italicised are incorrect. It is true that quo warranto is the appropriate proceeding to try the title to an office and mandamus does not lie. Compelling, however, the issue of a certificate of election is a different proposition. Such proceeding in no manner determines the title, nor is the certificate of election conclusive as to the right to the office, but is merely evidence prima facie of such right. It does not have the effect of ousting one in possession, nor affect his right to the office. This could not be done, as he is not before the court. It may be technically true that the writ does not lie to obtain possession of an office,

1 People v. Board of Apportionment, 64 N. Y. 627; People v. Lewis, 146 N. Y. 60. Rives, 27 Ill. 242; In re Strong, 20 Pick. 484; Brower v. O’Brien, 2 Ind. Cases cited on pp. —.

2 People v. Supervisors, 142 N. Y. 271.

3 State v. Gibbs, 13 Fla, 55; People v. Hilliard, 29 Ill. 419; Ellis v. Commissioners, 2 Gray, 370; People v. Rives, 27 Ill. 242; In re Strong, 20 Pick. 484; Brower v. O’Brien, 2 Ind. 423.

<br>

### --- Page 0378 ---

§§ 705, 706.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 921

but, as clearly appears in preceding pages, the same result may be accomplished by issuing and directing the writ to the incumbent, commanding him to deliver the books, papers and funds to the person who holds the proper certificate of election! And it is especially appropriate, as has also been stated, to compel one holding over to surrender the official records to one having such prima facie title. In fact, quo warranto can neither remove an officer from or place him in possession of an office. It merely determines the right and title, unless special provision is made by statute, and mandamus may be resorted to after the right is thus determined to accomplish these results.

Sec. 705. Where title determined by lot, statute must be strictly pursued.— Statutes sometimes provide that, where the vote is a tie between candidates for an office, the election of one or the other shall be determined by lot in the presence and under the direction of certain officers. This statute must be strictly pursued. Where there was a tie vote between two candidates, and the officers deducted a vote from one of the candidates and declared the other elected, this was held not a compliance with the statute, and that mandamus would issue to compel such officers to determine the election in the manner required by law.

Sec. 706. Duplicate appointments.— Where a person had been appointed a member of the board of superintendents of the poor by the board of supervisors, and another person had been so appointed by the board of county auditors and admitted to the office, it was held that mandamus would not lie to seat the one appointed by the supervisors. The particular ground upon which the action of the court was based was that this, in effect, would be determining the right and title to the office by mandamus, which could not be done. In fact the court would thus necessarily have to determine in which body

See also State v. Callahan (N. Dak.), 61 N. W. Rep. 1025; Reed, 15 Pa. Co. Ct. 440 (Pa. Com. Pl.); Hadfield v. Grace, 83 Wis. 295; Cameron v. Parker, 38 Pac. Rep. 14; State v. Johnson, 30 Fla. 433.

<br>

### --- Page 0379 ---

922 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 707, 708.]

vested the right of appointment in a proceeding to which they were not parties; that it would thus determine the right to an office in a collateral proceeding! That the title to an office cannot, in a collateral proceeding, be tried, is well settled.

Sec. 707. Removal and restoration of public officers.—Mandamus is the appropriate remedy to restore a public officer to the office from which he has been wrongfully removed. A distinction is drawn between those cases where a person seeks to be inducted into an office to which he has been duly elected, and those where, actually having been in the possession of the office and removed, he seeks to regain it.

Sec. 708. Rule where office has been filled.—Whether the writ will issue to compel the restoration of an officer improperly removed when another has been placed in the office under such circumstances as to constitute him a de facto officer may not be clearly settled. Mr. High, in his work, states that the writ will issue to restore an officer thus removed, though another has been appointed in his stead and is in possession; citing Drew v. Judges, 3 Hen. & M. (Va.) 1. The Wisconsin court left the question open. The general doctrine is that mandamus cannot be used to regain an office claimed by another; but in few, if any, of the cases where this doctrine was declared was the direct question here stated present; but the question involved was the right of two or more persons to an office which possessed an incumbent claiming under color of right. The arguments in support of the affirmative of the proposition, to wit: that the writ will lie, may be said to be, first, that there is no

1 Frey v. Michie et al. (Mich.), 86 N. W. Rep. 184.

2 Johns v. People, 25 Mich. 499; Curran v. Norris, 58 Mich. 512; Frey v. Michie et al. (Mich.), 36 N. W. Rep. 184; People v. Stevens, 5 Hill, 616.

3 State v. Watertown, 9 Wis. 254; Ex parte Diggs, 52 Ala. 381; Milliken v. City Council, 54 Tex. 888; State v. Atlantic City, 52 N. J. 322, 19 Atl. Rep. 780; Howard v. Gage, 6 Mass. 462; Strong v. Pettibone, 20 Pick. 484; Green v. Episcopal Society, 1 S.-& R. 254; Fuller v. Trustees, 6 Conn. 532; County Court v. Sparks, 10 Mo. 118; Welch v. Passaic Hospital (N. J.), 36 Atl. Rep. 702.

4 State v. Watertown, 9 Wis. 254.

5 Kennedy v. Board of Education, 82 Cal. 483; People v. Olds, 3 Cal. 167; Meredith v. Supervisors, 50 Cal. 433; Kelley v. Edwards, 69 Cal. 460; Fish v. Weathernoux, 2 Johns. 217; Rex v. Mayor, 2 Term R. 259; People v. Stephens, 5 Hill, 626.

<br>

### --- Page 0380 ---

§ 709.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 923

other remedy; that quo warranto could only oust the incumbent, and would not restore the rightful claimant; second, that the removal being unlawful, there could be no vacancy, and consequently no appointment; and the mere decision that the removal was unlawful of itself would not only restore the former incumbent, but operate to oust one appointed to his place, who can be nothing else than a mere intruder; and third, that if such were not the rule, then any board or person clothed with the power of removal could not only evade the provisions of law, but deprive one of substantial vested rights, by immediately, upon making an order for removal, fill the place with another. This reasoning was applied to the extent that, where the removal of a police sergeant had been determined to be unlawful so as to become res judicata, the office was not full de facto by reason of the appointment of a subsequent incumbent. The supreme court of Utah, however, held that mandamus was the proper remedy, though the office be in possession of another.

§ 709. Question further discussed.—The only argument against the proposition is that a party has a remedy by proceedings in the nature of quo warranto. Conceding that this is true, it is manifest that in many cases it is not an adequate remedy. It involves serious delay, and in cases where the term of office is short amounts practically to a denial of the right. In most cases the cause for removal is matter of record, and simply a question of law is presented, whether the cause alleged is one sanctioned by law or whether the proceedings have been lawfully conducted. The merits of the controversy as to the sufficiency of the proof cannot be investigated. Upon that question the removing power has exercised its judgment, which is beyond control by mandamus. It was held that mandamus will not lie in behalf of a public officer to obtain books, papers and funds belonging to a particular office, where it appears that he has been removed by the proper authority, and a subsequent appointment made and commission issued to another for the

1 State v. Atlantic City (N. J.), 19 Atl. Rep. 780.
2 Pratt v. Commissioners (Utah), 49 Pac. Rep. 747.
3 See Harwood v. Marshall, 9 Md. 82; Conlin v. Aldrich, 98 Mass. 557.

<br>

### --- Page 0381 ---

924 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 710.

same office; that the proceeding in such a case would be an attempt to avoid such latter commission, which would necessarily involve the actual right or title to the office; and the same rule was applied where an officer had been superseded by the governor and a successor had been duly commissioned. It was held in one case, where the appointment and term of school teachers was regulated by statute, that the writ would lie to restore a teacher unlawfully removed; that the place was not an office, and therefore the case was not within the doctrine that mandamus would not lie to regain an office claimed and in possession of another. Blackstone in enumerating the cases in which the writ may be issued states, among others, that "it lies to compel the admission or restoration of the party applying to any office or franchise of a public nature, whether spiritual or temporal." The writ has been applied, as we have seen, to restore attorneys who have been unlawfully disbarred; to restore a superintendent of schools, a road supervisor, a superintendent of a state insane asylum, as well as to restore persons wrongfully removed to other offices. The writ will not issue, however, in all cases. Thus, it will not issue to seat a person in an office, even on proof of prima facie title, if it appears that the claimant’s title is such that if seated he must be ousted from his seat upon a contest. The applicant must show a clear prima facie legal right to the office, that he is an officer de jure, and it is not sufficient for him to show that he is the de facto officer.

Sec. 710. Cause of removal — Jurisdiction.— As has been stated, the question that is most frequently presented involves the power of the board or officer who causes the removal so to act. In some cases the power under the law is absolute, and

1 Ewing v. Turner (Okl.), 85 Pac. 7; Miles v. Stevenson (Md.), 30 Atl. Rep. 951.  
2 State v. Johnson, 30 Fla. 433.  
3 Kennedy v. Board of Education, 63.  
4 Eastman v. Householder, 54 Kan. 82 Cal. 483.  
5 People v. Turner, 1 Cal. 148; People v. Judges, 1 Johns. Cas. 181.  
6 State v. Watertown, 9 Wis. 254.  
7 State v. Board of Health (N. J.), 8 Atl. Rep. 509.  
8 Justices v. Clark, 1 Mon. 82; Justices v. Harcourt, 4 B. Mon. 499.

<br>

### --- Page 0382 ---

§ 711.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 925

It is clear that in such cases the writ cannot lie! Thus, where the grant of power is to remove for “incompetency, improper conduct or other cause satisfactory to the board,” the power is absolute to the extent that the board may remove without examination of witnesses under oath, or even giving the officer previous notice of the investigation of charges against him. The words “other cause” mean other like causes; that is, one affecting the officer’s fitness for the office. Hence the board may remove for any cause affecting his competency. But such is the limit of the power. If the board should attempt to remove for any cause, it would be an excess of power, and not a removal within the statute. The only question in such a case, for judicial cognizance, is as to whether the board has kept within its jurisdiction or whether the cause assigned is a cause for removal under the statute. The grant of the power of removal is frequently expressed for “due cause,” and where such is the grant then a very different question is presented. What is “due cause” for removal of an officer is a question of law to be determined by the judicial department, and in the absence of any statutory provision as to what shall constitute such cause it must be determined with reference to the nature and character of the office and the qualifications requisite to fill it. There must exist a legal cause of removal, and the decision of the board will be controlled by mandamus as to what the law has allowed them to determine, but their decision upon the matters which the law has allowed them to determine will not be so controlled.

Sec. 711. Writ will lie at instance of one appointed to obtain possession of records.—Where a county board having authority, and upon complaint and a hearing thereon, removed a county officer from office and rendered a judgment of ouster, and appointed a successor who duly qualified, and thereupon such appointee demanded possession of the office, books and papers pertaining thereto, which demand was refused, it was held that mandamus would lie to compel the person removed to surrender the office.*

1 State v. McGarry, 21 Wis. 502.  
2 State v. McGarry, 21 Wis. 502.  
3 State v. Watertown, 9 Wis. 254.  
4 State v. Meeker, 19 Neb. 444.

<br>

### --- Page 0383 ---

926 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 712, 713].

Src. 712. Whether incumbent can question right to remove or legality of proceedings.— Whether the officer removed or suspended in such proceeding could raise the question of the right to remove, or the legality of the proceedings which resulted in his removal, may not be clearly settled; but it would seem that in so far as such questions appeared from the record, not being questions of fact upon which the removing power had exercised judgment, they might be considered. The official, if unlawfully removed, so long as there was no other incumbent, could resort to mandamus to be restored to the office. This being true, I do not observe any objection to raising the same question where the appointee institutes proceedings for possession. It is no more the trial of title to the office in one case than the other. I find, however, that one court has held, where the fact was that a person had been appointed to the office of sheriff by the governor in place of one removed for causes stated in the statute, that the presumption was conclusive that the governor acted within the limits of his authority. Questions relating to the expulsion and restoration of officers in or members of benevolent, church and other societies, as well as in private corporations, are discussed in a subsequent chapter.

Src. 713. County seat — Election to determine location of.— In many of the states statutes have been enacted providing conditions that must exist as precedent to the submission to the electors of the question of a change in the location of a county seat. Usually it is provided that a petition must be presented to the county board, signed by a certain number or proportionate number of the freeholders or electors of the county, as preliminary to action by such board. The provision often is that such petition asking for removal shall be signed by at least three-fifths of the electors whose names appear upon the poll lists of the last preceding election as having voted thereat. Upon receiving such petition the county board has certain duties to perform in respect thereto, among others that of comparing the names to the petition with the poll lists.

1 Conklin v. Cunningham, 38 Pac. 179. 2 People v. M. M. P. Union, 118 N. Y. Rep. (N. M.) 101.

<br>

### --- Page 0384 ---

§ 718a.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 927

to ascertain the identity of the signers and the fact that their names were on such poll lists, as well as to determine the total number of votes appearing upon such poll lists, and whether the requisite two-fifths of such as can be identified are signers of the petition, after rejecting those names which cannot be identified. The principal questions involved in this discussion are whether the duties of the county board in such respect are judicial or merely ministerial, and to what extent, if at all, can the action of the board be compelled or controlled by mandamus. Ordinarily there is no right of appeal from the decision of the county board, or any other adequate remedy, other than by mandamus, unless perhaps by certiorari, in case the board arbitrarily refuses to pass upon the sufficiency of the petition and order the election in conformity to the provisions of law, or in case they act arbitrarily in refusing to count names to the petition that are or should be by them properly identified. Upon investigation I find that the courts are not agreed upon the questions stated. It is held by some courts that the county board, in receiving and acting upon the petition, exercises powers of an administrative character, and not in any proper sense in a judicial capacity, although, as in the case of many other public bodies and officers, it becomes necessary for them to pass upon and determine questions of fact in exercising their legitimate functions. In other states, it is held that the county board, or other body acting upon the petition, act in a judicial capacity, and that their decision upon the question submitted to them as to the sufficiency of the petition is conclusive until set aside or reversed by direct proceedings.

Sc. 713a. Subject continued.— The Wisconsin court, while holding that the duties of the board in the respect stated are administrative and not judicial, seem to confine such conclusion to the action of the board upon the face of the petition and the poll list. It holds, contrary to the Iowa court, that where there is a question as to the identity of a signer, by reason of an initial letter of the Christian name being signed either to the petition or appearing upon the poll list instead of the

1 State v. Supervisors, 88 Wis. 355. 2 Baker v. Supervisors, 40 Iowa, 226; State v. Carpenter, 54 Iowa, 340.

<br>

### --- Page 0385 ---

928 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 714, 715.]

full Christian name, or mistake in spelling, or the like, the board should receive extraneous evidence to determine the question of identity, and that their conclusion regularly and fairly made as to the facts proved will be conclusive. Hence in proceedings by mandamus their decision cannot be reviewed or revised. However, where the board illegally refuses to hear any evidence or consider any facts dehors the petition and poll lists, mandamus will lie to compel them to consider such evidence and act upon it. In other words, it will lie to compel them to exercise the discretionary powers vested in them according to law, as well as to correct arbitrary and illegal action.

Sec. 714. In Minnesota, decision of board as to genuineness of names and qualifications of signers to a petition conclusive.—It seems that in Minnesota, under its statute, the county board have authority to inquire into and determine the genuineness of signatures to a petition and the qualifications of signers, and if after they have deducted such as were improperly signed, if there remain a sufficient number, then they certify their conclusion to the auditor, who must order an election. It is held in that state that the decision of the commissioners with respect to the names either properly or improperly upon the petition is conclusive. The same court held in another case that if the action of the board in erasing certain names involved the consideration of certain questions of fact and the exercise of a discretion which it had lawfully exercised, then mandamus would not lie; but where the facts are conceded or no discretion is exercised, and the board erred merely as to questions of law, then mandamus would lie to compel the board to restore the names thus erroneously erased.

Sec. 715. Rule in Nebraska.— In Nebraska the action of the commissioners in rejecting certain signatures to a petition was controlled by mandamus where such action was clearly illegal. It appears that they only allowed the names of such persons as were personally known to be qualified signers under the law.

State v. Supervisors, 88 Wis. 355.
State v. Geib et al. (Minn.), 68 N. W. Rep. 854.
Currie v. Paulson (Minn.), 45 N. W. Rep. 1081.

<br>

### --- Page 0386 ---

§§ 716, 717.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 929

and refused to hear proof upon that question as to others. The questions of fact were referred to a referee to report, and the court found from such report that after deducting the names of persons who were not qualified petitioners and those who, after signing the petition, had subsequently signed a remonstrance against the same, there remained the requisite number, and thereupon issued a peremptory writ commanding the commissioners to call a special election in said county and submit the question of relocation of the county seat instead of commanding the board to proceed to determine the question of the sufficiency of the petition according to law, which would indicate that the duties of the board in that respect were ministerial.

§ 716. Rule in Nevada.—In Nevada the court went to the extreme in holding, where the statute merely directed the board of county commissioners to order an election of county officers, provided a certain number of qualified electors should petition therefor, and made it the duty of such board to ascertain whether the requisite number of qualified electors had joined in the petition, that the nature of their duties in such respect was judicial, and, the board having acted and refused to order an election, that such action could not be controlled by mandamus, nor could it be compelled to order an election in case the petition was sufficient.

§ 717. Canvassing the votes cast at such an election.—In a previous chapter it has been stated that the validity of such an election could be tested by mandamus to county officers to compel them to hold their offices at the county seat, and in such a proceeding the whole question of the legality of the election was open to investigation. The question now under consideration is to what extent in proceedings by mandamus can the court direct or control the board of canvassers with respect to the performance of their duties in canvassing the vote and declaring and certifying the result, and this involves the question whether their duties are judicial or ministerial in character.

1 State v. Commissioners, 8 Nev. 809. 2 Ante, § 684.

<br>

### --- Page 0387 ---

930: SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$717]

Statutes sometimes provide the method of procedure, though ordinarily it is provided that the votes shall be canvassed and return made thereof in the same manner as required by law in case of election held for certain officers. Where the latter is the method prescribed, then it follows that the duties of the canvassing boards are ministerial to the same extent as in canvassing and returning the votes of the election held for officers, and will be compelled by mandamus to the same extent. Where a special method is directed and required, then the question of the character of the duties of the board of canvassers and other officers who are to declare or certify the result, whether judicial or ministerial, depends largely upon the scope of the duties thus required.

It is the settled law in Michigan, under the provisions of their statute, that the decision of the supervisors in canvassing and determining the vote upon the question of the removal of the county seat is conclusive, and that no judicial review of their action is provided by law. The statute of that state is not unlike the statutes of many other states with respect to the powers and duties of the board of canvassers. After prescribing the acts to be done and the manner of procedure, it concludes, “and they shall enter upon their records the time when such removal shall be deemed to have taken place, and from and after that time the place so designated shall be and continue the county seat of said county for all purposes whatsoever.” The court states that “it is impossible to give due force to this language without holding that the decision of the supervisors was meant to be and must be conclusive. There is no intimation that any right to contest it was to be left open afterwards, but their action is to settle the question of the removal for all purposes whatsoever. It could not settle that question if a judicial review was still the right of the dissatisfied parties. The question was one of a nature peculiarly proper to be submitted finally to their determination, and this consideration is not without force when the question is one of

1 Pinkerton v. Staninger, 101 Mich. 273; Attorney-General v. Supervisors, 33 Mich. 289; Attorney-General v. Supervisors, 34 Mich. 211; Attorney-General v. Board of Canvassers, 64 Mich. 612; Double v. McQueen, 96 Mich. 39,

<br>

### --- Page 0388 ---

§ 718.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 931

construction. In a late case the doctrine was applied where the board rejected the vote of a township on the ground of fraud. Unless the powers of such a board are more extended than the provisions of the statute would seem to indicate, it must be conceded that their duties were mainly ministerial; that they were not invested with judicial power in the matter of the determination of the number of votes that were respectively cast upon either side of the proposition, much less have they jurisdiction to determine the question of fraud in the conduct of the election in any precinct. It would seem that the language of the statute is more consistent with that construction which would make their determination conclusive when they had proceeded within the limits of their powers. Determinations based upon a proceeding which was not within the power of the board or officer are of no force, and in the particular case was the same, in effect, as though the board had not completed their duties.

Sec. 718. Where canvassers act under special law.— Where the legislature of a state passed a special act providing for the submission of the question of the location of a county seat to the electors, and therein made it the duty of the county judge to canvass the returns of the election, it was held that the nature of his duties was judicial in determining whether the election had been properly held, and also in determining as to the genuineness and sufficiency of the returns, and therefore his action in rejecting certain returns was conclusive to the extent that mandamus would not lie to compel him to pass upon them and receive them. If the act either expressly or by implication gave the judge the power to determine all questions as to the validity of the election, then of course his duties in that respect at best were judicial. If, however, there was no such provision, and it was sought to imply such power from the mere fact that he was in other respects a judicial officer, then the decision is not in harmony with the general doctrine, because it is the character of the act and not the

Attorney-General v. Supervisors, 3 Arberry v. Beavers, 6 Tex. 457.
33 Mich. 289. See also Bell v. Pike, 53 N. H. 473.
Pinkerton v. Staninger, 101 Mich. 273, 4

<br>

### --- Page 0389 ---

932 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 719-723.]

official position of the officer that determines; and, as has been stated in other parts of this work, duties of a ministerial character can be imposed upon judicial officers as well as duties of a judicial character can be imposed upon ministerial officers.

Sec. 719. Announcement of result of an election.—Mandamus will lie on the relation of a voter or taxpayer interested to compel the proper officers to announce the result of an election.¹

Sec. 720. Oath of office—Administering of may be compelled.—Also to compel the proper officers to administer the oath of office to one duly elected.²

Sec. 721. Sufficiency of sureties on bond—Proper officers may be compelled to take action to determine.—Also to compel them to act according to their duty upon the sufficiency of sureties offered by a person elected to a municipal office. They cannot refuse because the title to the office is elsewhere disputed.³

Sec. 722. Issue—Certificate of election may be compelled.—The officer may be compelled to attach the proper seal to a commission; also to compel a secretary of state, or other officer whose duty it is, to issue certificates of election to persons duly declared elected to an office; also to attach their official signature and seal of office to commissions issued by the governor.⁴

Sec. 723. Appointment to office—When compelled.—It is well settled that mandamus lies to compel the proper board or officer having the requisite authority to perform duties required by law with respect to making appointments to fill official positions. The writ in such cases only lies to compel action, not to interfere with the discretion of the appointing

¹ People v. Salomon, 45 Ill. 415, 456; Magruder v. Swan, 25 Md. 173.

² In re Heath, 3 Hill, 42. See, however, State v. Governor, 1 Dutch. 331; State v. Governor, 39 Mo.

³ Commonwealth v. City of Philadelphia, 7 Am. Dec. (N. S.) 802, 888; Hawkins v. Governor, 1 Ark.

⁴ State v. Lawrence, 3 Kan. 95, 570.

⁵ State v. Wrotnowski, 17 La. Ann.

<br>

### --- Page 0390 ---

§§ 724-725a.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE—ELECTIONS. 933.

power in the matter of the person to be selected. It will not issue where the appointing power has acted and filled the position; nor unless it is clearly manifest that action is not intended to be taken.

Sec. 724. Board having the power of confirmation may be compelled to act.—Where it is made by law the duty of an executive officer to fill certain positions by appointment, which nominations are to be confirmed by the legislative branch of the municipal government, mandamus will lie to compel such legislative body to act upon the nominations that may be presented.

Sec. 725. Civil service—Appointment of veterans.—Some of the states have enacted statutes which direct that preference be given with respect to certain classes of officers to honorably discharged Union soldiers. The only decisions that I have at hand are those rendered by different departments of the supreme court of New York, and there may appear some little conflict of judicial opinion.

Sec. 725a. Appointment of veterans continued.—It was held that the writ would lie to compel an appointing power to observe the law of 1887 (ch. 464) in the respect stated, upon the ground that the act declared in terms that all public officials are charged with a faithful compliance with its terms both in letter and spirit; while under 1 Revised Statutes (8th ed., p. 407, sec. 1), which provides that honorably discharged Union soldiers and sailors shall be preferred for appointment in every department, it was held that such a soldier was not entitled to a writ of mandamus to compel a judge to appoint him to the office of crier of the court, where, notwithstanding he had made application, and was the only soldier who applied and was duly qualified; that, notwithstanding the provisions of such act, the judge had power to exercise his discretion to appoint from the whole body of electors, and, having

1 People v. Wendell, 10 N.Y. 8. 587;  
4 People v. Common Council, 6 Chi. Leg. News, 176.  
2 King v. Minister, etc., 1 Nev. & P. 56.  
5 Sullivan v. Gilroy, 8 N.Y.S. 401, 55 Hun, 285.  

3 People v. Regents, 4 Mich. 98.

<br>

### --- Page 0391 ---

984 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 726, 727.

Exercised that power and discretion, his duty was performed; and since mandamus lies only to compel action by inferior officers and tribunals, there was nothing upon which the writ could take effect. The writ will not lie to compel the appointment of a Union veteran where an appointee is exercising the functions of the office; nor where the board considers him incompetent. In New York recent statutes have provided for competitive examinations, and it is probable, where an applicant has passed the examination, the discretion of the appointing power is removed. Nor will an alternative writ be granted where the office is filled to admit a Union veteran. The writ, however, will lie to compel the board to place on the eligible list one whose name has been unlawfully removed therefrom.

Sec. 726. Boards having joint authority may be compelled to meet and appoint.— Where it is made by law the duty of separate boards to meet in joint assembly to appoint certain officers, and the time is fixed by law, the members who refuse so to meet may be compelled by mandamus.

Sec. 727. Distinction between officers and employees.— A distinction is made between officers and employees in respect to compelling appointment to position, and it is generally held that employees are not within the rule which prescribes that the right or title to a public office cannot be determined on mandamus. Thus, the position of teacher in a public school is not in the nature of an office, but as an employee; and persons to be appointed under an act which required a superintendent of public buildings to appoint all persons necessary to the maintenance of the department of public buildings are employees, and, being employees, they are within the provisions of the civil service act of New York; and where one is entitled to appointment, mandamus will lie to compel the superintendent to make

1 People v. Wendell, 10 N. Y. 8. 587.  
5 People v. Cobb, 48 N. Y. 8. 120.

2 People v. Trustees, etc., 41 N. Y. 6; Lamb v. Lynd, 44 Pa. St. 336.  
3 People v. Trustees, etc., 44 N. Y. 8. 449; People v. Sutton, 34 N. Y. S. 487, Hun, 173; Kennedy v. Board of Education, 82 Cal. 480.

4 People v. Trustees, 44 N. Y. S. 471.

<br>

### --- Page 0392 ---

§§ 728, 729.] RIGHT AND TITLE TO OFFICE — ELECTIONS. 935

the appointment in case he has without cause refused.1 The position of janitor of a city building being created by law is an office, and mandamus in New York will not lie to admit to possession another person who claims to be entitled to it, where the office is filled, though the applicant is a veteran.2

§ 728. Compelling a corporation to remove an officer.—It is stated by Judge Dillon in his valuable work on Municipal Corporations, section 251, note, that the courts may by mandamus compel a corporation to remove an officer, and the result of the cases on this point is considered to be that, where the offense of the officer is such that the corporation has the power to remove, the court will only compel it to do so where someone is injured by the omission to remove; but where it is required to remove, or the office is declared by the charter or statute to be void if such an act be done or committed, there the court will compel it to remove, though no one be shown to have been aggrieved. Citing Rex v. Truro, 3 Barn. & Ald. 592; Rex v. West Love, 5 Dowl. & R. 416; Grant on Corp. 243, and note.

§ 729. Acceptance of office.—In England, where one has been elected to a corporate office, he may be compelled by mandamus to serve if his refusal be without reasonable excuse. The statute there provides for a fine to be imposed in case of refusal. Under the common law such fine was not in lieu of service, but was made so by the provision of statute.3 In this country, where office is not usually regarded as a burden, but is quite generally coveted, there has not been much occasion to test the power of compulsion to serve, or to impose a fine in case of refusal. This, however, cannot affect the principle involved. Public offices must be occupied and their duties performed, and power must exist somewhere to compel performance of such duties. The supreme court of Illinois, it appears, had occasion to pass upon the question, and held that mandamus would lie to compel one who had been duly appointed to accept a municipal office, where he possessed the requisite qualifications.4

1 In re Ostrander, 34 N. Y. S. 295.

2 In re Hardy, 41 N. Y. S. 469.

3 Rex v. Bower, 1 Barn. & Cres. 585; Grant on Corporations, 222.

4 People v. Williams (IL), 33 N. E. Rep. 849.

<br>

### --- Page 0393 ---

936 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 730, 731.]

§ 730. Fitness for office.— The determination of the fitness and qualifications of applicants for office in certain cases in the state of New York being left to certain officers, their decision that an honorably discharged soldier does not possess the business capacity necessary to discharge the duties of the office, and hence is not entitled to preference under the law, is not subject to review by mandamus.

§ 731. Promotion of veteran.— Neither will the writ lie to compel the promotion of such a discharged soldier, giving preference. The civil service law only applies to appointments, not to promotions. The New York statute has been amended so as to apply to promotions.

1 People v. Trustees, 7 N. Y. S. 125; 2 Brown v. Duane, 14 N. Y. S. 450, People v. Commissioners, 20 N. Y. S. 21, 65 Hun, 169.

SPECIAL REMEDIES.

Opening a highway or street.

Right of appeal in such cases—Effect on writ.

Licenses—Duty in reference to.

Contracts—Letting of—Discretion exists where statute authorizes rejection of bids—Same where the notice for proposals so specifies.

[§ 732,

Meeting of board or council—Compelling attendance of members—Writ does not lie.

Ordinances—Enforcement of—Compelling.

Delivery of bonds—Same.

School boards—Where right of appeal exists to superintendent, mandamus does not lie—Writ lies to compel action.

Teachers not officers—School board—Duty to issue certificates—President of, to sign contract—Superintendent compelled to examine applicants—Discretion not controlled—Payment of compensation to.

Sec. 732. Municipal corporations.—In the preceding chapter the duty of public officers that may or may not be enforced by mandamus has been considered. We have now to deal with the duties of municipal boards or corporations as such, and ascertain to what extent their duties may be compelled or controlled by means of such a proceeding; and it may occur that some matters may be treated under this general division of the

<br>

### --- Page 0396 ---

§§ 733-735. MANDAMUS To MUNICIPAL BODIES.

subject that quite properly might be treated under the head of “Public Officers,” as well as that some matters may have been included under the latter division that properly might be clearly under this division. They are so nearly connected in many instances that it is difficult to determine the proper classification. With respect to the question of election for change of location of county seats, it was thought proper to treat the subject in connection with the questions relating to election of officers, as thereby repetition might be avoided, and, the general principles that control each class of elections being substantially the same, the general subject could be more thoroughly considered. So it was thought proper to treat the question of auditing claims under the head of “Public Officers,” as sometimes those duties are performed by a single officer and sometimes by a number constituting a board.

Sec. 733. County boards and municipal boards.— Counties are ordinarily classed as quasi-municipal bodies, and for the purposes of this discussion will be classed as municipal corporations; and if any distinction is made to rest upon the character of the corporation as not being distinctly municipal, it will be noted.

Sec. 734. Auditing claims.— The duties of officers in auditing claims have been the subject of discussion in a previous chapter. Upon county boards of commissioners or supervisors in many states is imposed the duty of auditing and allowing claims against the county, and it has been stated that mandamus lies to compel action merely, but not to review the exercise of discretion. Claimants ordinarily have an adequate remedy at law, either by appeal from the action of the board or by an action directly against the county, and this is a sufficient reason why the remedy by mandamus will not be allowed.

Sec. 735. Payment of claims.— With respect to the payment of claims the county board has no duties to perform. Upon 

1 See sec. 654 et seq. 50 Ill. 213; People v. Supervisors, 11 
2 People v. Common Council, 34 N. Y. 563; People v. Supervisors, 29 Mich. 201; People v. Clark County, Wis. 79.

<br>

### --- Page 0397 ---

940 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 736.

Allowance of a claim the clerk ordinarily is to draw an order or warrant upon the treasurer, and this duty, as appears from what has been stated in a previous chapter, may be enforced by mandamus, being merely ministerial, and the treasurer, if he have funds, is required to pay; a duty which in some cases, as has been stated, may be enforced by mandamus, in others not. There are some courts which have held that, as the party has an adequate remedy at law by action upon a county order, he must pursue such remedy; and this doctrine has been applied to the payment of salaries. This subject has been quite thoroughly treated and authorities cited in a previous chapter, and need not be the subject of further discussion here.

Sec. 736. Judgments—Providing for payment.—It has been held that mandamus is not the proper remedy, in the absence of a direct statute providing otherwise, to enforce the payment of a debt by a county or municipality other than bonded indebtedness, where a specific method is provided, where the claim has not been reduced to judgment; but is the proper proceeding to compel such a corporation to provide for the payment of a claim thus reduced to judgment by the levy of a tax therefor, when under the law of the particular state such judgment cannot be collected upon execution or other legal process. In the federal courts such a writ becomes the substitute for an execution. A new writ may issue as often as occasion requires. The amount for which the writ will lie can only be limited by what the judgment itself declares; and such amount may be included in one levy, though there is a large accumulation of indebtedness which should have been paid in installments.

See Payment of Claims, sec. 658 et seq.

People v. Clark County, 50 Ill. 213.

People v. Mayor of New York, 25 Wend. 680.

See sec. 658 et seq.

Hugg v. Jones (N. J. L.), 36 Atl. Rep. 685.

Riggs v. Johnson County, 6 Wall. 166; United States v. New Orleans, 98 U. S. 381; Cass County v. Johnson, 95 U. S. 360; Rees v. Watertown, 19 Wall. 107; Weber v. Lee County, 6 Wall. 210; East St. Louis v. Amy, 120 U.S. 600.

Weber v. Lee, 6 Wall. 210; Memphis v. Brown, 97 U. S. 300.

Rees v. Watertown, 19 Wall. 107.

United States v. Knox County, 122 U.S. 306.

East St. Louis v. Amy, 120 U.S. 600.

### --- Page 0398 ---

, § 137] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 941

Sec. 737. Cannot be compelled to levy tax in excess of maximum allowed by law.— The municipality cannot be compelled to levy a tax for the payment of a judgment where the proceeds of a maximum levy allowed by law are insufficient to meet ordinary expenses of the municipal government. This question has been before the courts many times, and becomes important in view of the fact that holders of county or other warrants, where the annual fund has been exhausted, place them in judgment and then seek to enforce a special levy of taxes to pay the judgment. In some of the states the law provides that a separate tax shall be levied to pay judgments. By means of such a proceeding the limit of the levy that is authorized by law is exceeded. The question whether the municipal authorities could be compelled to levy a tax to pay judgments of that character, when the amount of taxes they were authorized to levy was required to meet the current expenses, was before the supreme court of the United States. It appeared in that case that the power of the county to levy a tax for ordinary county revenue was limited to six mills annually on the dollar of the assessed value of taxable property. That the answer of the county, made in a proceeding to compel the levy of a separate tax to meet a judgment, was in effect that the full amount of taxes allowed by law for the ordinary revenue of the county had been levied, and that such levy was required, and more too, for the proper maintenance of the county government. That no part of the revenue for such years could have been devoted to the payment of the judgment without seriously impairing the efficiency of said government. That the maximum levy for the following year would not be sufficient to pay the ordinary expenses of said county, and that no part thereof could be applied for the payment of said judgment without seriously impairing the efficiency of said government. It was determined by the court that when the ordinary expenses of carrying on the government of a municipal corporation require all the proceeds arising from a tax which is the

1 East St. Louis v. Amy, 110 U.S. 821; City of Dennison v. Foster, 87 S. W. Rep. 167 (Tex. Civ. App.); Coffin v. Davenport, 26 Iowa, 515; Polk v. Minett, 37 Iowa, 34.

4 Clay County v. McAleer, 115 U.S. 616.

<br>

### --- Page 0399 ---

942 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 737.

full limit the corporation is authorized to levy, it cannot be compelled to apply a part of such fund to the payment of a judgment held by a creditor against it, nor can it be compelled to levy a special tax for such purpose. In another case in the same court it was said: “The question, what expenses are proper and necessary for the municipal administration, is not judicial; it is confided by law to the discretion of the municipal authorities. No court has the right to control that discretion, much less to usurp and supersede it. To do so in a single year would require a revision of the details of every estimate and expenditure, based upon an inquiry into all branches of the municipal service; to do it for a series of years and in advance is to attempt to foresee every exigency and to provide against every contingency that may arise to affect the public necessities.” That was said in a case in which a bondholder was seeking payment out of the ordinary revenue fund after the special tax authorized by law for his benefit had been exhausted. It was held, however, that the balance due him was just as much a charge on the ordinary revenue fund as if there had been no other provision in his favor. Mandamus, however, will lie to compel the levy of such a tax where the full levy is not required to defray the current expenses chargeable upon the ordinary revenue fund. Thus, where it appeared that a tax of only five mills upon the dollar had been levied, when by the charter of the city they could have levied ten mills, it was proof that the full tax was not needed for city purposes, and the court was therefore free to require them to proceed with the execution of the power which had been conferred by law until the judgment creditor was paid. The general doctrine also is that municipal officers will not be compelled to levy a tax in excess of the amount allowed by statute at the time when the debt was created.

The question whether mandamus would lie to compel the

1 See Coffin v. Davenport, 26 Iowa, 515; Coy v. City of Lyons, 17 Iowa, 1; United States v. Macon County, 99 U.S. 582. 4 Coy v. City of Lyons, 17 Iowa, 1. 2 East St. Louis v. United States, 110 U.S. 321. 5 United States v. Macon County, 99 U.S. 582; Stewart v. Police Jury, 116 U.S. 135.

<br>

### --- Page 0400 ---

§ 737.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 943

Levy of a tax generally upon the taxable property of a municipality to pay a judgment recovered upon bonds which were issued for a municipal improvement, where the cost thereof was to be collected by special assessment against the property benefited, was determined by the supreme court of the United States in United States v. Fort Scott, 99 U. S. 152. The judgment was in the ordinary form, except that the court added: “And it is further ordered and adjudged that the judgment now here rendered be enforced and collected pursuant to law in such case made and provided.” It was stated by the court: “That the bonds for the amount of which the relator obtained judgment constitute a debt or a portion of the bonded indebtedness of the city within the meaning of the statute cannot well be doubted. The ordinance which required the improvements in question in terms directs that the cost thereof shall be paid for in the bonds of the city, to be signed by the mayor, attested by the city clerk under the corporate seal of the city, and countersigned by the city treasurer. Further, each bond declares upon its face that it is a special improvement bond of the city of Fort Scott, Kansas, and that the city, for value received, acknowledges itself to owe and promises to pay the holder the amount thereof. Still further, the statute under which the ordinance was framed authorizes the council to pay the cost of such special improvements by issuing the bonds of the city. Finally, the bonds were issued by the city authorities, by whom the proceeds were received and expended under the direction of the council. They constitute, therefore, in every just sense, debts which the city in its corporate capacity is under a statutory and legal obligation to provide for in some effectual and substantial manner.” In reply to the contention on the part of the city that the holder of the bonds, by the terms of the statute and the ordinance, must look for payment exclusively to assessments upon the property specially improved and benefited, the court proceeds to state that they “cannot yield their consent to such an interpretation of the contract; that it is neither expressly nor by necessary implication provided that the holder of the bonds may not be paid in some other mode, or that the city will not, under the authority derived from other sections of the statute, comply with its promise to pay the bonds.

<br>

### --- Page 0401 ---

944 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 787.

with interest at maturity. As between the city and its taxpayers it was certainly its duty, through the council, to provide, if practicable, payment by taxation upon the property improved, rather than upon all the taxable property within its corporate limits. But the duty to make such distribution of the burden of special improvements did not lessen its obligation in accordance with its express agreement to pay the interest and principal of the bonds at maturity. . . . The agreement is that the city shall pay the interest and principal at maturity. There is no reservation, as against the purchasers of the bonds, of a right, under any circumstances, to withhold payment at maturity, or to postpone payment until the city should obtain by special assessments upon the improved property the means with which to make payment, or to withhold payment altogether if the special assessments should prove inadequate for payment.”

The same question was considered by the supreme court of Wisconsin (Fowler v. City of Superior, 85 Wis. 411), and the court took ground, if possible, in advance of that taken by the federal court. It was said by Orton, J., in delivering the opinion: “Although such bonds are a mere substitute for assessments which the city owns as a resource for their payment, they constitute nevertheless an indebtedness of the city within the meaning of the constitution;” and it was held that special improvement bonds, containing an unconditional promise of a city to pay, constitute an indebtedness of the city, although they recite that they are payable out of the proceeds of certain improvement assessments and are issued upon the faith and security of said assessments, and their payment is made chargeable upon the property benefited by said improvements. The peculiar feature of this decision is that, unlike that of the federal court, where the principle was applied to enforce the payment of the indebtedness, it was applied to defeat the collection of the bonds. By reason of the bonds constituting an indebtedness of the city, its effect was to increase the indebtedness to an amount in excess of the constitutional limit, and hence such indebtedness could not be enforced against the city, the purpose of the action being to restrain the city officers from selling or disposing of the bonds. Many cases are

<br>

### --- Page 0402 ---

§ 737. MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 945

referred to as holding the doctrine thus announced. It is to be observed, however, that the conclusion of the federal court is not concurred in by some courts, at least unless there is that in the mere wording of the bond which distinguishes the case cited from those cases where the conditions were similar in most respects except that bonds were not issued. Thus, where a city advertised for proposals for grading and paving a street, the expense thereof to be assessed pursuant to the provisions of the city charter, and the plaintiff made proposals which were accepted without qualification, no provision in respect to payment being made except such as might be inferred from these facts, it was held that the provisions of the charter in the section referred to in the advertisement in respect to the time and mode of payment were to be taken as a part of the contract. The effect was to require the city authorities to make and collect an assessment with reasonable diligence after the expense of the work should be ascertained, and pay over the money when collected; that no action was maintainable by the plaintiff against the city until after the assessment and collection of his compensation, or the default of the officers to proceed therein as required by law. The reasoning of the New York court is not the same as that indulged in by the federal court. Thus they state: “The common council probably were not willing, if they could do so, to subject the city to the payment of this expense otherwise than by raising the amount by assessment and then handing it over to the contractor; and a contract to this effect would seem to be precisely what the charter contemplates in reference to paying for such improvements. The charter may properly be regarded in giving a construction to the contract, as both parties must be considered to have acted in view of it; the common council because it prescribes their powers and duties, and the contractor because it is expressly referred to in the notice, and because it was his duty in making the contract to look into the charter and learn the duties and powers of the public agents with whom he was

1 Fuller v. Chicago, 89 Ill. 282; Atkinson v. Sen, 48 Kan. 138; Argenti Sage v. Brooklyn, 89 N. Y. 190; v. San Francisco, 16 Cal. 256. Reilly v. Albany, 112 N. Y. 30; State Hunt v. City of Utica, 18 N. Y. Commissioners, 87 Ohio St. 526; 442.

<br>

### --- Page 0403 ---

946 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 738.

dealing.” The supreme court of Michigan apparently is in accord with the doctrine stated by the New York court. In fact they hold the city is not liable in any event; that action will not lie; that mandamus is the only remedy, and that will not lie unless the fund has been collected and covered into the treasury.”

Sec. 738. Amount of tax cannot be restricted after debt contracted.—The courts will treat as void all legislation abrogating or restricting the power of taxation delegated to the municipality upon the faith of which contracts have been made with it. Where there are no restrictions or limitations upon the amount of tax that a municipality may lawfully levy at the time it creates an indebtedness, the legislature of the particular state cannot, by imposing such a restriction or limiting the amount of taxes that the municipality may levy for municipal purposes to a per cent. upon the assessed valuation of the property therein or otherwise, thereby defeat or impair the right of the creditor to enforce the payment of his debt. It was stated in a leading case: “It is well settled that a state may disable itself by contract from exercising its taxing power in particular cases. It is clear that where a state has authorized a municipal corporation to contract and to exercise the power of local taxation to the extent necessary to meet its engagements, the power thus given cannot be withdrawn until the contract is satisfied. The state and the corporation are equally bound in such cases. The power given becomes a trust which the donor cannot annul, and which the donee is bound to execute; and neither the state nor the corporation can any more impair the obligation of the contract in this way than in any other.” Speaking with reference to the facts of the particular case it was further stated: “The laws requiring taxes to the requisite amount to be collected, in force when the bonds were issued, are still in force for all the purposes of this case. The act of 1863, so far as it affects these bonds, is a nullity. It

See, however, Morrison v. Bernards, 36 N. J. L. 219; Argenti v. San Francisco, 16 Cal. 256; Bank v. City of Lansing, 25 Mich. 207; People v. Township, 10 Mich. 274; Von Hoffman v. City of Quincy, 4 Wall. 535; Wolff v. New Orleans, 108 U. S. 358.

<br>

### --- Page 0404 ---

§ 738.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 947

is the duty of the city to impose and collect the taxes in all respects as if that act had not been passed. A different result would leave nothing of the contract but an absolute right of no practical value, and render the protection of the constitution a shadow and a delusion. The prohibition of the constitution against the passage of laws impairing the obligation of contracts applies to the contracts of the state and to those of its agents acting under its authority as well as to contracts between individuals. And that obligation is impaired in the sense of the constitution when the means by which a contract at the time of its execution could be enforced, that is, by which the parties could be obliged to perform it, are rendered less efficacious by legislation operating directly upon those means.

Without the remedy the contract may indeed, in the sense of the law, be said not to exist, and its obligation to fall within the class of those moral and social duties which depend for their fulfillment wholly upon the will of the individual. The ideas of validity and remedy are inseparable, and both are parts of the obligation which is guaranteed by the constitution. The obligation of a contract is the law which binds the parties to perform their agreement.

Peculiar conditions existing in Tennessee prevented the application of this principle. By legislative act the charter of the city of Memphis in that state was repealed. In the title of the act one of the purposes was stated to be “to provide for the compromise and make settlement of the debts of such corporations.” A bill was filed in behalf of certain creditors whose demands had been reduced to judgment, praying for the appointment of a receiver to take charge of the assets, tax-books, etc., and to collect all outstanding indebtedness, etc. It appeared that, pursuant to writs of mandamus, taxes had been collected and applied to the payment of some of such judgments, but the full amount had not been collected. The following propositions were decided by the court: (1) Property held by the city of Memphis for public use, such as public buildings, streets, squares, parks, promenades, wharves, landing-places, fire-engines, hose and hose-carriages, engine-houses,

1 Von Hoffman v. City of Quincy, 4 Wall. 535.
2 Wolff v. New Orleans, 103 U.S. 358.

<br>

### --- Page 0405 ---

948 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 738.

Engineering instruments, and generally all things held for government purposes, cannot be subjected to the payment of its debts. (2) The private property of individuals within the limits of the territory of the city cannot be subjected to the payment of the debts of the city except through taxation. (3) The power of taxation is legislative, and cannot be exercised otherwise than under the authority of the legislature. (4) Taxes levied according to law before the repeal of the charter, other than such as were levied in obedience to the special requirements of contracts entered into under the authority of law, and such as were levied under judicial direction for the payment of judgments recovered against the city, cannot be collected through the instrumentality of a court of chancery at the instance of the creditors of the city. Such taxes can only be collected under authority from the legislature. If no such authority exists, the remedy is by appeal to the legislature, which alone can grant relief. Whether taxes levied in obedience to contract obligations or under judicial direction can be collected through a receiver appointed by a court of chancery, if there be no public officer charged with authority from the legislature to perform that duty, is not decided, as the case does not require it. (5) The receiver and tax collector appointed under the authority of the act of March 13, 1879, is a public officer clothed with authority from the legislature for the collection of the taxes levied before the repeal of the charter. The funds collected by him from taxes levied under judicial direction cannot be appropriated to any other purpose than those for which they were raised. He, as well as any other agent of the state charged with the duty of their collection, can be compelled by appropriate judicial orders to proceed with the collection of such taxes by sale of property or by suit, or in any other way authorized by law, and to apply the proceeds upon the judgments.

In the course of reasoning by which these propositions were reached it was said by Field, J., speaking for himself and Justices Miller and Bradley, that “the powers of municipal corporations are such as the legislature may confer, and these may be enlarged, abridged, or entirely withdrawn at its pleas-

1 Meriwether v. Garrett, 102 U. S. 472.

<br>

### --- Page 0406 ---

§ 738.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES.

ure. There is no contract between the state and the public that the charter of a city shall not be at all times subject to legislative control. All persons who deal with such bodies are conclusively presumed to act upon knowledge of the power of the legislature. There is no such thing as a vested right held by any individual in the grant of legislative power to them.

Whatever property such a corporation holds subject to its debts will after its dissolution be so administered and applied by a court of equity. This does not include property held by the corporation in trust for private charity, nor in trust for the public. Upon the dissolution the property passes under the immediate control of the state; the agency of the corporation then ceases. Taxes levied but not collected do not constitute its property. Taxes are not debts. Debts are obligations for the payment of money founded upon contract, express or implied. Taxes are imposts levied for the support of the government or for some special purpose authorized by it. The consent of the taxpayer is not necessary to their enforcement. They operate in evitum. Nor is their nature affected by the fact that in some states an action for debt may be instituted for their recovery. The form of procedure cannot change their character. Nor are they different when levied under writs of mandamus for the payment of judgments, and when levied for the same purpose by statute. The levy in one case is as much by legislative authority as in the other. The writs of mandamus only require the officers of assessment and collection to obey existing law. In neither case are the taxes liens upon property unless made so by statute. Levied only by authority of the legislature, they can be altered, postponed or released at its pleasure. A repeal of the law under which a tax is levied at any time before the tax is collected generally puts an end to the tax, though the tax may be revived and enforced by subsequent legislation. We say generally; for there are some exceptions, where the tax provided is so connected with a contract, as the inducement for its execution, that the courts will hold the repeal of the law to be invalid as impairing the obligation of the contract. So long as the law authorizing the tax continues in force, the courts may by mandamus compel the officers empowered to levy it, or charged

<br>

### --- Page 0407 ---

950 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 739-741.]

with its collection, if unmindful and neglectful in the matter, to proceed and perform their duty; but when the law is gone and the office of collector is abolished, there is nothing upon which the courts can act. . . . The levying of taxes is not a judicial act. It has no elements of one. It is a high act of sovereignty, to be performed only by the legislature upon considerations of policy, necessity and the public welfare.”

Src. 739. Law authorizing contracting of debt presumes power to levy tax to pay it. Where, to be inferred, when the legislature of a state authorizes a county or a city to contract a debt by bond, that it intends to authorize it to levy such taxes as are necessary to pay the debt, unless there is in the act itself or in some general statute a limitation upon the power of taxation which repels such an inference. Where, however, the special provision for a tax for the payment of the indebtedness is limited by law, and that is insufficient, the writ will not issue to compel the levy of a greater sum.

Src. 740. The mandate — Broad enough to secure the fund. Where a judgment creditor has shown himself entitled to a mandamus to compel the levy and collection of taxes to liquidate such judgment by a county, he is entitled to such a mandate as will set in motion all the necessary machinery, including action on the part of the assessors and collectors, required to be taken after the levy is in fact made, without any special demand on the part of such officers to perform the required acts.

Src. 741. Merits of the action not open.—The merits of the action in which a judgment was obtained against a county is not open to inquiry in proceedings by mandamus to enforce payment of such judgment, nor can the county in such a proceeding interpose a set-off against such judgment. It was held, however, in North Carolina that mandamus would not issue to

1 Ralls County Court v. United States, 105 U. S. 728; Loan Co. v. Topeka, 20 Wall. 660; United States v. Macon County, 99 U. S. 582.

2 United States v. Macon County, 99 U. S. 582.

3 Marion County v. Color, 75 Fed. Rep. 352 (C. C. A.)

4 Ralls County v. United States, 105 U.S. 728; Sternberg v. State, 48 Neb. 299; United States v. County Court (U. S.), 7 Sup. Ct. Rep. 1171.

<br>

### --- Page 0408 ---

§ 742.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 951

compel the levy of a tax to pay a consent judgment entered into by town authorities, where it appears there was no authority to issue the bonds for which the judgment was rendered.

Sec. 742. Jurisdiction — Want of, may be shown. — It is always competent to show that the court was without jurisdiction. Where it appears on the face of the record it is always sufficient to defeat the enforcement of a judgment, and a judgment thus void may be disregarded in a collateral proceeding. This is but the application of a familiar rule which has been discussed in preceding chapters, and especially under the head of collateral attack. The rule goes farther when applied in the federal courts. It is not confined to the record, but want of jurisdiction may be shown by extrinsic evidence. The jurisdiction of the federal courts, when diverse citizenship is the ground, depends upon and is determined by the condition of things existing on the day action is brought. If on the day suit is brought the parties to the controversy are citizens of different states, the court will have and retain jurisdiction, notwithstanding that afterwards they may become citizens of the same state. A petition for removal on a similar ground will not be granted unless it appears that, at the time of action brought, the diversity of citizenship existed, even though at the date of the preparation and filing of the petition the parties had ceased to be citizens of the same state. If it appear from the record that such diverse citizenship did not in fact exist at the time action was brought, then the judgment, being a nullity, could be attacked whenever and wherever sought to be enforced. Whether, when the record disclosed that the parties were residents of different states, it could be impeached by extrinsic evidence showing it to have been untrue in fact, I do not find to have been determined. Yet other questions essential to jurisdiction have been thus controverted, and the rule declared by the federal court is broad enough to include the impeachment of such a record.

1 Union Bank v. Oxford, 119 N.C. Rep. 498; Elliott v. Lessee of Peirsol, 214. 1 Pet. 328.  
2 Moore v. Town Council, 32 Fed.  
3 Connolly v. Taylor, 2 Pet. 556.  
4 Bruce v. Gibson, 108 U. S. 563.

<br>

### --- Page 0409 ---

952 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 748, 744.

Sec. 743. Distinction between negotiable bonds and ordinary warrants.— A distinction is made between the rights of holders of the negotiable bonds of a county issued under special authority of the legislature to make subscription to the capital stock of railroads, or to fund floating indebtedness, or for other extraordinary expenditures, and the rights of the holders of ordinary county warrants. The obligation and duty to levy the tax to pay the former class of indebtedness, as provided by the act authorizing its issue, continues until the indebtedness is extinguished, notwithstanding the repeal of the statute. The provision of the law (where such provision exists) making it obligatory to levy a tax to pay such indebtedness enters into and becomes a part of the consideration of the contract and cannot be rescinded without impairing the obligation of the contract. In the case of ordinary warrants there is commonly no obligation resting upon the county authorities to levy a special tax for the exclusive purpose of paying the warrants of some particular holder thereof. When a county is authorized to levy a given rate of tax for general county purposes, no holder of county warrants, or of a judgment rendered thereon, has a right to demand that a special tax shall be carved out of this general rate and levied for the exclusive purpose of paying his warrants or judgments, unless the statute requires it and leaves the levying board or officer no discretion.

Sec. 744. Discretion conferred as to manner of providing fund not controlled.— Where the statute makes it optional with the county commissioners to pay either by levy of a special tax, or by warrants payable out of the general fund, and gives such commissioners such a discretion as to levying such tax, that discretion is not subject to control by mandamus. Quite frequently, in some of the states, a practice is resorted to of bringing actions against municipalities upon depreciated orders and warrants, resulting in judgment, and then to have the clerk place the amount of such judgment in the tax-roll as

1 United States v. Miller County, 4 Dill (C. C. A.) 233; Board of Commissioners v. King, 67 Fed. Rep. 2 Board of Commissioners v. King, 67 Fed. Rep. (C. C. A.) 202.

<br>

### --- Page 0410 ---

§ 745.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 953

a special tax. In this manner the holder obtains the face of the warrant, interest thereon, and recovers costs. The practice is a pernicious one. It encourages the issuing of warrants in excess of the anticipated revenue, compelling the taxpayer, oftentimes when the revenue collected for ordinary expenses has been diverted, to pay double, and enables the municipality, by this method, to evade statutory, and even constitutional, limitations with respect to levies for particular purposes. Statutes in some of the states provide for the collection of judgments against municipalities, the only requirement being an affidavit setting forth facts connected with the recovery of the judgment, which is filed with the clerk, and with this as his authority he levies the tax. It is doubtful if courts generally would sustain such a practice with respect to judgments thus obtained upon ordinary warrants. That they will not do so in the absence of an express statute has been held, not only by the United States supreme court, but also by the supreme court of Iowa and the circuit court of appeals. It was said by the former: “In this case the warrants upon which the relator’s judgment was obtained were all ordinary warrants, drawn upon the treasurer of the county, and, as admitted by the demurrer, drawn for the ordinary expenses of the county. None of them were issued in pursuance of a popular vote, or for any extraordinary expenditure. They were such instruments as the legislature contemplated might be employed in conducting the current and usual business of the county. The act which empowers the county board to levy a tax for ordinary county revenue speaks of them, and evidently intends that they shall be satisfied either from the proceeds of that tax or by their being received in payment thereof. They are simply a means of anticipating ordinary revenue.”

SEC. 745. Demand — When required.—It is clear that no demand ordinarily is necessary as a condition precedent to the granting of the writ. The law authorizing the issue of bonds ordinarily makes it the duty of the council from time to time

1 Supervisors v. United States, 18 Wall. 71; Commissioners v. King, 67 Fed. Rep. 202; Clark, Dodge & Co. v. Davenport, 14 Iowa, 494; Board of

<br>

### --- Page 0411 ---

954 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 746.

to levy a tax to pay interest and principal, and does not make that duty contingent upon a demand being made. The rule may not be the same with respect to ordinary judgments—that is, judgments obtained where equivalent provisions of law with respect to payment are absent; yet no good reason can be suggested why a demand should be required as a precedent condition in cases against municipalities where demand is not required in other cases. The supreme court of Florida (State v. Jacksonville, 22 Fla. 21) seem to have decided that a demand must precede mandamus, and where the statute makes provision requiring acts as conditions precedent which are equivalent to a demand, that such requirements must at least be substantially complied with.

§ 746. Writ not granted pending appeal from judgment—State court may issue to enforce judgment of federal court.—The writ will not ordinarily be granted pending an appeal from the judgment, even when by writ of error the case has been taken to the supreme court of the United States. Whether it shall issue or not in such cases is undoubtedly within the discretion of the court, and ordinarily the court would require an undertaking as in other cases, for costs at least, and conditioned against damage or injury by reason of the delay, and other conditions when the character and importance of the question demand it. An undertaking would not be required in cases where a municipal corporation is the appellant. The jurisdiction of state courts is ample to compel the levy of a tax to pay the judgment of a federal court, and it will exercise its power in proper cases. The court in all such cases acts to compel the performance of duty, and it is by law made the duty of proper officers to levy a tax sufficient to pay all judgments in whatever court obtained. The mere fact that the judgment was rendered in a federal court cannot in the slightest degree affect that duty, nor can it influence the state court. In the New England states judgments

1 State v. Racine, 22 Wis. 258; Fisher & Riggs v. Johnson County, 6 Wall. 166.  
2 Territory v. Woodbury, 44 N. W.; State v. City of Madison, 15 Wis. Rep. (Dak.) 1077. 30; State v. Supervisors, 20 Wis. 79.  
3 Territory v. Woodbury, 44 N. W. Rep. (Dak.) 1077.

<br>

### --- Page 0412 ---

§ 747-749.] 055

MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES.

Against municipalities are not enforceable by mandamus, but in a proceeding regulated by their laws. By the common law of those states the property of individuals is liable to execution for public debts.

Sec. 747.
