Payment of bonds — Compelling levy of tax therefor — Judgment essential in federal courts.— Although in a state where the courts generally have original jurisdiction in mandamus, a judgment may not be necessary to lay the foundation for the granting of a writ, yet as in the courts of the United States, except the Supreme Court of the District of Columbia, such writ can only be granted in aid of an existing jurisdiction, a judgment at law is necessary to support the issue of such a writ to enforce the levy and collection of taxes to pay bonds.1

Sec. 748. Rule in state courts.— It is quite generally held by the state courts that mandamus will lie to compel municipal authorities to levy a tax to pay not only the interest upon municipal bonds as it becomes due and payable, but also to meet the principal as it becomes due, when, either by the provisions of the act under which the bonds were issued or the provisions of general law, such duty is imposed upon such authorities.2 This doctrine proceeds upon the ground that the law has imposed a duty upon such authorities which is plain and imperative, ministerial in character, the performance of which will be compelled.3

Sec. 749. Rule not absolute in all cases.— This duty, however, is not absolute in all cases. If the bonds are absolutely invalid, as, for instance, that the assent of the electors or tax-

1 Hawks v. Kennebeck, 7 Mass. 461; Chase v. Merrimac Bank, 10 Pick. 564; Beardsley v. Smith, 16 Conn. 368; Hill v. Boston, 122 Mass. 344.

2 Greene County v. Daniel, 102 U.S. 187; Davenport v. Dodge County, 105 U.S. 237; Chickaming v. Carpenter, 106 U.S. 663.

3 Robinson v. Supervisors, 43 Cal. 353; State v. Board of Education, 27 Ohio St. 95; Shelley v. St. Charles Co., 80 Fed. Rep. 603; Board of Supervisors v. Randolph (Va.), 16 S.E. Rep. 722; People v. Getzendaner, 137 Ill. 234; Commonwealth v. Pittsburg, 34 Pa. 496; State v. Mayor, 52 Wis. 423; State v. Clinton County, 6 Ohio St. 280.

4 Commonwealth v. Pittsburg, 34 Pa. St. 496; Maddex v. Graham, 2 Met. (Ky.) 56.

<br>

### --- Page 0413 ---

956 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 750, 751.]

payers was not obtained where such assent was made by law a condition precedent to their issue, the court will not upon mandamus compel the levy of the tax to pay them; and where the validity of the bonds is disputed, and it is apparent that there is a question thus presented which ought to be determined in an action, the relator may be obliged to resort to proceedings to establish their validity. In other words, in cases of this character as well as all others, the relator must show a clear right to the relief demanded.

Sec. 750. Accurate statement of the rule.—A more accurate statement of the rule is, that where there is a clear duty to levy and collect a special tax to pay a special debt or class of debts, and where the genuineness of the debt is not questioned, and no valid defense is alleged or claimed, the levy and collection may be enforced by mandamus without a prior judgment at law.

Sec. 751. Irregularity in proceedings cannot be urged as a defense.—Mere questions of irregularity in the manner of conducting the election, or in the proceedings preliminary to delivery of the bonds, will not be sufficient to defeat the issue of the writ. A bona fide holder of bonds may rely upon the presumption that attaches from the fact of their issue and delivery that the formalities of the law which authorizes their issue have been complied with. All parties, however, are bound to a knowledge of the law authorizing the issue of bonds, whether the municipality under the constitution had the authority to issue them. Innocent holders for value, however, are only bound to ascertain whether the bonds could have been properly issued under any circumstances. The decisions of the New York court are contra.

1 Springfield & I. S. R. Co. v. County Clerk, 74 Ill. 27; State v. Mayor, 52 Wis. 423; People v. Mead, 36 N. Y. 224.

2 State v. Mayor, 52 Wis. 423.

3 State v. Mayor, 52 Wis. 423; Riley v. Flagg v. Mayor, 33 Mo. 440; Mayor v. Lord, 9 Wall. 409; State v. Anderson, 8 Baxter, 249.

5 Mayor v. Lord, 9 Wall. 409; Aurora v. Township, 54 Kan. 463; Cassett v. Board of Commissioners, 39 Kan. 505, 18 Pac. Rep. 517.

6 People v. Mead, 36 N. Y. 224.

<br>

### --- Page 0414 ---

§ 752. Payment of money collected.— Mandamus is the appropriate remedy to compel municipal authorities to pay to the bona fide holder of municipal bonds the money that has been collected pursuant to law for the purpose of meeting the payment of such obligations, where such authorities refuse. The reasons given for this result by the New York court may be said to be at least two in number: First, if it be conceded that an action at law would lie, it would not prove an adequate remedy; second, that an action would not lie. It was stated: “Upon the argument of defendants’ counsel, judgment must be recovered against the town, which would have no means of satisfying it without further legislation, while at the same time the money collected specifically to pay such creditor must remain in the hands of the treasurer, no person being by law entitled to take it from him, except these official persons, who refuse to have anything to do with it. . . . A town has only limited corporate powers, and no power whatever to become a debtor for borrowed money, or to provide for the payment of such a debt, except what is conferred by the special act. That act does not contemplate a suit against the town in any event; and the only method which it provides for the payment of the debts which it is thereby authorized to incur was the one which has been pursued nearly to its proper consummation, but which has become ineffectual by the refusal of the defendants to perform the part assigned to them in the detailed arrangements for the satisfaction of the debt.” The court quoted with approval from the opinion in People v. Supervisors, 10 Wend. 363 (that being a proceeding to compel the levy and collection of a specific tax), the following: “If an action lies in this case, then a mandamus should be refused. I think an action would not lie. The statute directs the supervisors to levy and collect the amount of the deficiency. It is a duty imposed upon those officers which should be performed by them; but for this neglect the county, in its corporate capacity, should not be punished; nor does any liability attach to the county to pay the money in any way other than that pointed out in the statute. Should it be thought that the offending supervisors ought to respond personally in damages,

1 People v. Mead, 24 N. Y. 114.

<br>

### --- Page 0415 ---

958 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 752.

which is certainly very questionable, still there is no principle which would graduate the damages to the deficiency which would arise from the mortgage in question, and, for aught the court can know, the money possibly might not be collected in that way. Besides, the law does not contemplate satisfaction in any other manner than by an assessment upon the taxable property of the county. An action, therefore, is not the appropriate and specific remedy.” The court proceeds to state: “If the opinion should be thought to go too far in denying the liability of the county to an action, still the case (People v. Supervisors, 10 Wend. 363) is an authority for holding that, where a particular method of raising money for local public purposes is prescribed by statute, the party entitled to receive it has a right to the full and perfect execution of the power conferred, which may be enforced by mandamus.” The same court subsequently, and in the same case, distinctly held that no action could be maintained on the bonds in question, but this ruling was upon the merits in the mandamus proceedings; that the question whether the bonds were lawfully issued was proper to be considered, even though the bonds were in the hands of innocent purchasers for value. The distinction which the court makes between this and other cases is that here the writ was addressed to the supervisors to compel them to apply to the county treasurer for the money received by him and in his hands, and thus being directed, the town, or such supervisors in behalf of the town, could make the defense of want of power to issue the bonds; while if the writ was directed to the officer who had the money, and whose duty it was to pay it, it having been raised for such specific purpose, he could not question the validity of the bonds.

That the only remedy in such cases is by mandamus is the determination of the United States supreme court. That court states that the legal obligation arising on such a subscription is purely statutory, if the subscription itself be valid and binding, and the statutory obligation is satisfied by the issue and delivery to the railroad company of the bonds of the county in payment of the subscription. On the supposition that the sub-

1 People v. Mead, 36 N. Y. 224; Ross v. Curtiss, 81 N. Y. 606; Murdock v. Aiken, 31 N. Y. 616.

<br>

### --- Page 0416 ---

§ 753.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 959

scription creates a legal obligation for its payment in bonds, the refusal of the commissioners of the county to issue and deliver the bonds, however wrongful, is not a breach of the obligation of the county, which would give rise to an action against it for the recovery of damages. The breach of obligation in such a case would consist simply in the refusal on the part of the commissioners of the county to perform a ministerial duty, the only remedy for which would be a proceeding at law in the name of the railroad company by a writ of mandamus. The writ, if granted in a direct proceeding therefor by a proper judgment, would be directed against the officers of the county, and would command the performance of the specific duty which they had refused to perform, and would give to the company the precise and specific relief to which it would be entitled. It was further held that such relief could not be had in equity.1

It must follow that the federal courts are without jurisdiction to render any relief in such cases; it being settled that the circuit court is without authority to issue writs of mandamus except in aid of a jurisdiction already acquired by that court, and the lack of jurisdiction cannot be supplied by converting the proceeding into a bill in equity.2

The Wisconsin court are of the opinion that where the validity of the bonds is controverted, the writ should be withheld until the plaintiff establishes their validity in the usual method by an action at law; that the relator must show a clear right to the writ; and where this right is thus disputed, the peremptory writ will not issue; that such writ will only issue where, after a hearing upon the rule, no issue of fact appears to be involved. Yet I do not understand that more was intended than that the issue should be determined upon the award of an alternative writ;3 and such is the rule in New York and elsewhere.

Sec. 753. Enforcing subscription—Issue of bonds.— Courts have held that the power is ample in the legislature of a state to compel municipalities, even without their consent, to subscribe to the capital stock of a railway company, whether the

1 Smith v. Bourbon County, 127 U.S. 105; Rosenbaum v. Bauer, 120 U.S. 105, 450.

2 Smith v. Bourbon County, 127 U.S. 105.

3 State v. Mayor, 52 Wis. 423.

<br>

### --- Page 0417 ---

960 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 753.

railway has been constructed or is merely proposed. The reasoning upon which the conclusion reached is based is that railroads concern the public interest as a matter of legal judgment. That the power of the legislature is absolute and not subject to judicial control. The legislature can impose a general tax upon all the property of the state, or a local tax on the property of particular political subdivisions, as counties, cities and towns. The cases in which its powers shall be exercised, and the extent to which the taxation in a particular instance shall be carried, are matters exclusively within its own judgment, subject to the qualification of equality and uniformity in the assessment, and, except as specially restricted, its power of appropriation of the moneys raised is co-extensive with the power of taxation. It may appropriate them to claims which have no legal obligation and are founded only in justice. The power of appropriation which a legislature can exercise over the revenues of the state for any purpose which it may regard as calculated to promote the public good, it can exercise over the revenues of a county, city or town for any purpose connected with their present or past condition. An act of the legislature of a state empowered the board of supervisors of a county to subscribe to the capital stock of a railroad company a sufficient sum to pay the indebtedness of such company, not exceeding $30,000. The road was one that had been completed, and the question was never submitted to the electors or their consent obtained in any manner. It was held the board of supervisors were without discretion in the premises, that the provisions of the law were mandatory, and mandamus would lie to compel subscription to the capital stock of the company and issue its bonds for the stock, and to raise money by taxation and appropriate it to the payment of the bonds.

Ordinarily the law which provides that municipalities may subscribe to the capital stock of enterprises of a public character requires a proposition to be made on the part of the corporation which is constructing the work or operating it and assented to by the municipal authorities.

Napa Valley R. Co. v. Napa County, 30 Cal. 455. See also Pattison v. Supervisors, 18 Cal. 175.

<br>

### --- Page 0418 ---

§ 753.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 961

Municipal officers in respect to the terms of this agreement, or whether any agreement shall be made, are discretionary, and not subject to control by mandamus in the absence of express provisions in the law of a mandatory character. When such an agreement has been made it becomes a valid and binding contract. The law also usually provides for the submission of the question of approval of the contract to the electors, sometimes by obtaining the written consent of a proportionate number thereof or by means of an election, and such approval so obtained is made a condition precedent to the completion of the contract or agreement. When, however, such assent of the electors has been expressed in conformity to the provisions of the law, the municipal authorities are without further discretion in the matter, and mandamus will lie to compel them to subscribe to the capital stock of such companies and to issue and deliver the bonds of the municipality to the extent and in the manner provided by the contract. It follows that the officers whose duty it is to sign, seal or countersign such securities may likewise be compelled to perform their duty. And the fact that an injunction may have been issued out of another court in proceedings to which the company is not a party, restraining the municipal authorities from issuing bonds, will not have the effect to supersede the writ. Where, however, such conditions do not exist, and the relative action of the company and the officers of the municipality does not in terms constitute a contract subject only to ratification by the electors or taxpayers, as the case may be, it has been held that the municipal authorities have a discretion, even after the proposition has been sanctioned by the requisite number assenting to it, to either consummate the contract by making the proper subscription and issuing the bonds or to refuse so to do, and the exercise of that discretion will not be controlled by mandamus.

State v. Jennings et al, 48 Wis. 549; Houston v. The People, 55 Ill. 398; Cincinnati, W. & Z. R. Co. v. Commissioners, 1 Ohio St. 77; People v. Ohio Grove Town, 51 Ill. 192; Osage Valley R. Co. v. County Court, 53 Mo. 156; Ex parte Selma & Gulf R. Co., 45 Ala. 696.

<br>

### --- Page 0419 ---

962 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 753.

Where the act provided in terms that the town may issue its bonds in such manner as may be agreed upon by and between the directors of the railway company and the proper officers of the town, as thereinafter provided, and power was conferred upon the town board of supervisors to submit any proposition of the railway company to a vote of the electors of the town, or to refuse to do so, in the discretion of the board, it was held that the board may dictate the terms of the proposition by refusing to submit it to the electors unless it conforms to the views of the board, and the company is powerless to compel a submission. When the terms of the proposition are settled and agreed upon by the board of supervisors and the company, the contract is made between the town and the company; but it is a contract upon condition precedent, and inoperative until the condition is performed. That condition is a vote of the electors duly taken in favor of the proposition. The electors cannot change the contract. They can only affirm or disaffirm it. If they affirm it the contract becomes operative and binding upon both parties. If they disaffirm it neither is bound. If the contract is thus made and the condition precedent performed, nothing remains but for the chairman of the board of supervisors and town clerk of the town, upon tender of the specified railway stock, to receive it and issue and deliver the bonds according to the contract. This duty is purely ministerial, and mandamus is the appropriate process to compel performance of it, if performance is refused. Speaking with reference to an act containing somewhat similar provisions, the supreme court of Ohio had occasion to state as follows: “The act under consideration is mandatory in some of its provisions, and leaves a discretion in others. It commands the vote to be taken, and, if the subscription is made, it imperatively directs all the subsequent proceedings. But it submits to the discretion of the voters of Clinton county, who are chiefly interested and to bear the burden if assumed, whether the subscription shall be made and the law thus fully executed. It is not the vote that makes, alters, or even approves the law, but it is the law that makes the vote and prescribes everything to be done consequent upon it. . . .

1State v. Jennings et al., 48 Wis. 549,

<br>

### --- Page 0420 ---

§ 753.) MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 963

Subscription having been made with the assent of a majority of the electors of Clinton county, and the county having thereby become absolutely bound by contract, and the commissioners in pursuance of the law having elected to deliver the bonds in payment of the subscription, and afterwards upon demand refusing to do so, . . . mandamus is the proper remedy."

Much, however, is made to depend upon the provisions of the act which authorizes the proceeding. Thus, where the taxpayers of a town in their petition were authorized by the act under which the proceedings were had to impose, as a condition precedent to the subscribing for stock and the delivery of the town bonds, that the road should be located and constructed through the town or a specified portion thereof, the commissioners were subjected to the condition. They could neither issue the bonds nor subscribe for or take stock, or by agreement bind the town, until the condition was fully complied with. They could not substitute an agreement by the railroad company to comply with the condition, or any other agreement on its part for actual compliance, and until such compliance they had no authority whatever to act in any manner. Town bonds having been duly executed and placed in escrow to be delivered to the railway company upon compliance with the conditions prescribed in the petition, this did not operate as a complete contract. The constitutional amendment, having become operative before the conditions were complied with, to the effect that no county, town or village should thereafter give any money or property, or loan its money or credit to or in aid of any individual, association or corporation, nullified all the proceedings. It was stated by the court: "If, when the constitutional amendment took effect, the vote had been duly taken and the requisite vote of the majority of the taxable inhabitants to the raising of the money had been secured, the constitutional amendment prevented further action and rendered the prior proceedings null and void. The authority to create the debt was taken away, and no power existed to borrow money or to issue the bonds of the village for the purposes of the act."

Cincinnati, W. & Z. R. Co. v. Buffalo & Jamestown R. Co. v. Commissioners, 1 Ohio St. 77. Weeks, 69 N. Y. 491.

People v. Trustees, 70 N. Y. 28;

<br>

### --- Page 0421 ---

964 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$§ 754, 755.]

Sec. 754. Statute must be pursued.— Statutes which authorize subscription to the capital stock of railroad companies and kindred enterprises, and the issue of corporate bonds in payment therefor, being in derogation of the common law, are to be strictly pursued; and where the provisions of such laws have not been complied with in essential matters, the municipal authorities not only will not be compelled to subscribe for stock and issue bonds, but may be restrained from doing such acts in case they should attempt so to do. In this respect there is a wide distinction between their duties in respect to issuing bonds, and raising the means to pay them after delivery. Hence if the notice required to be given of the election, and published, is not published the requisite time, the election will be invalid. Thus, where the statute provided that the notice of the special election should be published for at least two weeks previous to the time appointed for such election, it required that two full weeks should intervene between the publication of the notice and the election; and hence where the first publication of the notice was upon April 24th, for a meeting held May 5th, this was not a compliance with the statute, and the election was invalid. The further provision, that the taxable inhabitants may at such meeting, by a majority vote, decide as to raising a sum for the purposes of the act, required a majority of all the taxable inhabitants, not a majority of the votes cast. And if the paper containing the assent of the majority of the taxpayers is not filed and recorded as required by law, mandamus will not issue. In case the election is invalid for any cause, or the act under which it is held is void, or the municipality is indebted beyond the constitutional limit, then the writ will not issue. So it will be withheld if it appear that the corporation, otherwise entitled to receive the bonds, has failed or neglected to comply with the terms and conditions of the agreement on its part to be performed.

Sec. 755. Fraud in enacting ordinance.—It was held by the supreme court of Minnesota that the writ would be denied.

1 People v. Trustees, 70 N. Y. 28; 4 People v. Trustees, 70 N. Y. 28; Alcott v. Robinson, 21 N. Y. 150. People v. Batchellor, 53 N. Y. 128.  
2 People v. Trustees, 70 N. Y. 28.  
3 Essex County R. Co. v. Town, 49 Vt. 143.  
5 People v. Glann, 70 Ill. 232; People v. Town, 88 Ill. 469.

<br>

### --- Page 0422 ---

§§ 756, 757.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 965

where it was alleged that the municipal officers who enacted the ordinance authorizing the issue of bonds were influenced by corrupt motives, and a secret agreement with the company, by which they were to profit individually as a result of their action.

Sxo. 756. Levy of taxes for other purposes than payment of bonds — General principles—Public improvements.—The doctrine declared by the courts with respect to the levy of taxes to pay corporate bonds issued to corporations applies with equal force to other subjects where the duty has been imposed by law. Thus, it has been held that mandamus would lie to compel the levy of a tax to pay for the erection of public buildings where such is specially made the duty of the municipal officers; and also that it would lie to compel the levy of taxes to pay a judgment obtained by a contractor for work in constructing a harbor, where the duty was imposed upon the municipal authorities to levy a tax to pay for such improvements. It is true that in the particular case the contractor had obtained a judgment, and it is quite probable that mandamus would not issue to compel the levy of a tax to pay his claim before having the same determined in an action at law, though it might lie to compel the council to proceed to perform the duty required by law of levying a tax, leaving the amount to be determined within reasonable limits to the discretion of the council, where the amount was not fixed by the provisions of the law.

Sxo. 757. For payment of awards.—The municipal authorities may also be required to levy and collect a tax sufficient to pay the appraised value of lands that the municipality has properly condemned, where the amount has been regularly, definitely and finally fixed. The doctrine of the cases is that, up to the period when the owner had a fixed right to a particular compensation for his property, the municipal authorities might discontinue and abandon the proceedings, but, after such compensation becomes unalterably fixed, property owners have a

1 State v. City of Lake City, 25 Minn. Ann. 141; Commonwealth v. Pittsburgh, 83 Pa. St. 66.  
2 Tarver v. Commissioners, 17 Ala. 527; Watts v. Police Jury, 11 La. N.Y. 56; In re Com’rs of Washington Park, 56 N.Y. 144.

<br>

### --- Page 0423 ---

966 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 758.

vested right to such compensation, and payment may be enforced. If the statute requires confirmation of the report of the commissioners, then prior to that being done the municipality may discontinue the proceedings, but they cannot do so after that event. If the statute provides for an appeal within a stated time, then within that time the proceedings may be discontinued; but not after such period has elapsed. By allowing the period to elapse they will be deemed to have acquiesced in the report and the award must be regarded as a finality, and in the nature of a judgment, which the property owner has a vested right to have assessed and collected according to the terms of the statute. Mandamus will also lie to compel the assessment and levy of a tax to pay for buildings demolished by the municipal authorities in opening a street, when such duty is specially imposed. This result would not follow until the damages thus occasioned had been fixed or determined in a proper proceeding.

Sec. 758. To take proper proceedings to enable contractor to collect.— The writ will also lie to compel the officers of a municipal corporation to take the proper steps, where a street improvement has been ordered, that may be necessary to enable a contractor with the municipality who has made the improvement to collect the amount due him under the contract from the owners of the property benefited thereby. The New York court had held, as we have seen, that in cases of public improvements, directed at the cost of the property benefited, the city was not primarily liable, and in this respect they might not be in accord with the decisions of some other courts. In a recent case it would seem that the contract provided that no payment should be made to the contractor until the cost of the work should have been ascertained and assessed upon and collected from the taxpayers liable to local taxation upon the same, and by reason of such condition it was held that no action would lie against the municipality; that, the relator not having any remedy by action, his proper remedy was,

People v. Common Council, 78 N. Y. 56. City of Greenfield v. State, 118 Ind. 63.

Schoolbred v. Corporation, 2 Bay, 597. 15 N.E. Rep. 241.

<br>

### --- Page 0424 ---

§§ 759-761.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 967

where payment had not been made or the amount collected from such taxpayer, by mandamus to compel the officers of the city having the matter in charge to proceed with their duties as required by law.

Sec. 759. For water rentals duly ascertained.—So the writ will lie to compel a city to levy a tax to pay ascertained water rentals due under a valid contract for a water supply, and where the city had repudiated the contract, if such duty was enjoined by law. The general rule applicable to all such cases is that the amount of the indebtedness must be liquidated and not in dispute to justify proceedings by mandamus.

Sec. 760. Rights of individuals or corporations must be involved.—Another rule equally essential to jurisdiction is that rights of private individuals or corporations must be involved which require protection, and the proceeding is prosecuted for the protection of such rights.

Sec. 761. Other duties relating to public funds—Turning over funds.—The duty with respect to the payment of funds that have been collected for specific purposes, to be used and disbursed by other bodies, is in general ministerial, and therefore, in case of refusal, performance may be compelled by mandamus. Hence the writ will lie to compel county commissioners to turn over road funds in the county treasury by issuing a warrant for that purpose, when, under the law, such money can only be drawn on such a warrant. Such warrant is limited in amount to the extent of funds in the treasury, even though a part of the fund has been illegally diverted to other purposes. It was held, however, in another court that county commissioners would be compelled to pay over, without diminution, the full amount of taxes levied for school purposes.

1 People v. Mayor et al, 144 N. Y. 17 Ala. 527; Watts v. Police Jury, 11  
63; Hunt v. City of Utica, 18 N. Y. 442,  

2 State v. City of Great Falls (Mont.), 49 Pac. Rep. 15.  

3 State Board v. West Point, 50 Miss. 638.  

4 Commonwealth v. Pittsburg, 83 Pa. St. 66; Tarver v. Commissioners, La. Ann. 141,  

5 Commissioners v. Jacksonville, 36 Fla. 196.  

6 Commissioners v. Jacksonville, 36 Fla. 196.  

7 Commissioners v. Gault (Md.), 21 Atl. Rep. 548.

<br>

### --- Page 0425 ---

968 SPECIAL REMEDIES. § 762.

Where a township which had been set off from a county, and which had a balance to its credit on the books of the county treasurer, sought by mandamus to compel the county board of supervisors to provide for its payment, the answer of the board admitting such fact, but denying liability on the ground that the books were not correct, stating in general terms only that taxes had been charged back and some were liable to be enjoined, and that the full amount of taxes was never paid, it was held the answer was insufficient as showing cause against the writ.

NOTE.—This proceeding was commenced in the supreme court, and the court announced the doctrine that the prerogative writ in that court was meant to be a speedy and summary remedy, whose chief value would be destroyed by delays and complications of special pleadings authorized under the practice of alternative or double writs; that in Michigan mandamus proceedings are begun by an order to show cause, and such issues of fact as are introduced by or under the return are disposed of specifically, not by sending down the entire case in the record, but in the same way as are particular issues sent down in chancery cases to be passed upon.

And where an agreement between a city and its contractor for the building of certain sewers provided that no payment should be made until the cost of the work had been collected by assessment and the contractor had received from the proper officer a certificate that the work had been properly completed, and the city officers had refused to collect the assessments, it was held that mandamus would lie to compel the city to perform its duty in this respect. The same rule was applied where county commissioners refused to set aside a designated portion of funds raised for specific purposes where the duty was required by the positive provisions of law; and likewise where town authorities were required to appropriate and pay over a certain per centum of the taxes raised for the support of teachers’ institutes.

Sec. 762. Creation of a public or sinking fund.— Where the creation of a public fund for certain specified purposes is directed by provisions of law, the officers of the municipality

1 Township v. Supervisors, 49 Mich. 454.
2 Humboldt County v. Churchill County, 6 Nev. 80.
3 People v. Syracuse, 144 N. Y. 63.
4 Hall v. Selectmen, 39 N. H. 511.

<br>

### --- Page 0426 ---

§§ 763, 764.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 969

are without discretion, and hence the writ will lie to compel performance of the duty; and it will also lie for like purpose in cases where the municipality is required by law to raise a definite sum of money annually for the support of the poor, even though the amount thus fixed is determined by another board; and where the law provides that, upon adoption of the particular act, it shall be the duty of the appropriate board to appropriate and raise by tax, in the manner that city taxes are assessed and collected, a certain sum to be paid to the trustees of a public library, mandamus will lie to compel the board to levy and collect the tax. There is nothing left to the discretion of the board.

Sec. 763. Highways — Keeping in repair.—It being the duty of supervisors of towns in some states, and in others commissioners of highways, to keep and maintain highways in a suitable state of repair, mandamus will lie to compel the performance of this duty, but will not interfere with the discretion of such bodies or officers as to the character of the repairs that shall be made. An abutting land-owner has sufficient interest in the matter to institute the proceedings. The commissioners of public works of a city may be compelled, where they refuse upon proper application being made, to allow the sidewalk over relator’s vault to be removed in order to make necessary repairs.

Sec. 764. Highways — Obstructions in — Compelling removal of.— While the writ may lie to compel a municipal body clothed with the power to proceed to remove obstructions in a highway, such as fences, houses and other obstructions, yet it will not issue where the existence of the highway is denied. A party aggrieved has an adequate remedy as well as other remedies both at law and under the statute; nor where the mu-

1 People v. Common Council, 45 Barb. 473. 2 Ex parte Common Council, 8 Cow. 358. 3 State v. Board of Finance, 53 N. J. 62, 20 Atl. Rep. 755. 4 Commonwealth v. Doylestown Supervisors, 16 Pa. Co. Ct. Rep. 161 (Pa. Com. Pl.); Perrault v. Les Syn-  
dics Des Chemins Rap. Jud. Quebec, 9 C.C. 8. 512. See sec. 766.  
5 People v. Collis, 45 N. Y. S. 282. 6 People v. City of Bloomington, 63 Ill. 207; Larkin v. Harris, 36 Iowa, 93.  
7 French v. Common Council, 85 Mich. 135.

<br>

### --- Page 0427 ---

970 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 765.

Municipal authorities have a discretion in the matter which they have lawfully exercised. Thus, it will not lie to compel such authorities to remove poles erected under their permission by an electric railway company, nor to compel them to reverse their decision; nor to compel them to remove a railway track from an alley laid and operated under like conditions.

Sxo. 765. Highways — Opening of.— It depends largely upon the wording of a statute whether the duties of supervisors or highway commissioners are mandatory, or whether they have a discretion in respect to opening highways which have been laid out or otherwise declared to be such. Where there is a discretion to be exercised, mandamus will not lie. Thus, where by statute section lines are declared to be highways, and the statute provided that the county board of each county may, whenever the public good requires it, open such roads, etc., it was held that the question of opening such a highway was a matter of judicial discretion on the part of the board. Where in one section of a statute it was provided that the boards of supervisors of two counties should meet, etc., and upon the receipt of the report of the viewers, etc., to declare the road as located by the viewers thereof a public highway, and in the succeeding section of the act it was provided that when the report of the viewers shall have been received and approved, the said boards of supervisors shall declare the road open, it was held, where the boards, or one of them, refused to act upon such a report, that mandamus would lie to compel action; but, under the particular wording of the latter provision, a discretion was vested in the board with respect to the matter of approving the report of the viewers. Hence the court would not direct the manner in which such discretion should be exercised. Again, where an act commanded a municipal body to proceed and grade a certain street, prescribing the way and manner of doing the same and the grade to be adopted, leaving nothing to the discretion of such body except certain incidents to the main work, the duty to proceed to the performance of the 

1 Commonwealth v. West Chester, Throckmorton v. State (Neb.), 31 Pa. Co. Ct. Rep. 542 (Pa. Com. Pl.). N. W. Rep. 232. 

2 State v. City of New Albany (Ind.), 4 People v. Board of Supervisors, 33 Cal, 487.

<br>

### --- Page 0428 ---

§ 766. MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES.

Work in the manner thus directed cannot be evaded on the ground of the discretion which they may exercise as to the incidental details; and the same rule is applied with respect to public improvements in general, where the duty imposed is mandatory. This principle was applied by the Massachusetts court with respect to the construction and completion of a highway, where, by statute, it was made the duty of the commissioners to complete a highway, where the town which may have ordered it neglected to make it within the prescribed time. That by the very terms of the act a public duty was thus imposed upon the commissioners for public reasons, in the exercise of which no discretion was left to them.

Sec. 766. Bridges — Construction and repair of.— The municipal authorities in many cases are vested with a discretion not only as to the character of the structures they propose to erect, but also as to whether the public necessities demand their erection. In some cases their duty in respect to the erection of a bridge is mandatory, and in others becomes of such a character upon the existence of certain conditions. When the duty is discretionary, mandamus will not lie. It will lie, however, when their duty is mandatory. Thus it will lie to compel a county board to appropriate sufficient money to meet one-half the expense of a proposed bridge, when it appears that certain conditions have been complied with whereby the law makes it their duty to make such appropriation; and when it is by law made the duty of county commissioners to repair a defective county bridge on a notice given by three taxpayers, such taxpayers, after giving notice, may, upon refusal of the board to act, maintain proceedings by mandamus to compel the commissioners to make such repairs. In the absence of special legislation, quite generally the duty in respect to when and in what manner bridges shall be constructed is discretionary with the public board, subject, however, to the general duty of keeping them in a reasonably safe condition for public travel. In cases

1 People v. San Francisco, 86 Cal. 595.  
2 Richards v. County Commissioners, 120 Mass. 401. See also People v. Collins, 19 Wend. 56.  
3 Board of Supervisors v. People, 24 Ill. App. 400.  
4 Dutton v. Pankonin, 42 Neb. 804.

<br>

### --- Page 0429 ---

972 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 766.

Involving that question the writ proceeds only to compel action, and does not control discretion as to method. The discretion thus possessed by public bodies is one that they may constantly exercise, even after the work has been commenced, to the extent of abandoning it before completion. Thus, where county commissioners were authorized by special act to build a bridge and to raise the necessary funds for the purpose by successive levies upon the people of the county, and the commissioners, after levying and collecting a part of the taxes necessary, for reasons in their judgment sufficient, abandoned the purpose of building the bridge, and declined to make further levies, it was held that mandamus would not lie at the suit of taxpayers to build the bridge or to make further levies to raise the necessary funds therefor; and where county commissioners, who after adjudging that a town way which the town had refused to lay out is required by the wants of the town, and giving notice that they will lay it out, lay out only a part of it, being of the opinion that the remainder had been rendered unnecessary, since the adjudication, by the construction by the town of another nearly parallel way, it was held the writ would not lie to compel the commissioners to complete it.

Note.— A county board has no authority, under the statutes of Wisconsin, to levy a tax or issue bonds for the purpose of rebuilding a bridge bought by the county, over a navigable river, which has been destroyed, without first submitting the question to the electors; and in the absence of the necessary means for defraying the expense thereof, or of authority to procure such means, mandamus will not lie to compel the rebuilding thereof. State v. Wood County, 72 Wis. 629. The writ, however, will lie in that state in behalf of a town to compel a county board to make an appropriation and levy a tax to pay for one-half the cost of rebuilding a bridge over a navigable river which such town had legal authority to build and maintain, when it refuses to do so upon proper petition of the town. A statute of that state provided for the county bearing a portion of such expense in certain contingencies. State v. Racine County, 70 Wis. 543. The duty is also upon the county to pay a proportion of the expense of a bridge across a dry ravine as well as across a stream of water. State v. Pierce County, 71 Wis. 321. It would seem that ordinarily public boards should be allowed some discretion in the matter of building or repairing bridges. It is true that it is made their duty by law to keep and maintain highways and bridges in a reasonably safe condition, and their discretion as

State v. Commissioners, 31 Ohio St. 211. Hill v. County Commissioners, 4 Gray, 414.

<br>

### --- Page 0430 ---

§§ 767, 768.] | MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 973

to what is required to fulfill this obligation ought not to be interfered with only in a clear case of abuse. It has been held that their duty in respect to keeping highways in repair was absolute, and the lack of funds was no such excuse as would relieve the town from liability for damages in case of injury caused by defects therein. It seems, however, with respect to bridges, that mandamus will not lie to compel the repairing of bridges in the absence of funds that could be devoted to such purpose. Whether a tax levy would be enforced the court did not decide; merely stating that, as the statute limited the amount that could be levied for such purpose in any one year, the board could not be compelled to repair the bridge at their own expense, nor under such circumstances as would preclude them from making the city liable for the cost of the work. State v. Mayor, etc. of Ahnapee, 99 Wis. 322. Where a county acquired by purchase a bridge erected by private parties, under a special charter, which act gave the county an option to purchase after a specified time, and the county soon after its purchase declared the bridge free of toll, and the county thereafter for a period of time maintained such bridge and kept it in repair, after which it refused to longer so maintain it, whereupon mandamus was applied for to compel it to continue to maintain the bridge, the contention being that the county was not under such an obligation, but rather that, upon making the bridge free, that duty immediately devolved upon the cities which it connected and which formed a continuation of one of their public streets, it was held that the duty of the county to keep the bridge in repair resulted from its ownership and the public character of the bridge; that as incident to the right of ownership was the right of control, the latter right necessarily being exclusive. State v. Supervisors, 41 Wis. 28.

Sec. 767. Where duty fixed by judgment of a court.—Where a judgment has been rendered in proper proceedings, which requires a city to alter a highway so as to cross a railway track by a bridge, the duty on the part of the municipal authorities becomes imperatively fixed, and this duty cannot be evaded by a failure to provide the proper means, even though the charter of the city prohibits all city officers and boards from incurring any liability in excess of the sums appropriated by the council.

Sec. 768. Bridge over stream dividing two counties.—Where the law provides, in cases where a bridge shall span any portion of the navigable waters of the state, forming at the crossing the boundary line of two counties, that the expense of maintenance and repair of any such bridge shall be a joint and equal charge on the two counties, etc., such duty is adminis-

1 Williams v. New Haven, 68 Conn. 263,

<br>

### --- Page 0431 ---

974 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 769, 769a.

trative and mandatory, and not discretionary. It is a power conferred in the public interest and for the public benefit, and is therefore imperative. Mandamus will lie upon the relation of a citizen of one of such counties who is put to inconvenience by reason of the non-repair of such a bridge, or failure to rebuild where destroyed, compelling the authorities of such counties to proceed to repair or rebuild such bridge. It is, however, for the boards of supervisors themselves to determine the character of the bridge to be built, so only that it meets the public need. The court cannot control their discretion in determining the particular form and manner in which they shall execute the duty imposed upon them so long as they act in good faith. The same conclusion was reached by another court where it was sought to compel the commissioners of one town to join with those of another to rebuild a bridge that had been constructed by a joint agreement of the two towns, the law making provision for the enforcement of such joint agreement. It was held also that it could not be urged that the commissioners were vested with a discretion as to the kind of bridge to be built, in opposition to the granting of the writ; that such discretion, if abused or made to work injustice, may be controlled by mandamus.

§ 769. Duty must be plain.—Municipal authorities, in the absence of a statute or judgment requiring a particular improvement upon a highway, will not be required to make such an improvement, unless at least the duty to do so is so plain and imperative, having regard to all the considerations which may affect the action of such officers, that the neglect to do it cannot reasonably be justified upon grounds of discretion.

§ 769a. Payment of damages awarded—Abandonment of proceedings.—The question of the use of the writ for the payment of awards has been heretofore referred to, and it is only important here to state that, when damages occasioned by the opening of or improvement of streets has been determined and awarded, the writ will lie to compel the proper steps to pro—

1 People v. Supervisors, 142 N. Y. 271.  
2 State v. Supervisors of Somerset, 44 Minn. 549, 47 N. W. Rep. 163.  
3 People v. Highway Commissioners, 158 Ill. 197.

<br>

### --- Page 0432 ---

§ 7690. MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 975

vide for their payment. At what stage of the proceedings the municipal board can abandon the proceedings has been the subject of some discussion, and it is quite evident from what has been stated in preceding sections that the board, in the exercise of a sound discretion, may abandon them at any time before the award has been made. But, as stated in a leading case, “when the case becomes one between two parties, and the city is one party seeking to take the property of an individual for the public use, it is claiming too much that one of the parties should have the option to reject an assessment which may not happen to meet his views.” It was further said by the court, however, in reply to the argument that the city must have some power of action to provide for a case where the assessment of damages is more than the worth of the improvement, that they were not prepared to say that the city has not the power to abandon the proposed work before the property of individuals is taken.

§ 769. Opening of highway or street.— The question, however, is different when the writ is applied for to compel the opening of a highway or street. It is the rule in such cases that the writ will be denied whenever it appears that the claims of abutting owners, or of those otherwise entitled to damages which were produced by the proceedings taken with reference to the improvement, have not been determined and paid. If not paid, it must appear that the funds have been provided for that purpose, and the neglect or delay is not that of the municipality. It is one of the fundamental rules, or rather a principle which lies at the foundation of the whole proceeding, and as has before appeared, that the writ will not issue to compel the doing of an unlawful act; hence it will not lie to compel the commission of a trespass. Thus, where a highway was laid through the door-yard of a person who had not consented to the proceeding, and the statute forbade the laying out of a highway through any building, or any fixtures or erections for the purposes of trade or manufacture, or any yards or inclosures necessary to the use thereof, it was held such proceedings were without juris-

1 State v. City of Keokuk, 9 Iowa, 438; Harrington v. County Commissioners, 22 Pick. 263.  
2 State v. City of Keokuk, 9 Iowa, 438.  
3 People v. Curyea, 16 Ill. 547; Hall v. People, 57 Ill. 307.

<br>

### --- Page 0433 ---

976 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 769c, 770.

diction and absolutely void; that the words “yards and inclosures” applied to buildings as well as to fixtures for the purposes of trade.

Sec. 769c. Right of appeal in such cases — Effect upon allowance of the writ.— It has been held that the writ will not lie in highway cases of the character mentioned, where there exists a right of appeal or other special remedy. But this is not the rule in all jurisdictions to that extent. If a method of review has been provided by which the unlawful refusal can be fully corrected, and which will result in the subsequent performance of the specific act required, and such delay will not prejudice the right of the relator, then such remedy is sufficient to preclude mandamus.

Sec. 770. Licenses — Duty in reference to.— The granting or refusal of a license to sell intoxicating liquors is quite generally a matter resting in the discretion of the board as to any particular applicant. The law quite generally provides conditions of fitness, and whether an applicant is a proper person to traffic in beverages is a matter which the authorities clothed with the power to grant licenses must determine, and sometimes from extraneous evidence; therefore it is quite generally held that mandamus will not lie to compel the granting of a particular license, unless the refusal is placed on grounds which are clearly arbitrary or illegal. The proper authorities may be compelled by mandamus to convene and revoke a license, where the essential proceedings requisite to granting a lawful license have not been complied with. So the writ will lie to compel such authorities to decide a complaint presented to them for violations of a license by one to whom it has been granted. In many of the states the law confers upon certain

1 Ex parte Clapper, 8 Hill, 458.

2 Commissioners v. The State, 38 Ind. 193; Eva v. Simon, 78 Ga. 120; Swift v. People, 63 Ill. App. 453; McHenry v. Township Board (Mich.), 31 N. W. Rep. 612; City of Louisville v. Kean, 18 B. Mon. 9; People v. Supervisors, 20 Cal. 591; Schlandecker v. Marshall, 72 Pa. St. 200.

4 Re Excise License, 88 N. Y.S. 425; McHenry v. Township Board (Mich.), 31 N. W. Rep. 602; Smith v. Mayor, 1 Gray, 72; Zanone v. Mound City, 103 Ill. 552.

5 State v. Johnson (Neb.), 55 N. W. Rep. 874.

6 People v. Meakin, 56 Hun, 626.

<br>

### --- Page 0434 ---

§§ 771, 772. MANDAMUS To MUNICIPAL BODIES. 977

municipal officers or boards the power to fix the fee within certain limits. Substantially the same considerations apply to the granting of other licenses, as for ferries and the like. Where the proper authorities have acted, their discretion will not be controlled. Where, however, a license has been refused under a mistaken construction of the law, in a case where the applicant presented a clear right, the writ will be allowed.

§ 771. Contracts — Letting of.— The duty of examining proposals, determining the responsibility of bidders and awarding a contract, under a statute which gives the officers the discretion of rejecting any and all bids, is judicial in its nature, and therefore ordinarily not subject to control by mandamus. In such case the mere fact that the statute authorizes such officers to let to the lowest responsible bidder will not have the effect to change the rule. And the same rule applies where the statute is silent upon the subject, but where the notice calling for bids reserves an option to reject any and all bids. Under a statute which provided that “none but the lowest responsible bid shall be accepted; but the board may, in its discretion, reject all the bids, or accept any bid for both labor and material which is the lowest in the aggregate for such improvement or repairs,” it was held that the discretion given by this clause is to reject all the bids; this is the only discretion given. If it is determined to accept a bid, there is no discretion as to which bid is to be accepted. If the lowest responsible bid be rejected, and any other be accepted, the action of the board may be controlled by mandamus without violating the rule that a matter of discretion is not subject to control by proceedings in mandamus.

§ 772. Contracts for public printing.— In the absence of any statute directing or authorizing the letting of a contract for printing and publishing the delinquent tax lists and proceedings of a county board to the lowest bidder, it seems that

Territory v. McPherson et al. 163 Ill. 285; State v. Board of Edu-

cation, 18 Ohio Cir. Ct. R. 603.

Oxford Ferry Co. v. Commissioners, 19 Kan. 293.

People v. Aldridge, 43 N. Y. 8.

Hanlin v. School Dist., 66 Iowa, 69.

Thomas v. Armstrong, 7 Cal. 286.

Ross v. Board of Education, 42 Ohio St. 374.

<br>

### --- Page 0435 ---

978 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 773.

The board have a discretion which they may exercise in letting it to one not in fact the lowest bidder, which cannot be controlled by mandamus. In fact, they are not required to receive competitive bids, but may award the contract independent thereof. Nor will mandamus lie in any event where the contract for the official printing has been let to another and partly performed; nor where the contract has been let to another and is still in force. But the writ will lie to compel the board of supervisors of a county, where it is made their duty by law, to designate the newspapers in the county in which the public laws shall be published. Where, in response to notice given for the reception of bids for apparatus which is covered by patents, bids are received for different kinds, each covered by patents, the board will not be compelled by mandamus to let the contract to any one of such bidders upon the ground that his bid is the lowest.

Sec. 773. Contracts—Performance.—Where under the terms of a contract entered into by a municipality and its contractor for the performance of such work it is provided, or necessarily implied, that the municipality shall do and perform certain things essential to the performance of such work under the contract, and as preliminary thereto, if the municipality refuse, it may be compelled by mandamus. Thus, the writ lies to compel a city engineer, or the city council to require him or other competent person, to furnish lines and levels in accordance with the terms of such contract. The object and purpose of the writ of mandamus, as we have seen, is to enforce the performance of some duty created by law; or, as stated and embodied in the statutes of some of the states, to compel the performance of an act which the law specially enjoins as a duty resulting from an office, trust or station. Hence obligations arising out of contract are not within its reach. Another reason is, that to determine questions arising out of contract

1 State v. Dixon County, 24 Neb. 3 People v. Aldrich, 43 N. Y. S. 99. 106; State v. Lincoln County (Neb.), 4 People v. Supervisors, 56 N. Y. 249. 53 N. W. Rep. 147; Maxwell v. Supervisors (Cal.), 32 Pac. Rep. 443. >State v. Board of Education, 14 Ohio Cir. Ct. Rep. 15.

2 Clark v. City of West Bay City (Mich.), 70 N. W. Rep. 367. State v. Bell, 49 La. Ann. 676.

<br>

### --- Page 0436 ---

§ 774] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 979

relations the law always provides remedies which are deemed amply sufficient and adequate. Thus, the writ was refused where it was sought to compel a turnpike company to contribute three-fourths of the expense necessary to keep a bridge in repair, pursuant to the terms of a contract entered into between a county and the turnpike company, upon the ground stated, that the writ could not be made use of to enforce a contract obligation. The supreme court of Vermont, recognizing this distinction as well as the want of power in the court, though it was conceded that the court had all the power to issue the writ that was exercised by the court of king’s bench, refused the writ on the ground that it could not be used to compel a mere service, and that its proper use was to compel the discharge of duties imposed by law, as distinguished from duties imposed by contract merely. Thus, where a legislative committee, appointed under a joint resolution to inquire, among other things, whether money had at any time been paid by certain parties to any member of the legislature for the purpose of influencing legislation, with power to send for persons and papers, but without authority conferred by the resolution to employ a clerk, employed a stenographer to take down the testimony given before the committee, it was held that the writ would not issue to compel the stenographer to furnish the committee a transcript of his minutes that it might be made a part of its report. It thus appears that the mere fact that the contract is one made on the one part by public officers, and in behalf of the public, does not have the effect to take it out of the operation of the rule.

Sec. 774. Reversing action taken— When may and may not be compelled.— The rule being as often herein repeated, that the writ does not lie to control discretion unless in cases of clear abuse, it follows that, where the municipal board has acted in a particular matter, mandamus will not lie to compel it to reverse its decision, unless its action is clearly in violation of law. The rule is often stated broader than this, and to the effect that action once taken will not be reversed; yet it

State v. Zanesville and Mayville Turnpike Co., 16 Ohio St. 308.
Bailey et al. v. Oviatt, 47 Vt. 627.

<br>

### --- Page 0437 ---

980 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$ 774.

is clear that such is a misstatement. It is true that the court will not compel the reversing of an act already done which involved the exercise of discretion in the doing of it, or to change the judgment of those who did the act. This follows, however, not from any distinct rule other than the general one that the discretion of a court officer or body will not be controlled by mandamus; and this rule is of equal force after as well as before the discretion is exercised; and the same result follows with respect to compelling further action in the same matter upon the same question once determined. It is not true, however, that where the action taken was in violation of law, or the decision made was contrary to law, the decision or determination thus made will not be reversed, and the party not compelled to do the act in a different way or to reach a different conclusion. In fact the more frequent use of the writ is to accomplish such a result. There is no such rule as that above referred to. It is merely a result of the application of well settled rules. If a court erroneously dismisses an appeal, if there is no other adequate remedy mandamus lies to compel the changing of such a decision and to reinstate the appeal. If a court decides that it is without jurisdiction in a pending matter, the writ lies to compel the changing of such decision and to the resuming of jurisdiction, the exercise of which was suspended only. So if an inferior tribunal, such as a board of supervisors, should in the exercise of their discretion determine upon evidence that a ferry had not been properly kept, and for that reason should grant the license to another, there would be no authority to compel them to change their determination; but if they act under a mistake of law in assuming that the license granted was void or had been surrendered, then mandamus would lie to correct the error or to compel the recognition of the license, which in effect would be to compel them to change their determination. It has appeared in preceding pages that boards of canvassers, when they proceed unlawfully in the matter of canvassing votes and in determining the result of an election, may be compelled, if they have adjourned, to reconvene and retrace their steps and perform their acts in a different way and arrive at a different result; and it has also  

1 Thomas v. Armstrong et al., 7 Cal. 286,

<br>

### --- Page 0438 ---

§§ 775-778.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES.  

It appeared that even in cases where the exercise of discretion was involved, if there was abuse in its exercise, the writ would lie to compel the discretion to be properly exercised.

Sec. 775. Subject continued—Fixing water rates.—Many cases rightfully decided have, however, been stated to have been so decided by application of such alleged rule; in other words, the decision has been placed upon the ground that a change of determination could not be compelled. Thus it was stated that ordinarily, where the duty is imposed upon municipal officers of fixing the water rates, it involves the exercise of discretion; and hence, where such officers have exercised their discretion by duly passing a proper order, mandamus will not lie to compel them to change their judgment or take further action. In fact, however, the rule involved, and which is discussed in the opinion, is that only which relates to the control of discretion where it has been exercised.

Sec. 776. Subject continued—Award of damages.—It was stated by the supreme court of Massachusetts that mandamus would not lie to compel county commissioners or the mayor and aldermen of the city of Boston to revise their decision upon the merits of the claim of an owner of land for damages sustained by the construction of a railroad. What the court decided was that it is not the province of the court, on the application for a mandamus, to revise the decision of such a tribunal upon the merits; in other words, that a determination of the merits involved the exercise of judgment and discretion, which the court was without power to control by mandamus.

Sec. 778. Subject continued—Location of county seat.—It has also been stated that a board of supervisors, after having refused an application for calling an election for location of a county seat, cannot be compelled by mandamus to reconsider their action; yet by this nothing more was meant than that, in so far as the granting or refusing of such application rested in the judgment or discretion of such board, the manner of its

Zanone v. Mound City, 103 IL 3 Smith v. Mayor and Aldermen, 1552. Gray, 72.

Jacobs v. Supervisors, 100 Cal. 121. State v. Nemaha County, 10 Neb. 82.

<br>

### --- Page 0439 ---

982 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 780, 781.]

exercise was exclusive. If, in considering such application, they act illegally in erasing names from a petition, they may, as we have seen ante, be compelled to restore them, and then call an election if the petition is then sufficient.

Sec. 780. Subject continued—President of city council.—Where the president of a city council illegally declares a resolution of the common council adopted, when in fact it was lost, by not having received in its favor the requisite number of votes, mandamus will lie to compel him to reverse his decision and declare it lost. His decision in such matter does not involve the exercise of discretion, but is a purely ministerial act.

Sec. 781. Board of supervisors.—Where the board of health of a municipality necessarily incurs expenses in providing for the safety of its inhabitants by removing, etc., any person infected with smallpox or other sickness dangerous to public health, such person, his parents or other persons liable for his support being unable to pay them, then under a statute of Michigan such expenses are made a charge against the county, which it is the duty of the board of supervisors to allow; and where a board of supervisors summarily rejected the claim of a private individual which had been thus incurred, adjudged and fixed by the board of health, without investigation, and peremptorily refused to hear proof of the facts, it was held that mandamus would issue to compel the allowance of the claim. It was said it was the duty of such board to institute inquiry and to hear proof touching the ability of the patient or other person liable therefor to pay such expenses, and, if found unable, then to allow the claim and make provision for it. Further, that the right of appeal had been taken away by statute. It seems to the writer that the mandate of the writ should have been to compel the board to proceed under the law to determine the question of fact, rather than to allow the claim without having made such investigation. See contra, as to the power of a legislature to vest in one municipal cor-

1 Tenant v. Crocker, 85 Mich. 328; 3 See also Board of Supervisors v. Plant, 42 Ill. 824; McCloughry v. Supervisors, 46 Ill. 356.
People v. Brinkerhoff, 68 N.Y. 259.
2 People v. Supervisors, 3 Mich. 475.

<br>

### --- Page 0440 ---

§§ 782, 783. MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 983

poration power to incur a debt which should be payable by another.

Sec. 782. Duty where amount of claim has been fixed by other officers.— Where the constitution of a state provided that the board of supervisors of a county shall have the exclusive power to prescribe and fix the compensation for all services rendered for and against their respective counties, and the sum so fixed shall be subject to no appeal, it was held that such provision did not embrace those cases where charges have been laid on counties simply as a fair way of apportioning the public debt, and where no benefit accrues to them in their corporate capacity, and it did not embrace the salary or compensation of police clerk of a city, as he was in no sense a county officer. By the provisions of a statute, the common council of a city were empowered to appoint a clerk of the police court and to prescribe his salary, which was made payable out of the county treasury, to be allowed, raised and paid by the board of auditors as other county charges. The county auditors refused to pay the salary of such officer thus fixed at the sum of $1,000. It was further held that mandamus would lie to compel payment; that where the law has in such cases pointed out any other mode of adjustment or regulation of compensation or salaries than that by the county boards, the latter have no right to review, but must allow and pay them as thus adjusted.

Sec. 783. Meeting of board or council — Compelling.— Mandamus will lie to compel the county or town board, or city or village council, to meet, levy taxes and attend to other legislative business, when by reason of a neglect of duty in failing to hold meetings and transact the regular business of the corporation the government of the municipality is practically suspended. Yet it was held that the writ would not lie to compel aldermen to attend meetings of the common council and to perform their general official duties regularly. In the latter case, if a member of the board neglects to attend, the board

1 People v. Mayor, etc., 51 Ill. 18; Commonwealth v. Town Council, People v. Solomon, 51 Ill. 88; Hessler v. Commissioners, 88 Ill. 105.
2 People v. Auditors, 13 Mich. 233. 
3 Lack. Leg. N. 234 (Com. Pl.).
4 People v. Whipple et al., 41 Mich. 548.

<br>

### --- Page 0441 ---

984 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 784, 785.]

itself, even though less than a quorum, have the means of compelling attendance. When, however, the entire board neglect or refuse to attend, then there is no other means of compelling attendance and action than by mandamus.

§ 784. Ordinances — Enforcement of.— Mandamus will lie on the relation of a private party to compel city authorities to enforce a certain class of ordinances. Thus, where it is made the duty of certain officers, by the city charter, to enforce the Sunday laws, but the method is left to their discretion, the writ will not lie to compel such officers to arrest without warrant and prosecute persons for violating such laws. It will lie, however, to compel the council to rescind and vacate an order or resolution passed by them directing the police not to interfere with the sale of liquor on Sunday; and when the law prescribes the method the officers will be compelled to pursue it. It is doubtful if a mere policeman is such an officer as can be compelled to execute a particular process.

§ 785. Production of books and papers— Corporation may institute proceedings to compel.— In proceedings other than those pending in courts of justice, it frequently becomes necessary that the books in which the corporate affairs of a municipality are kept should be produced. There can be no question but that a municipal board has the right to inspect such books, and may order the custodian thereof to produce them upon all proper occasions, and that such duty is subject to control by mandamus. In legal proceedings such books may be reached and produced by means of a subpoena duces tecum or by notice provided by statute. So where committees are selected to examine the accounts of certain officers, the delivery of the books and records pertaining to their office and in their possession may be compelled by mandamus for the purpose of inspection. The application must be made by or in the name of the municipality, and not by or in the name of the committee.

1 State v. Francis (Mo.), 8 S. W. 3 Bates v. Overseers of Poor, 14 Gray, 163; Perkins v. Weston, 3 Cush.
2 Anon., 2 Barn. K. B. 235; City of Keokuk v. Merriam, 44 Iowa, 432; Walter v. Belding et al., 24 Vt. 658,

<br>

### --- Page 0442 ---

Bde  
§§ 786, 787. MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES, 985

Sec. 786. Delivery of bonds — Corporation may institute proceedings to compel.— Where by a special provision of law a board of water commissioners was created and authorized to issue bonds and sell the same to raise money for the purpose of paying for a public improvement, and the bonds having been duly signed and in the possession of the treasurer of the town, who refused to deliver them to the board, claiming they had no right to them, and that they could only be delivered by him to such persons as should pay to him the money for which they were sold, it was held that mandamus would lie to compel delivery to such board of such bonds, as under the act they were clearly entitled thereto.¹

Sec. 787. Correction of record — When may be compelled. Where the clerk of a municipality in the discharge of his duties makes an error in the record of proceedings, it has been held that, upon the relation of a party interested or affected, he will be compelled by mandamus to correct the record so made. In other cases it has been held that, the record being subject to the control of the municipal board, it can be corrected only in some proceeding instituted by such board.² It has been held in one case that the president of a common council, who had declared a resolution carried, while in fact it had not received a sufficient number of votes in its favor to carry it, might be compelled by mandamus to reverse his decision and declare it lost; and in another that the writ would lie, directed to the chairman of the board of supervisors and the clerk, requiring them to convene the board and requiring the chairman to declare a resolution carried, which he had erroneously declared lost, and the clerk to so record it; that while the writ might be directed to the board, it was not error that it was not so directed.³ Where commissioners of a county are authorized by law to make changes in the records of the office of county officers, such as changing the manner and form of all volumes of index in the office of register of deeds, they have the right of access to and of the use of the records for such purpose, and a right to use a portion of the office where the records are kept for the

¹ Pearsons v. Ranlett, 110 Mass. 118.  
² Tennant v. Crocker, 85 Mich. 328.  
³ Farrell v. King, 41 Conn. 448, 5 People v. Brinkerhoff, 68 N. Y. 259, Wigginton v. Markley, 52 Cal. 411.

<br>

### --- Page 0443 ---

986 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$§ 788, 789.

purpose of performing their duties; and where such right and privilege is refused by the officer having custody of such records, mandamus will issue to compel him to permit the commissioners, or such persons as they may employ to perform such work, to exercise such right and privilege! The writ will also lie to compel boards of canvassers or other officers to deposit documents or records in any particular office, where such act is required by law.

Sec. 788. School boards.— The statutes in some of the states provide for an appeal from the decision of a school board to the county or state superintendent of schools, and where such statutes exist, quite generally it is held that such right of appeal precludes the remedy by mandamus. However, it is held that, where the school board refuses to act, mandamus will lie to compel it to proceed; and will also lie, notwithstanding such right of appeal, in any case wherein the jurisdiction and powers of directors or school officers are brought in question.

Sec. 789. Matters not within their discretion. Where a statute provides for the formation of a new school district upon it being made to appear to the board that certain prescribed conditions exist, the board are without discretion and may be compelled by mandamus to make the proper order. And where at a meeting properly held the electors have voted to construct a school-house and voted a certain amount of tax to be raised for such purpose, mandamus will lie to compel the directors to proceed with the erection of such building. The writ will also lie to compel the proper distribution of school books, where such duty is enjoined by law; and to permit children, on the application of their parent or guardian, to continue the use of such books as have been legally selected, where

1 Hawes v. White, 66 Me. 305. 5 Trustees of Schools v. People, 121 Ill. 552. See also Hancock v. District Township, 78 Iowa, 550.
2 McDiarmid v. Fitch, 27 Ark. 106. 
3 Albin v. Board of Directors, 58 Iowa, 77; Case v. Blood, 71 Iowa, 632, 33 N. W. Rep. 144; District Township v. Independent Dist. (Iowa), 34 N. W. Rep. 472.
4 Perkins v. Board of Directors, 56 Iowa, 476.
6 Benjamin v. District Township, 50 Iowa, 648. See Peters v. Warner, 18 Iowa, 335.
7 State v. Haworth, 122 Ind. 462; State v. School Directors, 74 Wis. 21.

<br>

### --- Page 0444 ---

§ 789.] MANDAMUS TO MUNICIPAL BODIES. 987

the action of the board in attempting to make a change is illegal; also, to compel the reinstatement of a pupil excluded under a rule of the board, which is void for want of power in the board to adopt it. Ordinarily the parent has the right to determine what studies, among those which are prescribed by the school board, his child shall pursue. His authority in this respect is paramount to that of the board or teacher; and where a pupil is excluded from a public school on the ground solely that she obeys the direction of her parent in this respect, mandamus will lie to compel her restoration.

The use of the writ for the purpose of reinstatement of a pupil suspended or expelled from the public schools has been quite frequent. It will be seen at a glance that in such cases there is no other adequate remedy. An action for damages is not an adequate remedy. Besides, the damages are of that character that they cannot be even approximately ascertained. The only other objection that can be urged is that, in the matter of the admission, suspension or expulsion of a pupil, the board necessarily exercises judgment and discretion, which, by familiar rules, is not subject to review by mandamus. If such rule was inflexible and arbitrary there might be much of force in this objection; but, as we have seen, it is not, but rather that it yields to exceptional cases and conditions. Courts have intimated that where a board or officers have exercised their discretion, and determined that the pupil is for any sufficient reason unfit to attend the public schools, and such reason as well as the determination appears, that then such determination will not be controlled; but where the action of the board or officers is arbitrary, or without any reason other than they will it that such pupil is unfit, then the writ will issue; the argument being that in such a case the discretion which the respondents had power to exercise was not in fact exercised. Such was the reasoning of the supreme court of Nebraska in a recent case. The policy of the law is that children between certain ages shall be kept at school. This is a matter of public

1. State v. Board of Education, 35 W. Rep. 393; Morrow v. Wood, 35 Ohio St. 368. Wis. 59; Trustees v. People, 87 Ill. 303. 
2. Perkins v. Board of Directors, 56 Iowa, 476. 
3. State v. School Dist. (Iowa), 48 N. 
4. Jackson v. State, 77 N. W. Rep. 662.

<br>

### --- Page 0445 ---

988 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 790.]

as well as individual concern; so much so that compulsory laws in this respect have been enacted in many states; and I doubt very much whether that public policy is satisfied by leaving it to the discretion of the ordinary school board or school teacher to determine conclusively whether a child shall receive education in the public schools. A board of health cannot determine conclusively a nuisance. Whether what is so alleged is a nuisance must be determined upon some proper proceeding. Whether a pupil is unfit to attend the public schools—so to speak, in one sense, a nuisance—should be the subject of proof, and ultimately to be determined by some proper tribunal; and it should require not only convincing proof, but to the effect that the pupil is so degenerate that either he is beyond reform, or that the welfare of the other pupils is endangered by his presence.
