As stated by a learned judge: “It is the policy of the law to encourage every person of school age to be a constant attendant at school, and no person should be deprived of this right except for adequate cause. The right to expel a pupil for disorderly conduct or other adequate cause no doubt exists in the school board, but it should not be exercised until other means have been adopted and failed, and only as a means of preserving good order in the schools.”+

§ 790. Colored children—Duty in respect to admission of.—In the absence of a statute providing other and equal facilities for the education of colored children, they are entitled to enjoy the privileges of and receive the benefits afforded by the public schools, and in case such privileges and benefits are denied them by those in charge of public schools, mandamus will lie to admit them as pupils.2 Where, however, separate schools have been established pursuant to a statute which requires the education of colored children therein, the writ will not issue to compel the reception of such children in the schools established for white children.3 But it seems that independent of such a statute school boards have no discretionary power to establish separate schools.4

1 State v. School District, 31 Neb. 552.  
2 People v. Board of Education, 18 Mich. 400; Clark v. Board of Education, 24 Iowa, 266; State v. Duffy, 7 Nev. 842.  
3 Ward v. Flood, 48 Cal. 36.  
4 Clark v. Board of Directors, 24 Iowa, 266.

<br>

### --- Page 0446 ---

§§ 791, 792. Manpamus To muNICIPAL BopIEs. 989

Ssec. 791. Reinstatement of teachers.— It has been held that the writ will also lie to compel the board to reinstate a teacher whom they have removed without having the authority. The fact that another teacher has been placed in the position from which the teacher originally entitled thereto has been unlawfully removed cannot affect the right of the removed teacher to a mandamus to regain the position. The position is not in the nature of an office within the rule that mandamus cannot be used to regain an office claimed by or in possession of another. The supreme courts of Nebraska and Wisconsin are in accord with the supreme court of California upon the proposition that a teacher is not a public officer. They hold, however, that his rights rest simply upon contract, and for that reason mandamus will not lie to compel the district school board to permit him to take charge of the school under his contract. The decision in California was based upon the provisions of a statute.

Ssec. 792. Payments out of school funds.— Where the statute provides that payments from the school fund of a city shall be made by the city and county treasurer only on drafts drawn on him by the board of education, mandamus in favor of one who had fully performed all the conditions of a contract entered into with the board would lie to compel the delivery of a draft for the amount due. The reason given is that the only action he could maintain would be against the individual members of the board for not drawing the draft, which would not be as convenient, beneficial and effective as the writ. The writ, however, will not lie upon the relation of members of the school board to compel one of their number who refuses to sign a warrant for the payment of teachers’ wages to do so. They have no interest sufficient to entitle them to the writ. Nor will it lie to compel a school board, which, after appointing one person as treasurer, rescinds its action and appoints another, who goes into possession, to revoke its action. The remedy,

1 Gilman v. Bassett, 33 Conn. 298; 3 State v. Smith, 49 Neb. 755; Board v. Morley, 5 Lea, 691; Kennedy v. Board of Education, 82 Cal. 480.  
4 Raisch v. Board of Education, 81 Cal. 542.  
2 Kennedy v. Board of Education, 82 Cal. 480.  
5 State v. James, 14 Wash. 82.  

<br>

### --- Page 0447 ---

990 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 793.

if any, is by quo warranto. Nor to compel such a board to make a special levy of taxes for the payment of orders which are illegal by reason of having been issued without the question of contracting the indebtedness represented thereby having been submitted to the people, such proceeding being required by a provision of the constitution of the state. In Wisconsin, provision is made by law for maintaining high schools by joint districts, so called; that is, school districts or towns may join in supporting such a school. Where one of such districts refuses to provide its proper proportion of the funds required for such purpose, it was held that mandamus to compel the levy and collection of the tax to raise such proportion was the only remedy. That neither an action in equity or at law would lie. No case is presented calling for damages, but one calling for the enforcement of official duty.

Sec. 793. Teachers.— A teacher in free public schools is not an officer, but an employee, and therefore cannot be compelled by mandamus, on the relation of a publisher of school books, to use his books, although the use of such books has been prescribed by statute. The president of a board of directors may be compelled to sign a contract for the employment of a teacher where the latter has been legally employed by the directors. So the proper authority may be compelled to issue a certificate to a teacher who has complied with the laws in respect to examination as to fitness, and who has been declared to be qualified. The duty in such case is mandatory, and the officer without discretion. And the superintendent of schools or examining board may be compelled to examine an applicant for the position of teacher, and if found qualified to so determine and certify. The profession of teaching is a property right. The courts, however, can only compel action. The duties of such officers involve the exercise of a discretion.

1. People v. Trustees of Schools, 42 Ill. App. 60.  
2. Dempsey v. Board of Education (W. Va.), 208 E. Rep. 811.

4. Heath v. Johnson, 36 W. Va. 782.  
5. Independent District v. Rhodes (Iowa), 55 N. W. Rep. 524.  
6. Cruse v. McQueen (Tex. Civ. App.), 25 S. W. Rep. 711.  
7. Kell v. Rudy, 15 Pa. Co. Ct. R. 309 (Pa. Com. Pl.); Bailey v. Ewart, 52 Iowa, 111.

which the courts cannot control; and where upon examination a certificate has been refused, the courts cannot compel one to issue. A school teacher who has obtained, by proper proceedings upon appeal, from the state superintendent a favorable decision upon the questions upon which her right to compensation for teaching depended, is entitled to a writ of mandamus to enforce such payment.”

Sec. 794. Writ only lies to enforce public duties or those arising from position of trust.—It has been stated in another part of this work that mandamus does not lie to persons acting in their individual capacity, or with respect to the performance of purely individual duties. Other remedies exist which are considered ample to enforce either public or private rights against an individual, and hence there is no occasion for a resort to proceedings by mandamus. The writ only issues to compel the performance of duties that are in their nature public, or such as the particular person or party is under obligations to perform by reason of some relation or position of trust.

Sec. 795. Private corporations—Nature of duties.—All corporations are in some respects public; that is, they derive
63

<br>

### --- Page 0451 ---

994 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 796.]

their very existence and franchise from the public, and owe to the public the performance of certain duties, which the public functionaries may enforce. They are private in other respects, but, with respect to the performance of duties which relate to the private management or operation of the corporate affairs or enterprise, the officers occupy a position analogous to that of trustees as respects the members of the corporation, and their duties as such may ordinarily be enforced by mandamus. The general rules which we stated at the outset of the discussion of this particular subject are applicable where the performance of duty by private corporations or their officers is sought to be enforced. Two things must concur: First, a specific legal right; and second, the absence of an adequate legal remedy to authorize the issue of the writ of, or the institution of proceedings by, mandamus to enforce the performance of duty.

§ 796. Discretion—Control of.—It is frequently stated that the discretion vested in officers of private corporations is not subject to control, and by implication at least that the rule applies with the same force and to the same extent as to public corporate officers. This, however, is incorrect. They occupy a different relation to the public than public officers. In some sense their interests are antagonistic. Their object and purpose is not the public good, but that of the corporation; and it would be strange indeed if their discretion was beyond public review by the courts. Hence it is that with respect to public duties, or those which they owe to the public, the courts ultimately determine whether they have been performed in the manner and to the extent required. Courts will not interfere with the internal regulations of private corporations in the enforcement of their rules, unless under very peculiar circumstances of substantial wrong. The reason given is, that members having grievances arising therefrom, for which they are entitled to redress, can proceed by action.

¹ State v. Patterson, etc. R. Co., 142 Vroom, 505; People v. State Ins. Co., 19 Mich. 392; People v. Cummings, 72 N. Y. 433; Price v. Riverside L. & L. Soc., 47 Mich. 648; Morton v. Timkin, 48 N. J. L. 87; Hargrell v. La Fayette Benev. Soc., 56 Cal. 481.

<br>

### --- Page 0452 ---

§§ 797-799.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 995

Sec. 797. Remedy by action — Adequacy of.— A remedy by action is not in all cases sufficient to supersede the writ, but rather that the court, notwithstanding such a remedy, may, under peculiar circumstances, consider that such a remedy is not sufficiently prompt, or for other reasons does not afford an adequate remedy. In other words, the court may exercise a discretion in granting or refusing the writ. In fact, it has been said that mandamus, being a discretionary writ, will not usually lie to settle the controversies of private corporations, where the facts are not important, on public grounds, or would not justify the interference of the court if corporate authority did not exist.

Sec. 798. Writ does not lie to determine liability upon or to enforce payment of contracts.— “Mandamus ordinarily does not purport to adjudge or decide any right. It is rather in the nature of an award of execution than of judgment. It is the mode of compelling the performance of acknowledged duty, or enforcing an existing right, rather than deciding what that right or duty is.” Hence the writ will not lie to enforce the payment of contracts, or to determine the question of liability, nor to compel a private corporation, such as an insurance company or benevolent association, to make an assessment upon its members to pay a contract claim.

Sec. 799. Public duties — Rule.— The distinction between public duties and those that are private may not in all cases be clearly drawn. There are many duties that are imposed upon private corporations that partake of the nature of both public and private. Many such cases arise with respect to railroad corporations and other corporate common carriers. In fact, though they are classed as private corporations, they are essentially quasi-public. It may be stated as a general rule that where, by provisions of their charter or by force of general law, private corporations are required to do and perform specific acts in a specific manner, mandamus lies to compel the performance of such duties.

1 Lamphere v. Grand Lodge A. O. U. W., 47 Mich. 429.  
2 Burland v. Mutual Benefit Ass’n, 47 Mich. 424. See sec. 773.

<br>

### --- Page 0453 ---

996 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 800, 801.

Sec. 800. Railroads — Construction of fences — Transportation of freight, etc.— Operation of road.— Hence mandamus is the proper proceeding to compel a railroad company to construct its fences in compliance with the provisions of the statute; also to compel it to receive and transport freight upon such terms as are reasonable and usual, and otherwise to perform its duties as a common carrier, and generally to exercise its franchise. A leading case illustrating this principle arose in Connecticut. By the provisions of the charter of the Hartford & New Haven Railroad Company, it was required to build and operate a railroad from Hartford to the navigable waters of New Haven harbor. The road was so constructed and operated as to connect with steamboats running from such terminus to New York. The railroad company subsequently constructed a track diverging from such original track, and thereafter ran its trains to a station in New Haven, forming a railroad connection there, and discontinued the use of the original track for passenger trains from such diverging point to tide-water. It was held that mandamus would lie to compel the company to run passenger trains to the original terminus of the road at tide-water.

Sec. 801. Water companies — Supplying water.— Another leading case arose in California. There a canal company was organized, pursuant to general provisions of law, for the purpose of taking and conveying water for irrigation, milling, manufacturing or other purposes, and the questions were as to the character of their duties and the power of the courts with respect to enforcing their performance. It was said that “every corporation deriving its being from the act above cited has impressed upon it a public trust — the duty of furnishing water to all those who come within the class or community for whose alleged benefit it has been created. Every such corporation may exercise in behalf of the public the power of eminent

Ohio St. R. Co. v. People, 121 Ill. 483.
State v. Hartford, etc. R. Co., 29 Conn. 538.
People v. New York, etc. R. Co., 28 Hun, N.Y. 261.
People v. Albany, etc. R. Co., 24 Conn. 538.
Price v. Riverside L. & L Co., 56 Cal. 431.

<br>

### --- Page 0454 ---

§ 801.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 997

domain; and no man or company of men, incorporated or otherwise, can take the property of a citizen for his or their exclusive benefit. The power — in its nature a public power — and the public duty are correlative. The duty exists without any express statutory words imposing it wherever the public use appears. Nor is it necessary, as the case is presented, to deny that a corporation may be formed to furnish with water for purposes of irrigation a particular community or even a particular territory, provided the territory is not within the exclusive occupation of the corporation itself.”

The question to what extent the courts will regulate the acts of quasi-public corporations, such as water companies exercising franchises, has been quite fully discussed of late, and the consensus of opinion seems to be that such companies may be compelled by mandamus to strictly perform their duties and obligations that are of a public character. The supreme court of New York recently had under review several questions of importance relating to the duties of water companies and the power of the courts to compel their performance. One involved the failure to furnish pure water, and the other that of reasonable terms. The usual objection was made, that the parties had an adequate remedy at law, and hence mandamus would not lie. The court, in disposing of this question, restated the language used in a prior case (In re McGrath, 56 Hun, 76), as follows: “The respondent is a corporation undertaking in view of its franchises of a public character, and expressly charged with the duty to perform a certain service to all who pay certain fees or rates therefor, and can therefore be compelled by mandamus to perform such service.” In that case it was held that mandamus would issue to compel a water company to furnish water. In the present case it was held that the obligation on the part of such a company was to furnish pure water, and that the writ was equally available to compel the performance of this duty.

With respect to the question whether the writ could issue to regulate or control the charges which should be made, it was urged that the relator must first pay the charges, whatever the company may fix, and seek redress by a suit at law to recover the excessive amounts exacted from him. The court

<br>

### --- Page 0455 ---

998 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 801a.

stated that they knew of no good reason for holding such a rule. They refer to the doctrine often applied where courts, at the instance of such quasi-public corporations, have enjoined the enforcement of laws prescribing that they should render service at grossly inadequate rates or prices, and conclude that it is applicable to all parties. They state also that there is no more difficulty in determining reasonable rates in a mandamus proceeding than in an equity action. That the estimate of water rates by taps or openings, as distinguished from consumption, may or may not be unreasonable. That question can be determined upon the trial. That the same is true of the requirement that the water rate should be paid in advance. That if the computation of the water rates by openings should be held unreasonable, then payment in advance would seem equally unreasonable, because the amount the consumer is liable to pay is definitely ascertained and fixed. If, however, such method of fixing the water rates should be held unreasonable, then the provision that payment should be made in advance would also be unreasonable, for the amount of the consumer’s liability would be undetermined. Still, in the latter case, we think it plain that the appellant is not bound to sell water on credit, but may require a reasonable deposit to secure any bill the consumer may incur.1 The principle here involved was recognized in a very recent case by the supreme court of Illinois. They state it to be that, where a corporation accepts a public franchise imposing certain duties to the public in return for rights conferred, the performance of such duties may be compelled by mandamus.2 Thus an obligation imposed upon a street railway company to demand no greater rates between certain points than between certain other points is a provision for the protection of the public against unjust discrimination, and hence any citizen is entitled to maintain mandamus for its enforcement.3

§ 801a. Individuals — Operating public franchise — The same rule applies to individuals who seek to distribute water

1 People v. Suburban Water Co., 56 N. Y. Supp. 364, Ill.
2 People v. Suburban R. Co., 178 Ill. 394.
3 Same case.

<br>

### --- Page 0456 ---

§§ 802, 803.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS—PUBLIC DUTIES. 999

by virtue of a franchise. They cannot, without reasonable cause, shut off the water from one of the inhabitants of the municipality who is using the same at a fixed rate. The use of water appropriated for sale, rental or distribution is a public use, and the right to collect rates or compensation for the use of the water furnished to the inhabitants of a municipality is a franchise which can only be exercised by authority of and in the manner prescribed by law.

Sec. 802. Visitorial power of the state—How exercised.  
The visitorial or superintending power of the state over corporations created by the legislature will always be exercised in proper cases, through the medium of the courts of the state, to keep those corporations within the limits of their lawful powers and to correct and punish abuses of their franchises. To this end the courts will issue writs of quo warranto, mandamus or injunction, as the exigencies of the particular case may require; will inquire into the grievance complained of; and if the same is found to exist, will apply such remedy as the law prescribes. Every corporation of the state, whether public or private, civil or municipal, is subject to this superintending control, although in its exercise different rules may be applied to different classes of corporations.

I. Railroad Companies.

Sec. 803. Railroad companies are quasi-public corporations.—Railroad companies are, as before stated, quasi-public corporations, having the right of eminent domain. Not only can they be compelled to the performance of such acts as are expressly required by their charter or by law, but also to perform such additional acts as may clearly appear to be incident to those expressly required and essential to their due performance. In fact they are constructed and operated for the public use, but for private gain as the result of private investment. Hence they more nearly concern the public than ordinary private corporations formed and organized for private purposes, and to a greater degree are subject to control by mandamus.

1 McCrary v. Beaudry, 67 Cal. 120. 2 State v. Milwaukee Chamber of Commerce, 47 Wis. 670.

<br>

### --- Page 0457 ---

1000 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$$ 804, 805.

Sec. 804. Writ to compel operation of road.—Whatever may be the rule in this country as to the construction of grants of franchises or authority to construct and operate railroads, whether compulsory or permissive on the part of such companies, it is well settled that the power exists on the part of the state, through its courts, to compel the operation of the road pursuant to and in accordance with the terms of the charter. In other words, it cannot materially change its route, after construction, to the injury of a considerable portion of the public, without legislative consent. Thus, as we have seen, where such a company was authorized to construct a road to New Haven Bay, it could not thereafter abandon that terminus and seek another.

Sec. 805. Deviation from established route.—Yet this rule does not go to the extent of absolutely compelling a railroad company to operate its road upon the exact line upon which it was constructed, nor to prevent it from deviating therefrom, so that it substantially complies with the requirements of its charter. Where a railroad company, by consolidation, becomes the owner of two lines of road between the same points, and can substantially accommodate the people of the state by operating one of them, and can abandon the other without serious detriment to any considerable number of people, it may not be compelled by mandamus to operate both, where the operation of the line which may thus be abandoned entails great expense without any return. In another jurisdiction it was held that a railroad company chartered to construct a road between certain points named could not abandon a part thereof by constructing an extension of the road from an intermediate point on the line of the road as built, thus excluding one of such points from the benefit of the road; that a power given in the charter in general terms to change the location of the road did not include the power to change the termini. It was further held that the company had not the right to abandon or discontinue any part of the road when once completed.

1 State v. Hartford, etc. R. Co. 29 Conn. 588. See also People v. Albany Vt. R. Co., 24 N. Y. 261; People v. 2 People v. Rome, W. & O. R. Co, 103 N. Y. 95.

<br>

### --- Page 0458 ---

§§ 806-808.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1001

and that mandamus would lie to compel the restoration of that part of the road which had been dismantled.¹

Sec. 806. Writ does not lie to compel construction of road. With respect to the proposition whether a railroad company can be compelled to construct a road for the construction and operation of which it has a franchise, there can be but one opinion where no substantial part of the road has been constructed. The writ certainly will not lie where it cannot be operative, and it would be absurd to issue it to compel the building of an improvement where the company was without funds to build it, or the means of raising the necessary funds. It seems that in England it was first held that the writ would lie to compel the construction of a road.² But the contrary was subsequently held.³

Sec. 807. Poverty as an excuse for not building extension. Where the writ was applied for to compel a railroad company to build an extension, and it appeared the company had leased its road to another company, whose property, including the leased road, was in the hands of receivers appointed by a federal court, which could not be compelled by another court to operate the extension, and the respondent company was financially unable to make the extension, the application was denied.⁴

Sec. 808. Must operate road as a continuous line.—Railroad companies also, under charters or laws which authorize the operation and construction of a railroad from a stated initial point to designated termini, are bound to operate and run the whole road as one continuous line, and may be by mandamus compelled so to do. Hence it was held that the Union Pacific Railroad Company was bound to operate and run its whole road from the initial point on the Iowa side of the Missouri river to the termini of that particular branch, including

¹ Attorney-General v. West Wis. Ry. Co., 36 Wis. 466. See also People v. Albany & Vt. R. Co., 24 N.Y. 261; North Midland R. Co. v. Queen, 1 El. & Ald. 646.  
² Queen v. Rochelle & Halifax Turnpike, 12 Ad. & E. (N.S.) 448; Regina v. York, 16 Eng. L. & Eq. Rep. 493.  
³ Rex v. Severn & U.R.R. Co., 2 Barn. & B.L. 858; Town of Strasburg v. Winchester & S. R. Co. (Va.), 278 E. Rep. 299.  
⁴ Ibid.

<br>

### --- Page 0459 ---

1002 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 809, 810.

the bridge across the Missouri river at that point. That the arrangement such as the company had made, by which freight and passengers destined for or beyond the eastern termini are stopped two or three miles from it and transferred to another train, and again transferred at the termini, or by which freight and passengers going west from the eastern end of the line must be transferred at Omaha, breaks the road into two lines and is inconsistent with the continuous operation of it as a whole.

Sec. 809. Manner of construction—Viaducts, construction of.—There are duties imposed upon railroad corporations, sometimes directly expressed and often by implication, with respect to the manner in which the road shall be constructed. Where such duty is express, as to do a particular act, then of course there can arise no question of the right to compel its being done by means of mandamus. Hence where the charter of a railroad company requires it to build viaducts in a particular city; where its road crosses certain streets, in case of refusal mandamus will lie to enforce performance of such requirements. Where such act is required by ordinance of a city, it would seem the duty should be clearly defined as to dimensions and material, and such ordinance must not necessitate an unlawful change of grade of streets.

Sec. 810. Power of legislature to impose additional restrictions and burdens.—The power of the state to impose upon railroad companies created by it such restrictions and burdens as the public good requires, though they are additional to those embraced in their charters, is undoubted. This power extends to regulating the mode in which they shall transact their business, the price they shall charge for the transportation of freight and passengers, the speed at which they shall run their trains, and the way in which they may cross or run

1 Union Pac. R. Co. v. Hall, 91 U.S. 343.  
2 State v. Missouri Pac. R. Co., 33 Kan. 176.

3 Chicago, B. & Q. R. Co. v. State, 47 Neb. 549; State v. Missouri, etc. R. Co. v. Brownell, 24 N.Y. 345, People v. Boston & Albany R. Co., 70 N.Y. 569; Albany & Northern R. Co., 33 Kan. 176.

<br>

### --- Page 0460 ---

§§ 811, 812.] Private Corporations — Public Duties. 1003

upon highways and turnpikes used for public travel. Hence it is no objection that the act was not required by the original act conferring the franchise, and therefore mandamus was held to lie to compel a railroad company to construct a bridge in a manner specified over a turnpike road.

Sec. 811. May direct changes and repairs.— The doctrine extends to the making of changes and repairs in such structures. It is no sufficient objection that the highway was graded and constructed at the particular place in view of the probable travel upon it, and was suitable for all kinds of conveyances then in use. If by the increase of population in the neighborhood, or by an increasing use of the highway, a crossing which at the outset was adequate is no longer so, it is the duty of the corporation to make such alteration as will meet the present needs of the public that have occasion to use the highway.

Sec. 812. Charters in some states considered contracts — Rule as to control.— In some of the states charters granted to railroad, turnpike and other corporations to perform acts for the public use, when accepted are considered as contracts, which the legislature may not change. Hence, it was held in one state that an act of the legislature authorizing the erection of a bridge over navigable water with two suitable draws of designated width constitutes, when accepted, a contract between the commonwealth and the corporation which cannot be affected by a subsequent act requiring the corporation to maintain draws of greater width. It was further held, however, that notwithstanding the width of the draws was designated in the original act or contract, such act obliged the corporation to enlarge their draws when necessary for the convenient accommodation of vessels having occasion to navigate the river, and the question whether the draws are thus suitable is not to be determined by the legislature nor by the corporation, but by the courts. The case cited was indictment for maintaining a nuisance. The duty being conceded, and that

1 People v. Boston & Albany R. Co., 70 N. Y. 569.  
2 Cooke v. Boston & Lowell R. Co., 133 Mass. 185.  
3 People v. Boston & Albany R. Co., 70 N. Y. 569.  
4 Commonwealth v. New Bedford Bridge, 2 Gray, 339.

<br>

### --- Page 0461 ---

1004 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 813.

duty being declared in that proceeding to construct a suitable draw, the question for the court was simply whether the draw was suitable or otherwise. In mandamus to compel the performance of that duty a more serious question would arise, and that is, Could the court in the proceeding determine the exact duty of the corporation in the premises? Could it determine the length of draws that should be constructed, and then require them to construct draws of such dimensions, or simply direct that suitable draws be constructed, leaving the dimensions to the judgment and discretion of the company, who must again answer to an indictment or other proceeding if the structure they build is not suitable, in the judgment of the court. It would seem upon principle that the latter is the only direction the court could make; but as we shall see, the principles underlying the writ are frequently warped to meet particular emergencies, and necessarily this might be done. In cases like the one under consideration the discretion is vested in the court. It is said in the opinion that the question is not one to be determined by the legislature or the bridge company, but by the courts; but I apprehend the statement extends no further than that the judgment of the bridge company is not conclusive. In the very nature of things they must determine it in the first instance. There is no way that it can call upon the court to exercise its discretion. It must act at its peril.

Sec. 813. Court may direct the manner in which a duty shall be performed.— This question is one of importance, and is to be met in other cases, and especially where the law requires railroad companies to restore highways which they cross to a suitable condition for travel. I find this question was met and decided by the New York court of appeals1 and the supreme court of Minnesota.2 It was stated that, “while a railroad corporation has discretion as to the manner of performing the duty imposed upon it by the general railroad act, of restoring a highway across or along which its road has been constructed, the discretion is a ministerial one. The act of restoration must be done; as to this there is no discretion. If it

1 People v. Dutchess & Columbia R. Co., 58 N. Y. 152.  
2 State v. Minneapolis & St. Louis R. Co. (Minn.), 39 N. W. Rep. 153.

<br>

### --- Page 0462 ---

§ 813.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1005

elects a manner which proves ineffectual and yet it claims to have performed its duty, and the aid of the court is invoked by the commissioners of highways to compel, by mandamus, the performance, the court has power to and should point out in the writ in what the corporation has failed, and to direct particularly what must be done so that it may not fail again.” It should be stated, however, that the rule in New York is that the mandatory writ may go into particulars. Such rule met the approval of Lord Denman, C. J., in Regina v. East. Co. R., 2 Add. & Ell. (N.S.) 569, and is the only one that can be effectually applied to enforce the complete performance of the duty required in the particular class of cases under discussion.

The supreme court of Minnesota not only approves of the doctrine of the New York court of appeals, but declares that the whole question of the plan and mode of construction rests with the courts, to be determined on the hearing for the writ in the first instance. Thus it is said: “It is urged by the appellant as an objection to the writ in this case, that it prescribes particularly the manner in which the alleged duty is to be performed, instead of allowing the respondent to adopt its own plan for restoring the usefulness and safety of these streets. Where, as in this case, it has been in no manner determined, either by the law, by the circumstances of the case, or otherwise, how the alleged duty should be performed, the course suggested by this contention of the respondent would be subject to the most obvious of objections. It may be assumed that where it is necessary to resort to compulsory process of the courts in such cases, it is because there is a disagreement between the public authorities and the respondent as to the duty of the latter to do anything, or as to what its duty requires it to do. Neither of the parties thus opposed in interest can determine these matters of difference. It is for the courts to decide. It is expedient that the thing to be done be effectually determined before a peremptory writ be issued, and that party upon which the duty may be found to rest be required to do that specific thing which, when done, must be accepted.

1 People v. Supervisors of Delaware, Dutchess & Columbia R. Co., 58 N.Y. 196; People v. Supervisors of Otsego, 51 N.Y. 408; People v.

<br>

### --- Page 0463 ---

1006 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 814.

as the performance of its duty. If the writ were to command generally the performance of the duty of restoring the street to a condition of safety and usefulness for public travel, the respondent being left to select its own plan and means of accomplishing this result, it might be found, after much time and money had been consumed in carrying out the plan adopted by the respondent, that it was not such as to accomplish the public purposes in view. The court might so decide, and command the work to be undertaken anew.

It will be noted that the court in reaching this conclusion cannot be charged with reviewing or encroaching upon a discretion possessed by the railroad company. It simply declares that the company is not clothed with the discretion with respect to the particular matter; that the discretion is vested elsewhere, to wit, in the courts; that its duty is subject to the exercise of that discretion, and it necessarily follows that the municipality is without that discretion. The court proceeds further to state: “The city authorities had no power to compel these corporations to adopt the plan which they thought best; and since, as has been decided, it could only be determined by judicial decision what the corporation should be compelled to do, it is obvious that any fixing of grade for these bridges by the action of the city council, prior to the decision of the controversy as to whether the corporations were under obligation to bridge the streets, and, if so, how it should be done, would have been speculative and subject to such changes as might become necessary as the result of the decision of this court. The authority of the court to determine the necessity and the mode of carrying these streets over the tracks necessarily involves the power to determine the elevation or grade of the bridges, and to this extent the power given by the city charter to the city council to establish and change the grade of streets must be regarded as qualified.”

Sec. 814. Restoring highways to suitable condition.— The duty is imposed upon railroad companies to restore a highway which it crosses to a suitable condition for public use. That the performance of this duty may be enforced by mandamus is unquestioned, and we have seen the determination as to whether

<br>

### --- Page 0464 ---

§ $14.) PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1007

this duty has been performed is ultimately for the court in proceedings by mandamus; and this duty will thus be enforced, though the corporation may be thus compelled to resort to proceedings to condemn land for such purpose, or to change the location of its main track.

In determining the duty of railroads under a statute and at common law to restore the condition of a public highway, it was said the right to interfere with a public highway is coupled with the duty to restore it to the condition of safety and usefulness in which it was before it was disturbed, or at least to restore it in such manner as not unnecessarily to impair its utility for public travel. This duty is violated if there is a failure to restore it to its former condition, in all cases where such restoration can be effected by the exercise of reasonable care and skill. The duty of restoration also includes the duty of maintaining the crossing in such manner as not unnecessarily to impair the convenience, safety and usefulness of the highway. The railway company must so improve and maintain the crossing as reasonably to subserve the growing needs of the public. It does not perform the full measure of its duty by making a sufficient crossing over a highway, when by the growth of population and travel the crossing becomes inadequate to accommodate in a reasonable manner the increasing needs of the public. Citing Railroad Co. v. Smith, 61 Fed. Rep. 885; Cooke v. Railroad Co., 133 Mass. 185; Railroad Co. v. Cluggish, 143 Ind. 347. Substantially the same was stated by the Massachusetts court, in substance that they could not accept that construction of the statute which would limit the duty and obligation of the railroad company to provide for the wants of the public at the time the structure was built. That the legislature intended to provide against any obstruction of the safe and convenient use of the highway for all time; and if by increase of population in the neighborhood, or by the increasing use of the highway, the crossing, which at the outset was

1 People v. Dutchess & Columbia R. Co., 58 N. Y. 152; State v. Minneapolis & St. L. R. Co. (Minn.), 39 N. W. Rep. 152.

2 People v. Dutchess & Columbia R. Co., 58 N. Y. 152.

3 State v. Minneapolis & St. L. R. Co. (Minn.), 39 N. W. Rep. 152.

4 State v. Lake Erie & West. R. Co., 88 Fed. Rep. 284.

<br>

### --- Page 0465 ---

1008 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 815.

adequate, is no longer so, it is the duty of the railroad corporation to make such alteration as will meet the present needs of the public who have occasion to use the highway. The duty imposed by law upon such corporations with respect to crossings extends to and includes such highways as may be laid out and established subsequent to the construction of the railroad.

In discussing this question of the existence and adequacy of other remedies, the federal circuit court, speaking with reference to the duty of a railroad company to restore the condition of a public street, said: “In order to justify the denial of a writ of mandamus, not only must the party have a different remedy, but that remedy must be equally as adequate, convenient and complete as the proceeding by mandamus. The controlling question is not, has the party a remedy at law? but, is that remedy fully commensurate with the interests and rights of the parties under all the circumstances of the particular case? It is equally settled that neither liability to indictment nor to a penal action under the statute constitutes a bar to relief by mandamus. Such remedies, if remedies they may be called, are merely cumulative. Mandamus proceedings will lie to require a railroad company having its track upon, along or across the street or alley of a city to so construct and maintain the crossing of the track as to render the use of the streets and alleys and the crossing suitable, convenient and safe for the public; and that the railroad company is liable to indictment for the obstruction, or that a penalty may be recovered from it, or that the city may construct a suitable crossing and recover its cost, is no reason why a writ of mandamus should be denied. None of those methods of procedure would afford a remedy so convenient, beneficial and effective as the proceeding by mandamus.”*

Sec. 815. Private crossings—Duty in respect to.—Where it in any manner becomes the duty of such company to construct a private crossing at a place to be designated by the owner of the land, such owner may have to resort to mandamus.

1 Cooke v. Boston & Lowell R. Co., 133 Mass. 185.

2 State v. Lake Erie & Western R. Co., 83 Fed. Rep. 284.

3 State v. Chi., B. & Q. R. Co., 29 Neb. 412.

<br>

### --- Page 0466 ---

§§ 816, 817. PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1009

to compel the proper performance of such duty. And it is no answer to say that the farm owner had another remedy by action to recover penalties imposed by statute for the failure on the part of the railroad company to comply with the provisions of the law. Such provision enables the owner to recover damages or penalties for failure of duty, but that will not secure the construction of the necessary farm-crossing, nor is it an adequate remedy; nor will the fact that a bill in equity lies for a mandatory injunction to compel the performance of such duty preclude resort to mandamus. It seems that such a bill has been sustained, but it is doubtful if the practice is correct.

Sec. 816. Cattle-guards — Duty in respect to. — The same rule applies with respect to the construction of cattle-guards, and in fact the rule is general that officers of a corporation created by the laws of a state and exercising franchises may be compelled by mandamus to perform specific acts enjoined by law for the benefit of the public; and the fact that an action is authorized by statute to compel the performance of such an act does not supersede mandamus.

Sec. 817. Fences — Duty in respect to. — In respect to constructing fences the duty depends upon provisions of statute or of the company’s charter. At common law the duty does not rest upon the corporation. In many of the states, in the absence of statutes imposing such duties upon the corporation, the presumption is that they have already paid the expense of fencing in the land in damages. However, at the present day, in most of the states, statutes are in force which impose the duty.

1 People v. Chicago & Alton R. Co., 67 Ill 118; State v. Chicago, M. & N. R. Co., 48 N. W. Rep. 243.

2 State v. Chicago, M. & N. R. Co., 48 N. W. Rep. 243.

3 Thorpe v. Railroad Co., 27 Vt. 140; State v. Chi, B. & Q. R. Co., 29 Neb. 412, 45 N. W. Rep. 469; People v. Rochester & State Line R. Co., 76 N. Y. 294.

4 Hurd v. Rutland & B. Co., 25 Vt. 116; New York & Erie R. Co. v. Skinner, 19 Pa. St. 298; Dean v. Sullivan R. Co., 2 Fost. (N. H.) 316; Alton & Sangamon R. Co. v. Baugh, 14 Ill. 21; Morss v. Boston & Maine R. Co., 2 Cush. 536.

5 Balt. & Ohio R. Co. v. Lamborn, 12 Md. 257; Madison & Ind. R. Co. v. Kam, 11 Ind. 875; Stucke v. Mil. & Miss. R. Co., 9 Wis. 202; Morss v. Boston & Maine R. Co., 2 Cush. 536; Sacramento Valley R. Co. v. Moffat, 6 Cal. 74.

<br>

### --- Page 0467 ---

1010 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 818, 819.

of fencing tracks upon the corporation, and it follows that this duty, where not performed, may be enforced by mandamus.

Sec. 818. Establishment of stations or depots — Duty.—It has been held that mandamus would lie to compel railroad corporations to establish depots and stations at convenient points; that such matters were not entirely within the discretion of the corporation. This result is reached from the fact that such roads are public highways; that the use, being public, does not become private from the mode of use; that railroad charters are contracts made by the legislature in behalf of every person interested in anything done under them. In consideration of the franchise they receive from the state, such corporations agree to perform certain duties toward the public. The power of determining those duties and enforcing their performance is vested in the appropriate tribunals of the state. Being creatures of the law, intrusted with the exercise of sovereign powers, and to subserve public necessities and uses, they are bound to conduct their affairs in furtherance of the public objects of their creation. The public use demands proper and reasonable facilities, and the question is whether such as the company has provided are in fact reasonable under the particular conditions and circumstances. The power to determine this question is in the courts, as is also the power to determine what is reasonable and proper, and require that which is so determined to be done; and such power may be used with respect to the establishment of depots as well as the construction of viaducts and crossings.

Sec. 819. Reasoning of the Massachusetts court.—The reasoning of the court in the Massachusetts case cited is very apt and pertinent. “If the directors of a railroad were to find it for the interest of the corporation to refuse to carry any freight or passengers except such as they might take at one end of the road and carry entirely through to the other end, and were to refuse to establish any way-stations or to do any business for

1 People v. Rochester & Hartford, etc. R. Co., 29 Conn. 528; R. Co., 76 N. Y. 294. Commonwealth v. Eastern R. R. Co., Railroad Com’rs v. P. & O. C. R. Co., 103 Mass. 258, 68 Me. 269. See also State v.

<br>

### --- Page 0468 ---

§§ 820, 821.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1011

That reason, though the road passed for a long distance through a populous part of the state, this would be a cause manifestly requiring and authorizing legislative interference under the clause in question. And on the same ground, if they refuse to provide reasonable accommodations for the people of any smaller locality, the legislature may reasonably alter and modify the discretionary powers which the charter confers upon the directors so as to make the duty to provide the accommodation absolute. Cases may occur where railroad directors might do this from motives of resentment or ill-will, or a pecuniary interest in building up the towns or cities at the termini of the road to the injury of intervening localities. In that case the legislature had passed an act compelling the company to establish a flag-station at a certain point and erect a station-house there at which at least two trains each day each way should stop.

Sec. 820. Abandonment and restoration of depots.—It appears that in another jurisdiction the very question which the court supposed was presented — that of abandoning a station which had been established, and was reasonably necessary for the convenience of the public, and establishing one four miles beyond, where there were but few houses, and which speculators were trying to build up as a rival town; and it was held that the railroad company could by mandamus be compelled to stop its trains, establish a depot and afford proper facilities for transacting business at the original station! The writ, however, will not lie to compel the location of a depot at any particular place in a town.

Sec. 821. Delivery of freight — Discrimination.—The same principle is involved with respect to the delivery of freight. The railroad corporation cannot discriminate among those of a certain class. In other words, it cannot deliver to one and refuse to deliver to another where substantially the same conditions exist as to each. Thus, it cannot refuse to deliver

1 Northern Pac. R. Co. v. Territory, 87 Conn. 154; State v. New Haven, 8 Wash. Ter. 303. See also establishment of an abandoned station, State v. New Haven, etc. R. Co., 41 Conn. 134.

2 Florida Cent. & P. R. Co. v. State (Fla.), 13 S. Rep. 163.

<br>

### --- Page 0469 ---

1012 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 822.]

grain to one particular elevator or warehouse to which it may be consigned, where such elevator or warehouse is located on the line of its road, and has reasonable facilities for receiving such grain as compared to other elevators and warehouses. Mandamus is the appropriate remedy to enforce the performance of duty in this respect.

Sec. 822. Carriage and delivery of freight—Conditions and terms must be reasonable.—The duty of such corporation is to receive and transport freight as well as passengers. It can make reasonable rules and regulations in respect thereto, to which the public must conform. But such rules and regulations in no manner have the effect to restrict the performance of its duty, which is that it shall receive and transport freight, duly delivered, upon such terms and subject to such conditions as are reasonable; and what may be reasonable under the circumstances is a question that may be determined by the courts. It must not be assumed that such corporations are obliged to receive whatever articles may be offered, without regard to their character or condition. Where property is publicly exposed to the depredations of a mob, or where the goods are not safe to be carried, it seems the carrier may refuse to receive them. A railroad company refused to transport a beverage billed as New Era beer into the state of Iowa upon the ground that it would be in violation of the provisions of the prohibitory liquor law of that state. It was insisted that this particular article was not intoxicating and therefore not prohibited. The writ was refused. It was said the article was denominated beer. Beer is expressly prohibited. The defendant had a right to assume that it was intoxicating. That the corporation was vested with a discretion as to what evidence in respect to its being intoxicating it should rely upon; therefore the writ could not issue to control that discretion.

1 Chicago & N. W. R. Co. v. People, 56 Ill. 365.
2 People v. New York R. Co., 28 Hun, How. Pr. 291.
3 Edwards v. Sheratt, 3 Kent’s Comm. 599.
4 Story on Bailments, sec. 328; 2 East, 604.
5 Mil. Extract Co. v. Chi. etc. R. Co., 73 Iowa, 98.

<br>

### --- Page 0470 ---

§ 823. Writ will not lie to compel performance of duties resting purely in contract.—Mandamus is not the appropriate remedy to enforce obligations on the part of such corporations resting purely in contract. There is nothing in their public character that makes resort to such remedy necessary; they are subject to the same liabilities and remedies as other corporations for the failure to observe their contract duties, and the ordinary actions at law or suits in equity afford an adequate remedy. Thus where, by the terms of a contract existing between county commissioners and a turnpike company, such corporation was to contribute three-fourths of the expense of keeping a bridge in repair, and mandamus was applied for to compel it to keep and maintain such bridge in proper repair, it appearing that the commissioners had offered to contribute the one-fourth part of the expense, or to make the necessary repairs upon joint proportionate account, it was held that the writ could not issue, on the ground that purely contract relations involving no trust cannot be enforced by mandamus. The corporate duty of the corporation to keep the bridge in repair was disposed of upon other grounds. The writ was denied in another case upon similar grounds. It was insisted that a railroad corporation should operate an abandoned line of road upon the ground of a condition of a contract, where the town had bonded itself to aid in the construction of such line of railroad, in substance that a permanent depot should be erected and maintained at a specific place in the town. It was said: The contract right and obligation are not in any proper sense a public matter in which the people of the state in their sovereign capacity are interested. The same rule was applied where the writ was sought to compel the building of a bridge over a river, the obligation to build it existing, if at all, by virtue of the conditions of a contract. The rule is not changed by the fact that the contract sought to be enforced was one which was required to be entered into by the provisions of the charter of the corporation. Other remedies exist and are adequate for such grievances.

1 State v. Zanesville & Mayville Turnpike Co., 16 Ohio St. 308.  
2 People v. Rome, W. & O. R. Co., 103 N. Y. 96.  
3 State v. Republican River Bridge Co., 20 Kan. 404.  
4 State v. Paterson, N. & N. Y. R. Co., 43 N. J. L. 505.

<br>

### --- Page 0471 ---

1014 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$$ S24-826.

Sec. 824. Writ only lies to compel performance of duties that are specific, and where the right is clear and undoubted.— When it is sought by mandamus to compel a railroad company to do any act in relation to the equipment and operation of its road, the courts, as a general rule, will not interfere with its management of its railway in these respects, except where the act sought to be enforced is specific, and the right to its performance in the manner proposed is clear and undoubted.

Sec. 825. Furnishing separate train for passengers — Duty. The duty of furnishing a separate passenger train for passengers only, and not for freight and passengers together, is implied in the duty of a railroad company to furnish necessary rolling stock and equipment for the suitable and proper operation of a railroad. Such duty is specific and sufficiently clear to authorize the issue of mandamus to compel its performance. It was held, however, by the supreme court of Kansas that the writ will not lie to compel the enforcement of an order directing a railroad company to restore and operate a passenger train. Such an order is not final and conclusive under the Kansas statute. It appeared that the company had been operating an exclusive passenger train and a freight train once a day each way, and, finding the revenue insufficient, abandoned the passenger train, operating only a combination train. It thus appears that there is a conflict of judicial opinion between the Illinois and Kansas courts upon this question.

Sec. 826. Regulating rates.— In many of the states boards or officers have been provided clothed with certain duties and powers with respect to supervision and control of railroads. They have the power, in some cases, to investigate charges of unlawful discrimination and to fix rates within certain limits, and their orders are ordinarily enforceable by mandamus. Thus, where a board of transportation thus authorized has investigated charges of unjust discrimination against a railroad company and found it to exist, and ordered the company to

1 People v. Alton & Chicago R. Co., 180 IL. 175; People v. St. Louis, H. & T. H. R. Co. (IIL). 35 L. R. A. 656. 2 People v. St. Louis, H. & T. H. R. Co, (IIL), 85 L. R. A. 565. 3 State v. Missouri Pac. R. Co., 55 Kan. 708.

<br>

### --- Page 0472 ---

§§ 827, 828.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1015

reduce its rates to conform to a schedule presented by such board, which order the company ignored, it was held that compliance therewith would be enforced by mandamus:

Sec. 827. Unlawful discrimination — Control.— The law with respect to controlling unlawful discrimination applies as well to persons as to rates. Such a company cannot select whom it will to enjoy exclusive privileges upon its road. Thus, it cannot grant the exclusive privilege to any particular express company, but must give to every one equal facilities and rights. In defining express facilities it was said: “As interpreted by the customs and usages of these parties (referring to the parties to the suit), and sanctioned and adopted by the decisions of the courts, these facilities may be said to include the right to enter depots and stations with loaded and empty wagons; the use of the platforms and space for the loading and unloading of express freight; sufficient space in suitable cars, drawn in passenger or quick trains, for the transportation of such freight, and a messenger in charge thereof, with room for its assortment while in transit, and a sufficient delay at stations for the delivery and receipt of express matter. Express facilities, from the nature of the business, cannot be limited to a definite space, but must correspond in this and other particulars to the public want and convenience to which the express company ministers.”

Sec. 828. Commutation tickets — Issue of.— A railroad company is under no obligation to issue commutation tickets for any particular locality or station. But when it has established such a system and rate to the public, it cannot refuse the advantages thereof to any particular individual. Such an act would be in law an unjust discrimination against such individual, and a violation of the principle of equality which railroad companies must observe in the management and conduct of their business.

1 State v. Fremont, E. & M. V. R. 3 Wells, Fargo & Co. v. Oregon Ry. Co., 22 Neb. 313, 85 N. W. Rep. 118. & Nav. Co., 15 Fed. Rep. 561.

2 Wells, Fargo & Co. v. Oregon Ry. & Nav. Co., 15 Fed. Rep. 561; Wells, Fargo & Co. v. Nor. Pac. R. Co., 23 Fed. Rep. 469.

<br>

### --- Page 0473 ---

1016 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 829-831.]

Sec. 829. Compensation for land.—In England the writ has been in many cases issued for the protection of landowners in their right to compensation for land taken by virtue of the right of eminent domain. Where a railroad company has failed to institute proper proceedings to ascertain such compensation, the courts have compelled it to do so by proceedings in mandamus; and in some cases such a company has been compelled to make payment of the amount of compensation thus determined, on the ground of the inadequacy of any other remedy. In this country, however, statutes usually provide that the landowner may institute the proceedings in case the corporation neglects. He also has a right of action for damages and for possession where his property has been unlawfully appropriated. It would seem that such remedies are entirely adequate.

Sec. 830. Writ issues on ground that there is no other remedy that is adequate.—In those cases which have been referred to where it is held that the writ would lie, and generally where duties of a similar character are imposed which relate to the rights and welfare of the public, there is no other adequate remedy. An indictment is not, nor is an action for damages, for the very plain reason, if for no other, that they do not affect existing conditions. They still remain.

II. Water-works Companies.

Sec. 831. Water-works companies are quasi-public corporations.—In preceding pages it has been stated that corporations created under the laws of a state for the purpose of vending and distributing water to the inhabitants of a locality are quasi-public corporations. They may exercise the right of eminent domain. Their purpose is public in character, and subject to the same general power and control which prevails with respect to railroad corporations.  

1 Queen v. Eastern Counties R. Co., 2 Ad. & E. (N.S.) 347; King v. Waterworks Co., 6 Ad. & E. 350; Queen v. Trustees of Swansea Harbor, 8 Ad. & E. 439; Queen v. Deptford Pier Co., 8 Ad. & E. 910.  
2 Habersham v. Savannah & Ogeechee Canal Co., 26 Ga. 665.  
3 See supra, sec. 801; Price v. Riverside L. & I. Co., 56 Cal. 431; McCrary v. Beaudry, 67 Cal. 120.

<br>

### --- Page 0474 ---

§§ 832, 833.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1017

Sec. 832. Subject to control with respect to public duties.— It was there stated that they could make no discrimination either in persons or price. It must follow from the rules which have been stated with respect to railroad corporations that all duties or acts which they are by law required to perform may be enforced by mandamus. The rates that may be charged, in the absence of any designation in their charters, evidently must be such as under all the circumstances must be reasonable, and that question ultimately is one to be determined by the courts. In many charters, however, the maximum rates are fixed, and the question arises whether charters of such a character so far partake of the nature of a contract that they become absolute and unalterable. Such charters usually emanate from the local municipality which permits the use of the streets for pipes and incident purposes. In most of the states at the present time, the state, by force of its constitution or laws, has a reserved power to alter or change the rights and privileges granted, and this extends to regulating the prices that may be charged consumers. The provision for fixing rates cannot be separated from the remainder of the statute by calling it a contract. Hence, laws requiring gas companies, water companies and other corporations to supply their customers at prices fixed by the municipal authorities of the locality are within the scope of legislative power, unless prohibited by constitutional limitation or valid contract obligations, and subsequent legislation affecting the franchise is not prohibited.

Sec. 833. Authority of courts with respect to rates.— The question that more nearly pertains to our subject is to what extent, if at all, can the courts upon mandamus regulate such rates, if as fixed they are unreasonable. The proposition was stated in Spring Valley Water Works Co. v. Schottler, supra (110 U.S. 347), but not decided. It was further stated that the prices fixed by the honest judgment of a commission duly authorized must be deemed reasonable both by the company and the pub-

¹ Spring Valley Water-works Co. v. Schottler, 110 U.S. 347; Munn v. Illinois, 94 U.S. 118; Sinking Fund Cases, 99 U.S. 700.

<br>

### --- Page 0475 ---

1018 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§§ 834-836]

lic. It would follow that, as mandamus does not lie to control a discretion honestly exercised, it would not lie to determine the question proposed. That it might lie where there had been fraud or a clear abuse of discretion is clearly a principle of the law of mandamus. The same rule applies to companies or persons operating under a franchise that we have seen is applicable to railroad corporations, and that is that they cannot discriminate either in respect to persons or rates.

Sec. 834. Compelled to extend mains.—Such companies may also be compelled by mandamus to extend their mains, where under the ordinance by which the right to construct and operate such works is granted the duty is imposed upon the company. Such a contract, if it be termed a contract, is not of that character which precludes the enforcement of its conditions by means of such a proceeding.

Sec. 835. To change location of pipes.—Mandamus is the appropriate remedy to compel a water company to change the location of pipes which obstruct the flow of water in the sewers, and it is no sufficient objection to the granting of the writ that the pipes were originally laid with the consent of the city. The question of the liability on the part of the city for the expense incident to such change is not a proper subject for consideration.

Sec. 836. To furnish pure and wholesome water.—Charters sometimes provide for the furnishing of pure and wholesome water in sufficient quantity to meet specified needs. The word “pure” in such a charter means wholesome or ordinarily pure, and not pure in a chemical sense. It is quite probable that the duty thus imposed, where the charter was silent in this respect, would include as much. The question not only of the duty of such a company to furnish pure water, but also of the power of the court to enforce such duty by mandamus, was de-

1 American Water Works Co. v. City Council v. Capital City State, 46 Neb. 194; McCrary v. Water Co. (Ala.), 98 S. Rep. 339.  
2 City of Topeka v. Topeka Water Works Co. (Kan.), 49 Pac. Rep. 79.  
3 Beaudry, 67 Cal. 120.  
4 Commonwealth v. Towanda Water Works Co., 15 Atl. Rep. 440.

<br>

### --- Page 0476 ---

§ 837.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1019

determined by the New York supreme court in a recent case. In prior pages we have stated the conclusion of the court with respect to these questions (ante, sec. 801a), which was in effect that having undertaken in view of its franchises of a public character, and expressly charged with the duty of performing a certain service to all who pay certain rates or fees therefor, it could be compelled by mandamus to perform such service, which in that case was the furnishing of pure water.

Sec. 837. The nature of the franchise does not prevent the enforcement of a clear duty.— Some courts and text-writers make use of the term “contracts” as applied to charters of such character. This is misleading. They are in no sense such contracts as to preclude supervision by the courts so far as they relate to public use and the public welfare. They are more in the nature of public duties voluntarily assumed, and incident to the nature of their franchise. The duty to furnish wholesome water is as much a public duty as that water shall be furnished to all consumers without discrimination. It is evident, therefore, that where such a company, exercising a franchise for public uses, fails or refuses to furnish wholesome water, its duty in this respect may be enforced by mandamus, as well as where it fails or refuses to furnish sufficient in quantity, having the power so to do. That an action in equity to rescind the franchise is not an adequate remedy, nor is an action for damages. Yet where the conditions prescribed by an ordinance have been accepted and fully performed, a contract is thereby created which cannot be violated, unless by reason of some reserved power existing when the contract was made. Thus, it was stated by the United States supreme court as late as 1898, that that court had too often decided for the rule to be even questioned, that the grant of a right to supply gas and water to a municipality and its inhabitants, through pipes and mains laid in the streets, upon condition of the performance of its service by the grantee, is the grant of a franchise, vested in the state, in consideration of the performance of a public service, and after performance by the grantee is a contract protected by the constitution of the United States against state legislation to

1 People v. Suburban Water Co., 56 N. Y. Supp. 364,

<br>

### --- Page 0477 ---

1020 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 837.

impair it. (Citing several cases.) It is true that in these cases the franchise was granted directly by the state legislature, but it is equally clear that such franchises may be bestowed upon corporations by the municipal authorities, provided the right to do so is given them by their charters. State legislatures may not only exercise their sovereignty directly, but may delegate such portions of it to inferior legislative bodies as in their judgment is desirable for local purposes.’

Note.— By an act of November 28, 1883, the legislature of Washington territory incorporated the city of Walla Walla, conferring upon it, among other powers, the power to provide a sufficient supply of water for the city, and the right to permit the use of the city streets for the purpose of laying pipes for furnishing such supply for a term not exceeding twenty-five years. The act contained a further provision fixing the limit of indebtedness of the city at $50,000. The city under this authority by contract granted the Walla Walla Water Company the right to lay and maintain water mains, etc., for twenty-five years, reserving to itself the right to maintain fire hydrants and to flush sewers during this term, each without charge. The contract further provided that it was voidable by the city, so far as it required the payment of money, upon the judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction, whenever there should be a substantial failure of such supply, or a like failure on the part of the company to perform its agreements, and that until the contract should have been so avoided the city should not erect or maintain, or become interested in, other water-works. These conditions were accepted by the water company and were complied with by it, and the contract was in force when a bill was filed as hereinafter stated. In 1893 the city authorities passed an ordinance to provide for the construction of a system of water-works to supply the city with water, and to issue bonds for that purpose to the amount of $160,000, which ordinance was accepted by the necessary majority of legal voters. The water company then filed its bill to enjoin the city from creating the proposed water-works, and from expending city moneys for that purpose and from issuing city securities therefor. To this bill the city demurred, resting its demurrer upon a want of jurisdiction, all parties on both sides being citizens of the state of Wisconsin. Held: (1) That the allegations of the bill raise a question of the constitutional power of the city to impair the obligations of its contract with the plaintiff by adopting the ordinance. (2) That the ordinance and acceptance constituted a contract as stated in the text. (3) That the plaintiff has no adequate and complete remedy at law and the court has jurisdiction in equity. (4) That as the contract was limited to twenty-five years, and as no attempt was made to grant an exclusive privilege, the city acted within the strictest limitation of its charter. (5) That if the contract for water supply was innocuous in itself and was carried out with due regard to the good order of the city and the health of its inhabitants, the aid of the police power could not be invoked to abrogate or impair it.

1 Walla Walla v. Walla Walla Water Co., 173 U.S. 1.

<br>

### --- Page 0478 ---

§ 837.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1021

The question was discussed in this connection of the power of one council to bind its successor or the city by present contracts of the character stated. It was said: “This court has doubtless held that the police power is one which remains constantly under the control of legislative authority, and that a city council can neither bind itself or its successors to contracts prejudicial to the peace, good order, health or morals of its inhabitants; but it is to cases of this kind that these rulings have been confined. If a contract be objectionable in itself upon these grounds, or if it become so in its execution, the municipality may, in the exercise of its police power, regulate the manner in which it may be carried out, or may abrogate it entirely, upon the principle that it cannot bind itself to any course of action which shall prove deleterious to the health or morals of its inhabitants. In such case an appeal to the contract clause of the constitution is ineffectual. . . .

Under this power and the analogous power of taxation we should have no doubt that the city council might take such measures as were necessary or prudent to secure the purity of water furnished under the contract of the company, the payment of its just contributions to the public burdens, and the observance of its own ordinances respecting the manner in which the pipes and mains of the company should be laid through the streets of the city. . . . If, as alleged in the answer, the water company failed to carry out its contract, and the supply furnished was inadequate for domestic sanitary or fire purposes, and the pressure so far insufficient that in many parts of the city water could not be carried above the first story of the buildings, the seventh section of the contract furnished an adequate and complete remedy by an application to the courts to declare the contract void.” Speaking with reference to exclusive franchises it was stated: “Cases are not infrequent where, under a general power to cause the streets of a city to be lighted or to furnish its inhabitants with a supply of water without limitation as to time, it has been held that the city has no right to grant an exclusive franchise for a period of years.” Citing many cases. Speaking further, with reference to limitations of indebtedness under charters, it was stated: “There is considerable conflict of authority respecting the proper construction of such limitation in municipal charters. There can be no doubt that if the city proposed to purchase outright or establish a system of waterworks of its own, the section (referring to that limiting the indebtedness to fifty thousand dollars) of the statute should apply, though bonds were issued therefor payable in the future. There are also a number of respectable authorities to the effect that the limitation covers a case where the city agrees to pay a certain sum per annum, if the aggregate amount payable under agreement exceeds the amount limited by the charter. But we think the weight of authority as well as of reason favors the more liberal construction that a municipal corporation may contract for a supply of water or gas, or like necessary, and may stipulate for the payment of an annual rental for the gas or water furnished each year, notwithstanding the aggregate of its rentals during the life of the contract may exceed the amount of the indebtedness limited by the charter.” The court distinguishes ordinary from extraordinary expenses, and states a rule which settles disputed questions with respect to the effect of such limitations upon indebtedness incurred by current expenses.

<br>

### --- Page 0479 ---

1022 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [§ 838.]

II. Gas Companies.

Sec. 838. Distinction attempted from water companies.—For reasons not satisfactory at least, some courts have made distinctions between gas and water companies in respect to their public character. Judge Sherwood, in Williams v. Mutual Gas Co. 4 Am. & Eng. Corp. Cases, says: “These rights and privileges (gas companies) are granted that corresponding duties and benefits might inure to the citizens when the rights and privileges conferred should be executed. The benefits are the compensation for the rights conferred and privileges granted, and are more in the nature of convenience than necessity. Hence the duty of such a corporation imposed cannot therefore be likened to the innkeeper or common carrier, but more necessarily approximates the telegraph, telephone, or mill-owner.” Substantially the same reasons were expressed by the supreme court of Connecticut. To these courts, then, it appears that the question of necessity as distinguished from convenience is the controlling element which determines the public character of corporations or those enjoying franchises granted by the state. That a use is not public—that is, not an absolute necessity—because it could be obtained from other sources; or that transportation by rail was not an absolute necessity, as the old stage-coach could be utilized for such purpose. The doctrine should rest on higher grounds. It is that declared by many courts, in substance, that corporations invested with franchises to subserve the public interests are subject to legislative control. That the visitorial or superintending power of the state over corporations created by the legislature will always be exercised in proper cases, through the medium of the courts, to keep those corporations within the limits of their lawful powers, and to control and punish abuses of their franchises. Notwithstanding the position assumed by Judge Sherwood as to the character of such companies, their relations to the community, and the rights and privileges they must neces-

1 McCum v. Norwich City Gas Co., Commerce, 47 Wis. 670; Attorney-General v. State, 80 Conn. 521. See also New York Central etc. R. Co. v. Metropolitan Gas Light Co., 63 N. Y. 826; State v. Cincinnati Gas Light Co., 84 Ohio St. 572; State v. Milwaukee Chamber of Commerce, 18 Ohio St. 237.

2 State v. Milwaukee Chamber of

<br>

### --- Page 0480 ---

§§ 839, 840.] PRIVATE CORPORATIONS — PUBLIC DUTIES. 1023

sarily exercise, give to them a public character, and to a certain extent a monopoly, which can be controlled only upon the ground of some corresponding duty to meet the public wants. That duty requires the company to supply gas to those who have properly prepared their premises to receive it, upon complying with such reasonable conditions as may be exacted of them by the company.

Sec. 839. They are quasi-public corporations.— It was said by the supreme court of the United States “that the manufacture of gas and its distribution for public and private use, by means of pipes laid under legislative authority in the streets and ways of a city, is not an ordinary business in which everyone may engage, but is a franchise belonging to the government, to be granted for the accomplishment of public objects, to whomever and upon what terms it pleases. It is a business of a public nature, and meets a public necessity for which the state may make provision. It is one which, so far from affecting the public injuriously, has become one of the most important agencies of civilization for the promotion of public convenience and safety.”

Sec. 840. Whether they can be compelled to furnish gas to all applicants.— The question whether such companies are public or private is manifestly important; upon the answer depends the right of those who desire the use of gas to enforce its delivery. In some jurisdictions it is held there is no obligation on the part of such a company to furnish gas generally to all persons who desire its use, while in others it is held that at common law gas companies are bound to furnish gas to all persons who have made the necessary preparations to receive the same, and especially where such companies have been awarded the exclusive right and privilege of vending gas.

1 Williams v. Mutual Gas Co., 52 Mich. 499.

2 New Orleans Gas Co. v. Louisiana Light Co., 115 U. S. 650.

3 McCune v. Norwich City Gas Co., 30 Conn. 521; Com. v. Lowell G. L. Co., 12 Allen, 75; Paterson G. L. Co. v. Brady, 27 N. J. L. 245; Commercial Bank v. London G. Co., 20 U. C. Q. B. 233.

4 Williams v. Mutual Gas Co., 52 Mich. 499; Shepard v. Milwaukee Gas L. Co., 6 Wis. 589; New Orleans G. L. & B. Co. v. Paulding, 12 Rob. (La.) 878; Gas Light Co. of Baltimore v. Colliday, 25 Md. 1.

<br>

### --- Page 0481 ---

1024 SPECIAL REMEDIES. [$$ 841, 842.

is held even in New York that the furnishing of gas is so far a public use that a charter authorizing a company to take private property upon making compensation therefor is constitutional.
